channel.title: eFinancialCareers Editorial Feed
channel.description: Browse news and career advice from the financial services, investment banking, fintech industry. Get expert insight into what it's like to work in finance.

item.title: The Coffee Run: This is who earns the most money in investment banking and what they do
item.description:
We've launched a new weekly video series <https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqJc_c5JT66B3Cp_26xsN8Xsv2EtvtEQQ> to accompany Morning Coffee, in which we look at one story in more depth to give you the full picture. This week, we're doing something a little different.
To celebrate the release of our 2026 salary and bonus report, we're diving deep into the highest paying divisions of banking and trading.
Why were some traders flying high last year while others were stuck? Watch the video below to find out.
Our 2025-2026 Compensation & Lifestyle Report can be downloaded here </banking-compensation-guide>

item.title: HSBC has some big gaps for people enthused about its capital markets business
item.description:
If you work in debt or equity capital markets and you think that HSBC might be the place for you, then we bring good tidings: it probably is. After several exits, HSBC presumably has several openings, many of them senior.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
There have been various departures from HSBC's capital markets business this week. In the latest exit, we understand that Rob Baker, a director in EMEA ECM syndicate has gone to Citi, where he will be reunited with the talented Ed Sankey, </news/hsbc-lost-best-banker-in-world-as-researchers-wait-to-learn-their-fates> his former boss.
In other exits, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-17/hsbc-s-head-of-mideast-banking-said-to-leave-after-year-in-role?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260417&utm_campaign=author_18657817> reports that Karim Tannir, HSBC's head of Middle East banking has left for an undisclosed 'external opportunity.' Earlier this week, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-13/hsbc-said-to-lose-second-dubai-bond-banker-to-wall-street-rival> reported that Khaled Darwish, HSBC's head of DCM for Central and Eastern Europe, the Middle East and Africa is going to Bank of America in Dubai. And Nour Safa, Dawlish's number two and the head of DCM for the Middle East and North Africa is going to Goldman Sachs.
HSBC declined to comment on the departures. The implication is that the bank needs to hire, particularly in the Middle East, where it lost head of ECM Chris Laing to EFG Holdin and Ramez Hazalun to PJT in November last year.
In the past 12 months, HSBC has also lost Sean Henderson (co-head of APAC DCM); Jerome Pellet (head of corporate and public sector DCM origination in France), Mohammed Albensaleh (head of Saudi DCM) and James Sadler (a director in Saudi Arabia capital markets and financing).
The latest departures follow bonus </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k>payments and may be entirely inevitable. However, they also follow HSBC's surprise decision </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts> in January 2025 to focus on debt capital markets and close its ECM and M&A businesses outside the Middle East and Asia, with the result that some insiders say it is less easy to win global deals.
If you feel inclined to work for HSBC's DCM business, the people to know are Adam Bothamley and Asif Sherani, both whom come from DCM syndicate rather than origination </news/hsbc-s-md-promotions-are-back-to-normal-but-never-simple> and who appear to pay their preferred people very well. </news/hey-hsbc-s-us-bonuses-were-really-great-actually>If you want to work in the Middle East, you could try Mohammed Fannouch or Samer Deghaili, who co-head the new CMA (capital markets and advisory layer) and who have advisory and ECM backgrounds respectively.
HSBC's DCM bankers are a big deal in the Middle East. Last year, Dealogic says they ranked second in the region. So far this year, they rank third. There are some fine seats vacant, if you're down with the structure.

item.title: "I am a financial services recruiter. This is why you can't find a job"
item.description:
I am a financial services recruiter. This is a job I have done for many years. In the current market, it is harder than ever. Almost no one is getting recruited.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This is how things work now:
A role is posted and hundreds of people apply. There is a hiring manager who usually has no idea who all these applicants are.
The applicants go through multiple layers of filtering: ATS filters, internal screening criteria, the generic internal acquisition team. At some point in this process they are rejected. Often they will be automatically rejected soon after applying.
I speak all the time to candidates who are applying for jobs they are appropriate for but are hearing nothing back. I also speak to hiring managers who say they can't find the right people.
Something is clearly going wrong.
In my opinion it's both the filtering and the internal acquisition teams. The hiring process has been so heavily automated and commoditised that the expertise for identifying strong candidates has disappeared. Hundreds of candidates are filtered out, but they include the people the business needs.
This isn't a criticism of internal talent acquisition teams. I understand that they are under pressure and are dealing with high volumes and systems they didn't design. But something is wrong. It's systematic. From where I sit, a huge amount of talent is being overlooked.

item.title: Prime broking professionals are all the rage. For now
item.description:
The first quarter of 2026 was a good time to work in the prime broking division of an investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>. Bank of America, </news/bofa-q1-2026>Citi </news/citi-seems-to-be-cutting-more-expensive-people-as-data-transformation-remains-a-sticking-point>, Goldman Sachs </news/goldman-sachs-immense-quarter-was-accompanied-by-400-job-cuts>, JPMorgan </news/jpmorgan-citi-q1-2026> and Morgan Stanley released Q1 results this week. Their soaring revenues in equities sales and trading were widely credited to soaring activity in prime.
Prime broking businesses in banks work with hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> and with other large institutional investors. As Mithra Warrier, a former Citi prime broking MD, explained <https://www.lseg.com/en/solutions/hedge-funds/podcast/hedge-fund-huddle/season-2-episode-1-prime-broker-hedge-fund-connection> two years ago, prime broking teams are "holistically" involved with their hedge fund clients: they lend them money for leveraged bets; they lend them securities for short bets; they help them with clearing trades; they help them with custody; they even help them raise money in the first place.
As hedge funds have grown, so have the bucks to be made from prime broking. The chart below, taken from this week's report <https://www.spglobal.com/ratings/en/regulatory/article/systemic-risk-trading-firms-expansion-affects-selected-ratings-s101674386> from S&P Global, shows revenues made over the past five years from banks' markets financing businesses, which sit within prime. 2025 was exceptional.
Source: Standard & Poor's
2026 may be more exceptional still. Speaking this week, Morgan Stanley CFO Sharon Yeshaya said the bank's growing prime balances supported its 25% year-on-year increase in equities sales and trading revenues, "particularly in Asia." Goldman Sachs CFO Denis Coleman said the firm "significantly expanded" its own equities financing activities in Asia. Citi CEO Jane Fraser said <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/2026psqtr1-transcript.pdf>the bank increased its prime balances by 50%.
Growth in prime reflects investment in the area, particularly in the Asian market. Citi hired Jignesh Patel <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-08-12/citi-hires-goldman-millennium-alum-as-asia-prime-finance-head> from Millennium last August as its APAC prime finance head, for example. It also hired Jillian Snyder from BofA as head of capital introductions in New York in December.
But, prime isn't a one way bet. When it goes wrong, it can go very wrong, as evinced by Credit Suisse's $5.5bn of prime broking losses </news/2021/11/credit-suisse-prime-broking-shutdown> relating to the collapse of hedge fund Archegos Capital Management in 2021. A subsequent report blamed "incompetence" </news/2021/07/credit-suisse-staff-incompetent-archegos> in the Swiss bank's prime broking team, following a weakening of risk management expertise in the area.
As banks double down on prime broking revenues, they need to hope their risk management processes are sound. The charts below, created last year by Matt King, </news/matt-king-citi>Citi's former head of credit strategy at Sartori Insights <https://satoriinsights.com/>, highlight the huge growth in hedge fund leverage since 2020, much of it driven by prime services teams. S&P Global notes that the average hedge fund is now 12X levered, versus 5X for much of the 2010s. This creates "significant aggregate risk across the balance sheet," S&P observes.
All will be well, unless a major fund blows up. At that point, the risk from prime businesses will become amply apparent. For the moment at least, prime services professionals might want to make the most of their moment in the sun.
Source: Sartori Insights

item.title: Nomura made another big hire for its Singapore macro team
item.description:
Nomura's Singapore macro franchise is in a state of flux. Some big names (Anshu Sidher, Herr-Ling Yeo </news/nomura-is-thought-to-be-hiring-anz-s-senior-escapees-in-singapore>) are supposed to be arriving but have not done so, yet. Others are turning up instead.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The imminent arrivals are understood to include Raghav Joshi, a senior G10 FX trader who was last seen at Santander. Joshi didn't respond to a request to comment and Nomura declined to comment but we have it on good authority that he is on his way.
Joshi could find himself in familiar company. Before he joined Santader in August 2024, he was at BNP and before joined BNP he was at ANZ. Sidher and Ling Yeo were at ANZ at the same time.
Nomura has made other major hires this year too. For example, Aleksander Ganchev arrived from Standard Chartered in London in March and is reportedly expanding the bank's bullion trading business, with more hires expected. <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-16/nomura-plans-precious-metals-trading-expansion-under-new-hire>

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs' highest paid traders made a mistake. Hedge funds are back and need top PMs more than ever
item.description:
Our new compensation report </banking-compensation-guide?_gl=1*12qkfyi*_gcl_au*NTcwOTI4NzQ3LjE3NzU1NDU3NjM.*_ga*MjI1NjQ1OTI5LjE3NzU1NDU3NjA.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NzY0MTIwODkkbzU3JGcxJHQxNzc2NDEyMDk4JGo1MSRsMCRoOTMxODk1NDQ.>says macro traders </banking-compensation-guide?_gl=1*12qkfyi*_gcl_au*NTcwOTI4NzQ3LjE3NzU1NDU3NjM.*_ga*MjI1NjQ1OTI5LjE3NzU1NDU3NjA.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NzY0MTIwODkkbzU3JGcxJHQxNzc2NDEyMDk4JGo1MSRsMCRoOTMxODk1NDQ.> are the highest paid. Macro traders trade FX. They also trade rates. Top rates traders at Goldman Sachs are therefore very likely to be among the group that always earns $9.5m+. </news/goldman-sachs-pay-london>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
In the first quarter, though, Goldman Sachs' highly paid rates traders made a mistake. Mike Mayo, the veteran banking analyst prone to frank-speaking, says <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/15/goldman-sachs-bond-traders-wall-street-rivals.html>a fire is probably being lit underneath them as a result.
Their mistake was forgivable, but maybe not if you're being paid for immaculate foresight. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/1f2d514d-4418-4b77-a3f5-2181080b670c?syn-25a6b1a6=1>says Goldman's rates traders, like most macro hedge funds </news/macro-hedge-fund-managers>, were positioned in anticipation that interest rates would fall in 2026, while simultaneously expecting a decline in tech and AI stocks. (This sounds a bit like they too are a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>. The FT says Goldman's rates traders take more risk than its other traders who also take more risk than the rest of the sell-side.)
The year has not worked out as planned. Interest rates may no longer fall as inflation inspired by the Middle East war takes hold. Tech stocks have held up and are rising again. And Goldman's rates desk has floundered.
Problems on the desk are being blamed for the 10% year-on-year decline in Goldman Sachs' fixed income sales and trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> revenues in the first quarter. Comparable revenues were up 29% at Morgan Stanley and 21% at JPMorgan.
The gnarly appendage of blame may point towards one particular man. The FT notes that Anshul Sehgal runs Goldman's rates group. Sehgal joined from Bank of America 15 years ago and sits on the firm's operating committee. He will now feel the heat to improve performance. Fortunately, his boss is likely to be sympathetic: the FT notes that Ashok Varadhan </news/2018/05/ashok-varadhan-goldman-sachs>, head of the markets business at Goldman Sachs, cut his teeth in rates trading too.
Separately, hedge funds had a hard time in March, but April is a different matter. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/hedge-funds-track-best-monthly-returns-over-decade-goldman-says-2026-04-16/> reports that April 2026 is not the cruellest month for hedge funds, but is their best month for over a decade.
This verdict is derived from the Goldman Sachs quarterly hedge fund report, which says that long-short equities funds have done particularly well. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-16/millennium-point72-and-jain-post-gains-after-us-iran-ceasefire?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260416&utm_campaign=author_22884492> reports that Point72, Millennium and Jain Global have a good start to April, with gains of over 2% in the first ten days.
However, Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/hedge-fund-industry-outlook-winners-losers-blackrock-2026-4>reports that BlackRock has simultaneously released its own hedge fund report, which says that investors should be cautious around hedge funds, because their value is "not inherent." In this circumstance, the "premium on manager skill" is greater, says BlackRock, suggesting portfolio manager pay might rise, again.
Meanwhile
The six largest US banks cut 5,000 jobs in the first quarter, even as profitabilty soared. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-15/wall-street-banks-cut-5-000-jobs-even-as-they-notched-record-profits?srnd=homepage-americas&sref=DK3y4h9m>
We are in the era of the "mega layoff." Witness Snao, Oracle, Amazon and Block. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/business/has-the-era-of-the-mega-layoff-arrived-928f061d?mod=e2tw>
It's a bad time for recruiters in Europe and the outlook is uncertain for the rest of the year. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/ec6dba23-79e5-4028-8f0b-3b91e6114350?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Tax-loss harvesting strategies where securities are sold at a loss to offset gains elsewhere in a portfolio are growing in popularity. Quant hedge funds have pioneered new systems that use leverage and algorithmic trading to buy and short securities at scale, and to systematically realise losses on positions. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/fad9d0ca-0b7a-4c22-8cfe-fb26fea9219b?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Cecilia Culver, was hired as an assistant economist by EY, but now she's been dismissed after her speech decrying Israel's treatment of Gaza at her graduation ceremony went viral. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/1ae1f06b-909d-4d01-af72-56504c839379?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Jamie Dimon didn't sell any of his stock in JPMorgan until 2023 when he sold $180m. Then he sold $233m last year, $20m in February and $41m this week. He still has $1.9bn to go. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-04-15-2026/card/jamie-dimon-sells-40-million-of-jpmorgan-stocks-MWyLG50rut4LheBuU4XO>
Singapore's family office want to invest in AI but lack the technical expertise to do so. (StraitsTimes) <https://www.straitstimes.com/business/companies-markets/ai-investment-boom-leaves-some-singapore-family-offices-on-the-sidelines>
The EU is thinking of making it easier for firms to do deals based on “innovation, investment and resilience of the internal market.” (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/75073836-d923-4b3f-a1ca-5ae83dcd705a?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Barclays CEO CS Venkatakrishnan is saying fine things about the Middle East and Saudi Arabia and says Barclays still wants a Saudi license: “The attractiveness of the UAE, the strength of the Saudi economy, will continue to be strong.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-16/barclays-pushing-ahead-with-saudi-bank-license-effort-ceo-says>
CS Venkatakrishnan says he wishes that Barclays had nothing to do with Tricolor and Market Financial Solutions. (Semafor) <https://www.semafor.com/article/04/16/2026/barclays-group-chief-executive-cs-venkatakrishnan-said-at-semafor-world-economy-that-client-selection-has-always-got-to-be-the-most-important-thing-in-banking>
Nearly nine out of 10 bank staff earning at least 1 million euros ($1.2 million) a year in 2024 were men. (Reuters) <https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/boards-policy-regulation/europes-top-banker-pay-rises-men-still-dominate-2026-04-16/>
Cybercriminals are stealing recruiters’ identities to hustle job seekers out of money. People posing as real recruiters send emails approaching candidates for real jobs, then ask for fees to amend CVs. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/scammers-are-stealing-recruiters-identities-to-target-job-seekers-7d5b2c96?mod=e2tw>
Angst of the Goldman Sachs MD. "There was one particular mathematical formula that is essential to understanding equity derivatives (the Black Scholes formula) that I never understood. Regardless of any positive feedback or promotions I received over the years, I felt like an imposter. I felt like I had tricked my bosses and co-workers into thinking that I was a lot more competent than I was." (Upbuild) <https://www.upbuild.com/reflections/trading-derivatives-and-the-shame-based-fear-of-being-ordinary>
The head of France and Benelux for Kepler Chevreux has a wife known for writing dark erotic fiction. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/3860b493-1312-4821-8717-2aa4263e6b53>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Yes, Citi's top quant has joined hedge fund Millennium
item.description:
As we reported last month, Anissa Dhouibi </news/one-of-citi-s-top-equities-quants-resigned-maybe-her-team-will-follow>is no longer at Citi. Having collected her bonus for 2025, Dhouibi resigned at the end of March. She has now revealed her destination: Millennium.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Writing on LinkedIn today, Dhoubi said she had "six wonderful years" at Citi and is "thrilled to join Millennium."
Millennium did not immediately respond to a request to comment. It's not clear whether Dhouibi is setting up a new pod or joining a centralised team of quant analysts. At Citi, she was the global head of equities quantitative analysis.
After Dhoubi resigned, Citi briefly advertised her role </news/citi-s-global-head-of-equities-quants-left-in-ny-and-now-it-would-like-a-new-one-in-london> in London, saying it was looking for a "visionary" managing director with a PhD or Masters and 15 years' experience to lead its team.
The advertisement has since disappeared, suggesting that someone has been hired or moved internally. There are unconfirmed reports that it might be EL Mostafa Ezzine, a London-based managing director who is head of hybrids.

item.title: Goldman Sachs and Citi are hiring AI talent from outside banking
item.description:
Many banks have built out their AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> capabilities in recent years, so the talent pool of AI experts </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking> in banking is ever-growing. Sometimes you need a bit of help from outside, however; Goldman Sachs and Citi are hiring new AI managing directors (MDs) from big tech and consulting.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This week, Goldman hired Washington-based Omer Zaki as an MD for AI and foundational infrastructure. He was most recently a VP of AI infrastructure innovation at Oracle, but he spent the bulk of his career with AWS. Latterly, he was a director of product management working on agent-based automation services. Goldman has a long history of hiring from AWS </news/2020/09/james-saull-barclays-aws>; its chief information officer, Marco Argenti, </news/english-as-a-coding-language-llm>was an AWS vice president.
Zaki isn't the only AWS alum to join this month. Goldman announced last week that Archana Vemulapalli joined as a partner and head of AI product management and strategic relations. She was most recently a corporate vice president for AMD, but she held multiple senior roles at AWS including head of Americas solutions architecture.
Citi, meanwhile, hired Jerry Lavish in New York as an MD and global head of AI risk, approvals and portfolio oversight. He was most recently an MD at tech consultancy Accenture, where he was the responsible AI lead for financial services in the Americas. He previously focused on using agentic AI in the governance process, which might be useful as the bank finishes its costly data transformation project.

item.title: JPMorgan's cluster of London millionaires diminished last year
item.description:
JPMorgan released its Q1 results earlier </news/jpmorgan-citi-q1-2026> this week, and as interesting as they were, something more interesting flew under the radar: 2025 disclosures for its main UK subsidiaries, J.P. Morgan Securities plc and J.P. Morgan Europe Limited.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
The disclosures relate to the compensation and employment of material risk takers (MRTs). MRTs are employees whose “professional activities have a material impact on the risk profile of the firm,” as well as all those who earn over £660k (€756k/$871k).
There were fewer really big earners at JPMorgan in 2025 than in 2024. JPMorgan reported that it had 358 people earning over €1m across its London office in 2025, down from 371 in 2024. It had 23 people earning over €4m in 2025, down on 29 in 2024; a decrease of over 20%.
While the number of highest earners fell, pay for the more average MRT in the investment bank rose. The average pay for investment banking </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> MRTs at JPMorgan went from $1.8m in 2024 to $2m in 2025, an 8.5% increase. This was mostly on account of bonuses - which went from $973k to $1.2m, a 20% increase. Average salaries for MRTs declined by 5%, from $840k on average to $798k, however,
JPMorgan fired exactly ten MRTs last year, They earned total severance of $3m, or $300k each on average. The implication is that none of these people were in the very highest earning pay categories. They might not even have been bankers.

item.title: Humble quant with exquisite tastes seeks $250k researcher for fun times
item.description:
We often write here about Giuseppe (Gappy) Paleologo </news/hedge-fund-quant-job> and would like to think that we were the first to recognise his wry talents </news/america-s-most-amusing-quant-is-showcasing-the-best-use-of-gardening-leave> back in early 2024, before he became famous and appeared on Bloomberg's Odd Lots <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-06-21/giuseppe-paleologo-on-quant-investing-at-multi-strat-hedge-funds> podcast last summer.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Paleologo is now at multistrategy hedge fund Balyasny Asset Management where he is global head of quant research </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund> and the sort of boss who says things like "the kindness of strangers is the best kindness" and "you can work 14hr/day at a firm and, within 6 months from your departure, you'll be vastly forgotten."
Paleologo also reads widely, quotes Borges and watches the the finest films. <https://x.com/__paleologo/status/2040515142853505070>If you want to work with him at Balynasy, he is hiring. If get to work with the most wryly refined of quants, Balyasny will pay you a salary of $225k-$275k (based on New York salaries), plus a bonus. There are openings on his team (for senior quant researchers in "portfolio manager engagement") in Hong Kong and London, too.
Writing on X, Paleologo says his jobs are fun. He says they involve modelling, but also humans. They are about the quantitative replication of the " almost infinite facets of the fundamental investment process," and the implementation of those insights into code. Portfolio managers then use these quantitative insights to become better investors.
It sounds fun. If you want the jobs you'll need seven years' experience. You'll need to be able to talk to portfolio managers about "offensive risk taking." You'll also need to be able to speak to Paleologo, who says he has high standards. If you want a primer, his quant reading list is here <https://www.montefischer.com/paleologo.html> and his own books are available here. <https://www.amazon.co.uk/stores/Giuseppe-A.-Paleologo/author/B0D1DB3MS1?ref=ap_rdr&shoppingPortalEnabled=true>Poetry, philosophy and gnocchi are also favourite topics.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Hedge fund managers have less money than you thought: "I've had to keep living on my savings"
item.description:
Our latest compensation survey </banking-compensation-guide> confirms that you can still earn good money </news/highest-paying-jobs-in-banking> working in financial services. But if you think that a career in as an investment banker is going to bring you £330k ($444k) a year to live on, and a career in hedge funds twice that, think again. There are income taxes. There are also fallow periods.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Financial services jobs can be unstable. Job security is eroded by the triad of market cycles, internal politics and automation. However good you are at your job, you are in danger of being hit by one or all of these things. The combination can be financially corrosive.
"I don't really have any savings," one senior hedge fund analyst tells us. "In the last decade I've had four years out of the market. Some years I was working I did really well but in other years things happened like my boss blew up. I've had to support my family on what I'd saved. Now I don't have much left."
Others in the market agree. "It's not like a public sector job, where you do 35 years and then retire," says one senior trader who's bounced between banks and hedge funds. "These are high risk and volatile jobs and you can never get too comfortable and presume that you will be somewhere for long."
With most banks signalling an aversion </news/banking-jobs-2026> to adding unnecessary staff and hedge funds licking their March wounds </news/macro-hedge-fund-managers>, the danger is that anyone ejected from the market now will be out for longer still. In the UK, this comes as London house prices are falling <https://www.ft.com/content/0ae454cb-285f-47a2-8f42-7e589ab8e307?syn-25a6b1a6=1>and as the City's position as a financial centre remains dented by Brexit. Meanwhile, the cost of living is heading in the opposite direction.
It's not desperate. People with histories of earning over $400k are not on the breadline. But nor are they as wealthy as might be assumed. The older you get, the greater risk of being locked out of the market permanently. "You can earn more in financial services than a doctor of lawyer, but it's compressed into fewer working years," says the senior trader. "When you're in your 30s, you're earning $1m and your lawyer mates are on $200k. But when you're in your 50s you're probably out of a job and your lawyer friends are still working."
While you're earning good money in finance, it's therefore advisable to save as much as possible. "There are a lot of victims who don't have a sensible lifestyle," says the trader. "They earn $1m, they spend the $550k they have left after high taxes and then they don't have anything and realise there are fewer and fewer jobs and the competition for them is more intense than ever."
Matters are made worse by notoriously aggressive hedge fund non-competes </news/killing-non-competes-will-make-hedge-fund-managers-more-boring-people>. It's not unusual for people to be asked to sit out of the market for two years when they change jobs. They are paid during this period, but only salary and not bonus. Hedge fund professionals with school fees and high mortgages say this depletes their savings further.
The hedge fund analyst says his experience has taught him the value of money. "It takes a long time to understand the meaning of the word 'need,'" he reflects. He says that it helps that his wife is frugal and that he didn't grow up financial privileged. "Honestly? I just want enough money to be free."

item.title: The spending diary of a 30-year-old investment banker: £1,600 a month on dates
item.description:
According to our salary and bonus report </banking-compensation-guide>, the average investment banking analyst earns £84k ($113.5k). In top global cities like London </news/the-monthly-spending-diary-of-a-london-trader-with-two-teslas> you can earn often earn upwards of £100k a year, but how far does that really go?
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Harry Billen, formerly of Stifel </news/stifel-job-cuts-2025>'s investment banking division, moved into banking in 2021 from Deloitte, seemingly at around the age of 30. In a recent YouTube <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VNXl_3dprIU> video, he revealed that he was on over £100k while working in IBD; he also broke down what he spent all that money on:
Gross Income: ~£8.3k per month
Net income (post-tax, pension and student loans): £4.5k per month
Rent and bills: £1.2k per month
Direct Debits (gym, phone contract, etc.): £300 per month
Food: £500 per month
Ad hoc toiletries and personal care: £200 per month
Miscellaneous spending: £1k per month
The remaining cash would either go towards savings (through a stocks and shares ISA) or more miscellaneous spending, of which Billen prefers the latter; "I'm a big believer that experiences make life," he said. While this would often involve going out with friends, he also said "there was a point in time I used to go on three or four dates a week </news/2023/02/dating-a-banker>... I'm paying for all of these and you end up paying between 50 to 100 quid on just a drinks date." This would mean he was paying £600 to £1,600 per month purely on dates. While that wasn't a frequent occurrence, he also had other big miscellaneous expenses, including studio time to launch his music career. <https://sonextyear.co.uk/#music>
Billen, who joined Stifel in 2021, has been running a startup focused on surprise events planning since 2022. One of his bigger regrets when earning £100k was contributing to his pension. </news/i-m-earning-500k-at-a-london-hedge-fund-and-am-concerned-about-my-future> "I think about the present value [of money] and because I have a business... I need that money now."
Pay for junior bankers can be volatile due to how much of their compensation is reliant on bonuses. Billen said his bonuses ranged from double his salary to nothing at all. On larger bonuses, </news/financial-services-bonuses-2025>he said that 30-40% of it was immediately lost to tax; "genuinely, I thought I was getting paid in two [installments]," he said.
Billen said that "£100k does not go a long way in this economy" unless your only goal is to save money. "If I didn't have the business, if I didn't do my music, I could probably save a grand a month quite easy," he said. "I just don't do that."

item.title: The healthiest bankers in the world aren't where you expect them to be
item.description:
People who work in financial services aren’t famous for their excellent health, Patrick Bateman aside <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PaxYErt9vO4>. Between the long work hours </news/finance-best-lifestyle> and high stress, it can be hard to keep up with a workout routine or even the occasional light meditation </news/investment-banking-yoga>. But there are bankers who manage. Where are they? And who do they work for?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Our Compensation & Lifestyle Report 2026 </banking-compensation-guide> is based on a survey that we ran across Q1 that received over 2,700 responses from financial services professionals. Aside from questions about their compensation, we also asked them how they felt about their health.
On average, financial services professionals rated their health a 7.2 out of ten, with physical health being rated higher than mental health, generally – 7.4 vs 7.1, respectively.
The healthiest professionals are to be found at either investment bank </banking-compensation-guide> Goldman Sachs or at asset management </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-asset-management> firm BlackRock. Both reported average health scores of 7.6, but while BlackRock reported equal mental and physical health scores, Goldman Sachs reported much higher physical health scores than mental health scores.
One respondent to our survey working for BlackRock said that there is “more time to exercise on the buy-side”, rating his physical health an 8 out of ten. He proceeded to rate his mental health a 5 out of ten, however, noting “existential dread of future of country.” Unfortunate.
The least healthy professionals, surprisingly, were both at European banks: Standard Chartered and Santander. Respondents from both reported average health scores of 6.1, with Santander respondents reporting equal self-ratings of 6.1 in both mental and physical health. Respondents working for Standard Chartered reported more average physical health scores of 6.8 and extremely poor mental health scores of 5.5.
One Standard Chartered director based in London responding to our survey gave his physical health a score of 5 out of 10. “Too much work and travel to exercise fully,” he said. A Santander analyst, meanwhile, rated their mental health a 3 out of ten, noting that they “developed thyroid cancer due to the pressures exerted on me by my manager.” They also rated their physical health as a 6/10, for what it’s worth.
Standard Chartered declined to comment, but it does have a number of initiatives to improve employee's health. For example, it provides access via Unmind to a 24/7 helpline offering financial and legal advice, as well as access to monthly appointments for coaching, counselling, and clinical psychotherapy.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Brave and humble quant buys a $350m home. Bank of America traders and their “extraordinary” goings on
item.description:
Quadrature Capital, </news/quadrature-capital> the quant trading firm, doesn’t seek publicity – it has an extremely generic website <https://www.quadrature.ai/> explaining that it “combines cutting edge technology with deep quantitative research” and that its purpose is to “Put People First and Innovate Together”. There’s a bit more colour on the website of its charitable twin, the Quadrature Climate Foundation <https://www.qc.foundation/about-us/>, which tells us that when they’re attacking the problem of climate change, the Quadrature values are to be “Collaborative, Humble, Objective, Brave and Driven”.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
But whatever they do, they seem to do it very well. The climate foundation, which was only set up in 2019, has already committed grants totalling $1.5bn to various environmental projects. Which would suggest that it’s made significantly more than that for its owners, as well as paying its employees an average of more than $3.5m each </news/quadrature-pay-london>. One of the founders, Suneil Setiya </news/quadrature-pay-london> has just bought Providence House, a London mansion with lake and pool, for £275m (about $350m). In case you have lost track of exactly how much is a lot in this world of incredibly rich-people numbers, when Ken Griffin of Citadel paid $238m for a penthouse <https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-us-canada-46986362> in New York in 2019, it set a record for the most expensive US house purchase <https://www.realtor.com/news/unique-homes/most-expensive-homes-sold-us-record-history/> ever. In a small touch of irony, the vendor of Setiya's new house was property developer Nick Candy, who is the treasurer of the UK’s Reform Party, while Quadrature made the largest ever donation <https://www.opendemocracy.net/en/dark-money-investigations/keir-starmer-labour-party-quadrature-donation-arms-companies-israel-war-gaza/> to the Labour Party.
This might tell us a bit about the potential future of the very top end of finance. Once upon a time, the biggest yachts and houses were owned by bankers, like J Pierpont Morgan. After a while, even the richest sellsiders started to be eclipsed by the hedge fund billionaires, who ended up themselves being left behind (apart from a small number of multi-strategy colossi) by the private equity industry.
But Quadrature Capital isn’t a fund – it’s a proprietary trading firm which invests its own capital. One of the reasons the website is so basic is that if you call them up and ask to make an investment, they’ll say no. And it doesn’t have anything like the headcount of a hedge fund either – there were only 173 employees in London at the end of last year, and while there will be some more in Singapore and New York, the entire staff wouldn’t fill a quarter of a floor in a bulge bracket firm.
Some people predict that this is how the industry is going to develop. More computer power, smaller organisations, more concentration of wealth and less need for ordinary employees. They might be right. Which would mean that it will become even more necessary for bankers to take a leaf out of Quadrature’s values and stay brave and humble.
Elsewhere, commodities traders are something of a breed apart; they trade some of the most volatile markets there are, and if you’ve got a few years of experience in that job, you’ve probably seen a lot of crazy things. So it takes a lot to make them break out the superlatives. But according to Denis Manelski and Soofian Zuberi <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-15/bofa-sees-60-jump-in-commodities-trading-fueled-by-oil-and-gold?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260415&utm_campaign=author_19908303>, the co-heads of markets at Bank of America, even by those standards, the oil and gold markets during the first quarter of 2026 were something else.
As Manelski put it, “the activity levels in those two commodities were extraordinary”. It’s hard not to detect a certain wistfulness to his added comment, that “it’s been so headline-driven”. Clients trading in a panic based on news stories that everyone can see are the very best kind of clients, particularly in a market like oil where the norm is big players trading strategically on the basis of private information. Zuberi added, also possibly with a touch of understatement, that “we were in a good position to help clients execute”. Overall commodities trading revenue was up 60% for BoA, and Morgan Stanley <https://www.ft.com/content/99af0006-d810-4616-8624-912dc830e5ac?syn-25a6b1a6=1> also seems to have had a very strong quarter driven by the same headlines. It seems that across the Street, this might have been a quarter where the sell side also did abnormally well.
Meanwhile …
Zohran Mamdani wants to tax Ken Griffin's New York penthouse. (X) <https://x.com/Acyn/status/2044509636817260567?s=20>
When he was at Merrill Lynch, Harley Bassman invented the MOVE index, a kind of equivalent of VIX for Treasury bonds. Inventing a good index is potentially incredibly lucrative, but it seems that Bassman has had trouble getting paid. He is currently suing Simplify Asset Management, an ETF firm that he invented funds for, in a dispute over exactly what royalties he’s owed. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-14/bond-market-veteran-bassman-sues-etf-manager-over-compensation>)
Some people hoped that AI would help to streamline the hiring process, but the most effective applications seem to be ways for candidates to cheat. From having offscreen advice on technical problems to straight up deepfakes in video interviews, some companies are concluding that they are going to have to go back to in-person chats. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/ai-job-interview-virtual-in-person-305f9fd0>)
Other financial firms are talking the talk, but Bain Capital has now walked the walk when it comes to expression of confidence in the Gulf region. It’s going ahead with the opening of its new office in Abu Dhabi. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/bain-capital-opens-abu-dhabi-office-ccfa727c?mod=topic_news>)
Mark Mobius, the legendary investor who was the face of emerging markets for years, has died at the age of 89. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/590c65f4-6261-4dd7-b8ea-73b78fa23479>)
The implicit deal between smart graduates and the big consulting firms was that you would join out of university, work like a dog for a few years, and then even if you didn’t make partner, you’d have the skills, experience and resume to have your choice of top jobs in industry. Now that AI is changing the role of junior consultants, that deal seems to be coming in to question – summer interns just don’t feel as “integral” as they used to. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-15/ai-influences-how-mckinsey-bcg-bain-hire-for-entry-level-consulting-jobs>)
It turns out that the “con” in the seasonal New York bar crawl “SantaCon” might not have been short for “convention”. The organiser is being prosecuted for wire fraud, after prosecutors allege that a lot of the money collected for charity was diverted to home renovations, luxury holidays and a resort in Costa Rica. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/736515e5-9195-496b-9d71-3fa61cdcc512?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)

item.title: BNP Paribas hired a veteran trading MD of mysterious provenance after some exits
item.description:
Last year, various people left BNP Paribas' equities trading team </news/bnp-paribas-hired-a-citi-equities-trader-after-various-departures>. This year, it has already recruited someone new.
The new person is Nigel Fenn, a veteran trader who's been in the market since at least 2001 (when the FCA Register has him at HSBC). Possibly longer. Nigel has also worked for Merrill Lynch, UBS and BTIG. But since 2023 he's been AWOL for regulatory purposes.
BNP Paribas declined to comment on Nigel's most recent occupation or his appointment. It's understood that he is joining the French bank as a managing director with a broad base.
BNP's equities departures in 2025 included the likes of Katrina Pattemore, a senior equities trader with a level of experience equivalent to Fenn. Last September, BNP hired Alex Ford, Citi's head of block trading.
Equities traders have had a good quarter. BNP Paribas has yet to report its first quarter results, but with the exception of JPMorgan US banks achieved </news/bofa-q1-2026> 20%+ year-on-year revenue growth.

item.title: 2026 is brilliant for some in banking. But if you're looking for a job, it's brutal
item.description:
It is the best of years. It is the worst of years. US banks from Goldman Sachs to JPMorgan, Bank of America and Citi have reported first quarter results this week and they are fine indeed.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Markets businesses in particular had a blowout quarter. In the words of JPMorgan CFO Jeremy Barnum, recent volatility has not been of the bad variety featuring "extremely gappy discontinuous markets with low liquidity that keeps clients on the sidelines." In the words of his boss, it's been of the good variety that encourages "much more volume and the volatility." Spreads are wider, volumes are up. Equities trading revenues benefitted; prime balances soared as hedge funds engaged.
Even deal-doing bankers basked in a balmy breeze of execution. First quarter M&A revenues rose nearly 90% year-on-year at Goldman Sachs and over 80% at JPMorgan. Barnum said M&A deals benefitted from "accelerated timing" thanks to "faster-than-expected regulatory approval." Morgan Stanley said today <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-15/morgan-stanley-says-capital-rule-rollback-boosted-trading-haul?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260415&utm_campaign=author_20461763>that lighter capital requirements boosted its trading revenues. The US government has been good for something.
Higher revenues mean higher pay. JPMorgan, Citi and Goldman all increased compensation spending in the first quarter. That's auspicious for bonuses.
However, three months are not a year. Even the most ebullient banking executives acknowledged that war in the Middle East might take its toll. For the moment, Barnum said clients still expect the situation to be quickly resolved. "If things start getting derailed, I would be surprised if you don't see some impact on sentiment and on deal decision-making," he observed.
With uncertainty comes caution, and with caution comes care over costs. As we noted </news/goldman-sachs-immense-quarter-was-accompanied-by-400-job-cuts>earlier this week, Goldman's blowout quarter was achieved with 400 fewer people than it had at the end of 2025. Citi cut 2,000 people </news/citi-seems-to-be-cutting-more-expensive-people-as-data-transformation-remains-a-sticking-point> in Q1 and they appeared to have been high earners. More cuts are coming. Goldman reportedly <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-layoffs-cuts-underperformers-sra-spring-summer-2026-3> intends to spend 2026 conducting waves of incremental cuts as it pursues a 60% efficiency ratio (it hit 60.5% in the first quarter). Citi CFO Gonzalo Luchetti said yesterday that the bank expects to end 2026 with lower headcount once again. AI will "turbocharge" efficiencies, said Luchetti. The mantra is the same everywhere.
All of which means that while some front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking>bankers and traders have had a brilliant quarter, new jobs remain hard to come by. It's brutal if you're out of the market now, says one London headhunter.
"Banks are making hundreds of millions in profits but they're also constantly making hundreds of people redundant," the headhunter tells us. "These aren’t marginal roles being cut; it’s often experienced men and women, capable people, senior individuals who’ve delivered for years, mid-level operators who keep things running. Entire teams are being reduced or reshaped. It's not about performance."
Once you've been cut, he says the hiring process has become so convoluted that getting back in is almost impossible. "Everything is going through layers and a process that involves robots, ATS filters, screening criteria and internal talent teams.
"Most people are automatically rejected, even when they are very strong."

item.title: How important are spring weeks and insight events when applying for electronic trading internships?
item.description:
Your best shot at a graduate </news/graduates-are-earning-250k-salaries-in-algorithmic-trading> job in an electronic trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> is to get an internship </news/high-frequency-trading-internship> and convert it. Those internships are themselves incredibly difficult to obtain, with acceptance rates as low as 0.1% </news/hudson-river-trading-intern-pay>for top firms. There are plenty of other events run by these firms, including spring-weeks and insight events, but do they really improve your chances of getting an internship? Students we've spoken to believe so.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Most pre-internship programs seem to accept ~1% of applicants, </news/internship-acceptance-rates-banking-finance> including Citadel/Citadel Securities' discover event in London, which was expanded from one day to four in this year's iteration. IMC Trading's launchpad internship in Amsterdam had a similar acceptance rate. Some events have acceptance rates slightly below that figure, including Optiver's career kickstarter in Amsterdam; one r/quantfinance <https://www.reddit.com/r/quantfinance/comments/1qf132c/optiver_career_kickstarter_2026cognitive/> user said that this is a 1-week training program in which top performers can receive full-time return offers. Jane Street's academy of math and programming (AMP) in New York, targeted at incoming first year students, has an acceptance rate of ~8%. These are all a lot easier to obtain than a summer internship, but by no means easy to get, which immediately helps you stand out.
The content of these events is also useful. One attendee of multiple events said that they were "clueless for the most part" about quant finance at first, but the events helped him "learn more about the industry and what specifically I wanted to do." Some programs are more advanced and give students a chance to participate in trading simulations, but even these events are "given a more mathematical twist instead of being too finance focused" to make it accessible to new entrants. Multiple students said that attendance of spring weeks directly came up in some (not all) internship applications. One student told us that, while you need to be "academically good" to get into these events, "they care a lot more about your story than your ability." He said that firms are "really pushing towards giving more access and opportunity" for less privileged candidates.
The application process for these events varies from firm-to-firm. Citadel's London insight event only assessed students on their CVs, but other events like Optiver's kickstarter, are thought to include interviews and online assessments in their application process.
Some events run by trading firms are free to enter and have a lot of upside. IMC runs Prosperity, a yearly themed trading competition; this year, students were tasked with building algorithms to trade resources on an alien planet and build out their own colony. We've noted that top performers in these events are often brought in </news/imc-trading-competition-internships> by IMC or other trading firms for internships, but one participant said that it helped them get a full time job at a trading firm despite having no summer internships. Various firms run datathons </news/citadel-s-datathon-winners-don-t-all-end-up-at-citadel> which you can also use to demonstrate your technical prowess.
There's also financial incentives for multiple programs. Prosperity's winners share a $50k prize pool while Jane Street's AMP attendees are given a $5k scholarship for "future educational opportunities." Hudson River Trading's WITTI (women in trading technology internship) has been known to pay up to $19k </news/2023/10/hudson-river-trading-internship-women> over four weeks.
The trading firms above each either declined to comment or did not respond to a request for comment regarding the acceptance rates for their programmes.

item.title: Bank of America's fixed income traders can look with schadenfreude at Goldman Sachs
item.description:
Q1 of 2026 reporting season for the world’s investment banks is well underway. Goldman Sachs reported </news/goldman-sachs-immense-quarter-was-accompanied-by-400-job-cuts> on Monday; JPMorgan and Citi reported </news/jpmorgan-citi-q1-2026> on Tuesday; and Bank of America <https://investor.bankofamerica.com/quarterly-earnings> and Morgan Stanley <https://www.morganstanley.com/about-us-ir/earnings-releases> reported today.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Bank of America didn’t do great. For the first quarter of 2026, it had the second lowest increase in M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> (+47%) and Equity Capital Markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> (+31%) of the big five American banks compared to the first quarter of 2025, ahead of only Citi and Morgan Stanley, respectively.
BofA's Debt Capital Markets business didn’t do much better, just 2.7% up, while Citi and JPMorgan's DCM revenues fell a few percent and Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs' nudged up.
BofA's equities team was the standout. The team posted results 30% in Q1 of 2026 on the same period in 2025, ahead of all other banks but Citi, who was up 39% on last year.
But BofA's fixed income, commodities, and currencies (FICC) traders were a disappointment, up just 2% while rivals such as Morgan Stanley and JPMorgan were up by over 20%. Only Goldman Sachs, which was 10% down, did worse.
It’s disappointing for Bank of America. It’s a team that has had great success in previous quarters </news/bofa-q2-2025>, and a team that BofA has invested heavily in, especially in Europe <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/bank-of-america-revamps-trading-tech-as-it-eyes-top-three-spot-in-europe-12384dda>. It’s hard to blame any specific team – the macro/credit split did not change substantially – and BofA did not point any fingers in its results disclosures. The implication is that its commodities team was rather flat, too.
What went wrong at Goldman? The firm's Q1 results pointed the finger at its rates and mortgage teams specifically. "That was basically just a function of the overall environment making markets," said the firm's CFO, Denis Coleman, on its Q1 call with investors. He also pointed out that commodities and currencies performed better. "We have big activities across all of those activities."
CEO David Solomon jumped in at that point in the call and pointed out that it was still the firm’s 10th best quarter historically. “FICC performed well in the quarter,” he asserted. Okay then.

item.title: Crypto trading firm GSR made its first hire in the UAE at an interesting time
item.description:
Conflicts in the Middle East </news/dubai-hedge-fund-jobs> seem to (hopefully) be subsiding, but a return to normal is a long way away. A sign that confidence in the region is resurging is that multiple firms are building out in the UAE; the latest to do so is crypto electronic trading firm GSR. </news/another-goldman-sachs-trader-has-left-crypto-market-maker-gsr>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
GSR, based primarily in London, hired Khushbir Dhariwal as a quant researcher based in Abu Dhabi. He spent the last two years at Laser Digital, the crypto arm of investment bank Nomura, latterly serving as its head of over-the-counter (OTC) trading. He's already based in the UAE but is relocating from Dubai </news/bankers-in-dubai> for the role. Dhariwal's background is in London, working in electronic FX trading across RBC, Credit Agricole and Commerzbank.
GSR, which did not respond to a request for comment, does not seem to have any employees in the UAE that predate Dhariwal. It's not clear whether the firm is building out an office there. If it is, the firm isn't the only one making inroads on Abu Dhabi. Hedge fund Balyasny, which already operates in Dubai </news/where-do-portfolio-managers-work-balyasny>, has been expanding its Abu Dhabi presence with ExodusPoint </news/hedge-fund-balyasny-s-abu-dhabi-office-has-its-inaugural-member> and Eisler Capital </news/balyasny-abu-dhabi> alums.
GSR, meanwhile has aspirations of being more than just a trading firm. Reuters reported last month that it wants to become a crypto-native investment bank a-la Galaxy Digital, and has been acquiring multiple firms to facilitate this.

item.title: Sovereign wealth fund with $1m+ salaries is leaking people to hedge funds
item.description:
If you work for a hedge fund, your salary will typically be low and your bonus will typically be high. In the good times, it therefore makes sense to work for a hedge fund. In the bad times, it's better to max your salary elsewhere.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Given that these have been bad times for some hedge funds, it's interesting to note - then - that people have been leaving the most salary-maxxing employer in the market: the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority </news/adia-pay> (ADIA).
As we reported last year, ADIA pays salaries of $3m+ </news/adia-quant-salaries> to its favourite people. Given current market conditions, it might seem a fine place to sit things out. Or not.
ADIA isn't commenting, but insiders at the fund tell us there have been exits. Many are from the systematic equities business, which focuses on data arbitration strategies.
The exits include Sébastien Andler, a senior quant who joined ADIA from BNP in London in 2022. Andler left for aeonea, a new Hong Kong-based multistrategy hedge fund in December. Patrick Yam, another senior quant, left for Jane Street in March. We understand that at least another two departures are in process, with people reportedly leaving for Millennium and Schonfeld.
Insiders said ADIA has become a good training ground for stat arb talent.
In light of the war, it's thought that people at ADIA are working from home. Last month, Marcos Lopez de Prado, one of ADIA's top quants declared </news/adia-quant-war> that he felt fortunate to be located in the emirate. It's thought that many of ADIA's senior people have asked their families to relocate elsewhere.

item.title: Citi's ex-Goldman Sachs global co-head of infrastructure is going
item.description:
Luisa Leyenaar Huntingford is leaving Citi. She was the bank's London-based global co-head of infrastructure and was hired from Goldman Sachs in 2022 to help the bank build out a franchise which works with investment firms on infrastructure deals.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citi isn't commenting on her departure and Leyenaar Huntingford didn't respond to a request to comment. Her co-head, Todd Guenther in New York, is presumably staying.
Citi insiders say Leyenaar Huntingford decided to leave. She is going on a high after building the bank's infrastructure franchise into one of of the leaders in both EMEA and globally and was one of the only women on the leadership team for the investment bank.
We don't know what Leyenaar Huntingford is doing next, but she is likely to be in demand. Before Citi, she spent nearly four years at Goldman and before Goldman she was nearly 13 years at RBC. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/moves-citi-hires-goldman-banker-share-leadership-global-infrastructure-memo-2021-10-05/> reportedly previously that she helped found Goldman's infrastructure franchise.
Citi's infrastructure banking business has been through various changes of people in recent years. Before Leyenaar Huntingford arrived, it was led by Guenther and Francisco Abularach, but Abularach escaped to Antin Infrastructure Partners in 2021 before going to JPMorgan last July and is now head of infrastructure for EMEA there. Jorge Ramos, another large cheese in EMEA infrastructure, is still at Citi but is now head of investment banking for Spain, while also working on infrastructure coverage.
Leyenaar Huntingford's arrival in 2021 was announced by Anthony Diamandakis </news/anthony-diamandakis>, the star Citi sponsors banker who moved to JPMorgan last year. Diamandakis left after Ashu Khullar was unexpectedly moved </news/anthony-diamandakis-citi>from Citi's corporate bank to become his co-head.
Khullar and Sidharth Punshi </news/leave-citi-for-jpmorgan> are now running Citi's European sponsors business and seemingly need to make new hires. Leyenaar Huntingford is not the only exit. Davide Russo </news/davide-russo> left in March. Theo Giatrakos </news/leave-citi-for-jpmorgan> went to JPMorgan last June after leaving when Punshi arrived. Kaan Kaesedar went to Evercore last October.

item.title: Morning Coffee: The 31-year-old who’s made $250m and is leaving the casino. The richest central banker there’s ever been
item.description:
There is no shortage of folk wisdom to tell you how to make a huge amount of money. “Fortune favours the brave”, for example, or “it’s better to be lucky than smart”. “The early bird catches the worm” and “where there’s muck, there’s money”. More actionably, it’s usually a good idea to see what the really rich are doing and try to copy them. So when someone like Christopher Eppinger <https://www.ft.com/content/c58370ba-0d31-4320-ad33-7c2236bd23c8> heads for a previously obscure Latin American market, it’s worth paying attention.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Eppinger has followed all the advice – in his relatively young career he’s been smart, lucky and brave, and has made profits of $250m </news/is-it-worth-working-for-goldman-sachs> in the oil trading market over the last three years. He’s achieved it by not being afraid of getting his hands dirty, both literally and metaphorically. When nearly everyone else in the oil market regarded Russia as a compliance risk not worth taking, Eppinger was one of the few traders to realise that the sanctions were not absolute and that it was possible to deal in Russian oil legally, at capped prices which provided for extraordinary margins.
Before he was able to get into that sort of position, he served a hair-raising apprenticeship in the commodities trading business. Having been introduced to Marc Rich by the father of a schoolfriend, Eppinger ended up in Kazakhstan, buying crude oil for cash and transporting it to a refinery in “weird, old, small cars”. There was apparently so much sulphur in the air that your teeth started to hurt if you stayed too long.
From there, he went through a few more rounds of adventures before setting up his own trading house with a specialism in getting crude oil to a UAE refinery from places that other traders don’t like to deal with. Eppinger apparently ran his company CE Energy from his iPhone and claimed not to touch a computer himself, but he seems to have not only managed the Russian trade skilfully, but got out at the right time. On the basis that “it’s important to leave the casino while you’re still winning”, he shut the operation down earlier in the year, and so has never had to worry unduly about the Strait of Hormuz.
Now he’s in Guyana, claiming that it’s a country with larger hydrocarbon reserves than Norway which are practically undeveloped. He’s set up a new company called Petrichor Energy, bought a quarry and is bidding on shipping contracts.
When you look at a story like this, it’s difficult to extract the career lessons. People like Eppinger (and Marc Rich, and many other of the legends of the physical commodities market) can build huge fortunes and accumulate fantastic anecdotes, but for every such story there might be a couple of dozen equally aggressive types who took similar risks with worse consequences. He might just be a perfect illustration of the best success strategy of them all; right place, right time.
Elsewhere, the financial disclosures <https://www.ft.com/content/2df21771-8684-4814-8816-ab84773f94ee> coming out of Kevin Warsh’s appointment to the Federal Reserve are pretty eye-popping. He has two investments in a hedge fund vehicle run by Stanley Druckenmiller, which are at least $50m each and might be quite a bit more. He also has stakes of undisclosed amounts in Polymarkets, SpaceX and several crypto firms, among his 300 investments in private companies, many of which couldn’t be fully disclosed because of confidentiality agreements. And just over $750k in checking and savings accounts, so he doesn’t need to worry about picking up any restaurant bills.
Where does it all come from? Back in the 2000s, Warsh worked for Morgan Stanley as an Executive Director in M&A, but no matter how frugal you are, that sort of bonus doesn’t compound to a nine-figure sum. He is also married to the heiress to the Estee Lauder cosmetics fortune, but it looks like his money has mainly been generated since his first appointment to the Fed by George W Bush. In the last year, he received more than $13m in consulting fees. It just goes to show that public service doesn’t necessarily have to mean taking a vow of poverty.
Meanwhile …
Despite complications in getting the stuff in and out of the Gulf, the volatility in oil markets has delivered very strong conditions for physical traders. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/936125bc-4b95-4daf-9c1d-a1f690984e9a?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
After only a year at Barclays, Joseph Kerkor has left to become head of emerging markets credit trading at ING. He was a former Goldman and BNP Paribas employee who was an Executive Director at Barclays and will run a team of five at the Dutch bank. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/ing-taps-barclays-for-emea-emerging-markets-credit-trading-head-60cce998?mod=topic_news>)
Alua Capital, which launched strongly in 2020, has departed the field. It didn’t lose money, but annualised returns of 4% since inception weren’t really worth the risks of long-short equity, so founders Marco Tablada and Tom Purcell decided to do the decent thing and wind up. The fund was launched with their family offices as the main investors after they left Lone Pine and Viking respectively, so they’re giving quite a lot of the money back to themselves. (Hedgeweek <https://www.hedgeweek.com/alua-capital-to-wind-down-2bn-hedge-fund-after-underwhelming-returns/>)
AI agents used to be like junior graduate employees; you needed to check up on them because they regularly made silly mistakes or made things up. Now they’re like slightly more experienced graduates, in that they are better at concealing their mistakes and it’s harder to check up on them. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/ai-is-getting-smarter-catching-its-mistakes-is-getting-harder-85612936?mod=hp_listb_pos2>)
The market for senior London lawyers remains extremely hot, and Tom Thesing, head of the fast growing local office of Sidley Austin, is getting a bit salty at the fact that the firms he’s poaching from seem to be extending their gardening leave and notice periods. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/sidley-austin-london-boss-law-firms-should-release-partners-quicker-8ce6123e?mod=topic_news>)
Continuing our occasional series on potential alternative careers in which junior bankers could be making the same money with less stress, a London teenager managed to shoplift roughly a second year Associate salary’s worth of cosmetics. He did end up getting prosecuted, but will only be sentenced in a lower court. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/fdab0a47-d7a2-4be5-b62a-06db708912f2?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)

item.title: Citi seems to be cutting more expensive people as data transformation remains a sticking point
item.description:
Like Goldman Sachs </news/yaoyao-liu-vitol>, Citi </news/jpmorgan-citi-q1-2026>ended the first quarter with fewer people than it began. The bank said today that 2,000 people disappeared during the quarter. They seem to have been paid relatively handsomely to go on their way.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The chart below, taken from Citi's investor presentation, shows the bank's headcount changes each quarter, and its quarterly severance payments. Headcount changes are net and may not exactly map the number of people cut, but using them as a proxy for layoffs it appears that Citi might be removing people higher up the pay scale.
In the first quarter of 2026, for example, Citi's headcount fell </news/citi-cutting-jobs-bonuses>by 2,000 people. This is double the 1,000 people the bank was said <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-12/citi-to-cut-about-1-000-jobs-this-week-as-fraser-trims-costs> to be cutting back in January. Citi spent $500m on severance in the quarter, implying $250k each for the 2,000 people who went on.
Using this methodology, Citi's first quarter severance payments look historically generous. In the third quarter of 2025, the bank only seems to have paid $67k each to its departing staff; in the fourth it seems to have paid them $100k.
Presuming net headcount figures are indeed an accurate proxy for job cuts, Citi therefore seems to have decided to be kind to its inadvertent leavers. Howeverm severance payments are not a reflection of benevolence but of salaries. And so, Citi seems to have been cutting more expensive people in Q1.
The bank declined to comment on this hypothesis.
Citi is likely cutting a particular type of person. The bank added tens of thousands of staff in recent years as it worked on a consent order issued by regulators in 2020. This consent order stated <https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/831001/000110465920113933/tm2032793d1_ex99-3.htm> that Citi had "failed to implement and maintain an enterprise- wide risk management and compliance risk management program, internal controls, or a data governance program commensurate with the bank’s size, complexity, and risk profile."
Following the consent order, Citi's headcount went from 200,000 people in 2019 to nearly 240,000 people in 2022 as it added data, compliance and regulatory staff. Recently departing Citi CFO Mark Mason </news/mark-mason-citi> said in 2024 </news/citi-lifted-the-veil-on-its-complex-data-issues-and-the-staff-needed-to-fix-them>that the bank was spending $250m on remediation, including new data professionals. In July 2024, Citi was issued a further </news/citi-insiders-complain-of-death-by-powerpoint> $136m regulatory fine for data issues in its commercial loan files.
Citi's headcount has since shrunk back again. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/citigroup-aims-complete-work-consent-orders-this-year-sources-say-2026-02-06/> reported in February that Citi is aiming to complete work on its consent orders this year. At the end of March, the bank employed 224,000 people and as the consent order comes to an end, more people are likely to leave.
Citi said today that it's 90% of the way through the transformation program born of its consent order. However, CEO Jane Fraser also said that the remaining 10% of its issues are related to data and specifically related to "data use in regulatory reporting" where Citi's "target state" has not been reached. Even in 2024, Mark Mason said the bank was prioritising the transformation of data in its 30 critical regulatory reports, but that one such report alone had 750,000 lines of code.
There have been various </news/kathleen-martin>comings and goings </news/david-zimmerman-citi> in Citi's data team as it gets to grips with the scale of this challenge. That team is now led by Tim Ryan, a former partner from PWC who was hired in June 2024 and promoted in September 2024. </news/citi-s-apparent-demotion-of-anand-selvakesari-is-not-what-it-seems-but-still-complicated>
The remaining 10% of regulatory data issues not withstanding, things seem to be going well. Citi CEO Jane Fraser said that the transformation has left Citi in a good place. The bank now has a single repository for data in the institutional area of the bank, for example, and Fraser said this leaves it well placed to make the most of AI. By comparison, Citi's wealth management business was still said to be heavily dependent on spreadsheets </news/citi-stands-accused-of-attempting-to-implement-ai-on-the-back-of-excel-spreadsheets> as recently as June 2024.
Citi has an investor day on May 7 and more will be revealed then. Gonzalo Luchetti, Citi's new and "energised" CFO said today that the bank expects headcount to come down throughout this year. Fraser added that the bank is focused on achieving "structural efficiencies over time.” She said the bank will use AI and automation and these efficiencies to "supercharge investments in a self-funded way."
This could be taken to imply that as AI reduces headcount, Citi will have more money to invest in more AI. Algorithms don't need severance.

item.title: Networking for $1m quant and AI jobs really begins at 14-years-old
item.description:
Getting a top job as a STEM graduate takes a lot of preparation and years of forward planning, but the process starts earlier than you might think in elite quant trading houses </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay>. Many of Hudson River Trading's </news/hudson-river-trading-intern-pay> earliest cohort of interns, who have since become acclaimed in the AI </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking> space, knew each other as teenagers.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Scott Wu, CEO of AI coding platform Cognition, said that Hudson River Trading's first intern cohort met in high school on the competitive Olympiad circuit, where competitors can be as young as 14 (or even younger for extreme prodigies). Multiple of Wu's Cognition co-founders also emanated from the Olympiad scene, and interned with him at HRT. There were just 10 interns in total, but other major names include Alexandr Wang, chief AI officer of Meta and Hyperliquid founder Jeffrey Yan. </news/jeffrey-yan-hyperliquid>
Craziest part is we all knew each other already in high school! Along with @randomjohnnyh <https://twitter.com/randomjohnnyh?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw> (Perplexity cofounder), @demi_guo_ <https://twitter.com/demi_guo_?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw> (Pika CEO), @stevenkplus1 <https://twitter.com/stevenkplus1?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw> and Andrew (Cognition), and many others. We all grew up in different states but met thru the olympiad scene.
Vividly remember… https://t.co/hQZIl4VEyW <https://t.co/hQZIl4VEyW>
— Scott Wu (@ScottWu46) April 14, 2026 <https://twitter.com/ScottWu46/status/2043936583968240103?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw>
As internship </news/high-frequency-trading-internship> programs at HRT and other quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> firms have grown, the Olympiad scene has become a significant source of talent. One insider in the space told us that they're particularly loved at 'New York' style trading firms </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-hft> like Hudson River and Tower Research, which are known for being more scientific and being founded by mathematicians. "You can probably get an internship with some New York-style firms just by virtue of having an Olympiad medal," he said.
That doesn't mean other trading firms like Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> don't love Olympiad medalists. Startup founder and ex-Jane Street intern Hilal Hussein said last year </news/jane-street-target-universities#:~:text=Hilal%20Hussein%2C%20an%20MIT%20student%20who%20interned%20at%20Jane%20Street%2C%20said%20in%20a%20YouTube%20video%20last%20year%20that%20Jane%20Street%20and%20other%20quant%20firms%20were%20attending%20his%20high%20school%20mathematics%20tournaments.%C2%A0> that various quant firms, including Jane Street, have strong presences at Olympiad events today. Zhuo Qun Song </news/2023/08/citadel-olympiad-hiring>, the most decorated Olympiad medalist of all time, previously interned at Jane Street and worked full-time at hedge fund Citadel, which hires tens of Olympiad competitors for its internship program each year.
What makes Olympiad competitors so valuable? Tom Lovering, a former medalist now working as a quant for Citadel, said in 2024 </news/citadel-olympiad-competition>that the competitions instil "a lasting appreciation for the joys of competition, collaboration, and continuous learning." Given their competitive nature, it's no surprise that so many of these Olympiad competitors have then gone on to become founders; now that people like Wang and Wu have paved that road, trading firms may have a hard time holding onto their top Olympiad stars.
