item.title: Citi's AI driven productivity boom spells hard times for banking contractors
item.description:
Banks have invested a lot of time and money into AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> so, naturally, they want to see some return on investment. While this has led many full-time employees to believe that their jobs will be at risk, the people in the most immediate danger are contractors. </news/banks-are-handing-out-unusual-bonuses-to-keep-contract-engineers>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
In Citi's Q1 earnings presentation released today, the bank declared a "reduction in technology contractors as a result of productivity." AI presumably has something to do with it; the bank said that advanced and agentic AI tools are being "utilized by 10,000+ engineers </news/developers-are-doubling-their-code-output-with-ai-is-that-a-bad-thing> to" - the presentation seems to have left the point unfinished but engineers are presumably doing something productive.
Citi said that AI has also "been used to remap 30+ years of legacy code in 2 days." Contractors in languages like COBOL, </news/cobol-jobs-banking> who specialized in working with this legacy tech, used to command great fees. </news/2018/09/cobol-programming-language-thats-pitting-dinosaurs-young-banking-engineers>.. perhaps not anymore.
It's not just Citi's tech teams being affected by AI. The bank said in its presentation that AI helped "creat[e] capacity of 1,700+ hours/month" in its markets team by automating tasks like "trade confirms, broker invoices, and interest claims investigations." It's notable that the language used is similar to the 'developer hours' that banks were reducing with their AI tools last year. Contractors in middle and back office functions may have equal cause for concern.
Contractors across banking have been complaining about their woes </news/tech-contractor-jobs-banking> for some time now. Day rates have been declining and roles are being outsourced to cheaper contractors in low cost locations. AI will only exacerbate this.
For full-time employees, AI appears to be more of a help than a hindrance. The presentation states that over 80% of staff have onboarded AI and that there have been as many interactions with AI tools in the past quarter than every prior quarter since their inception combined. There have been 42m total interactions but, at a headcount of ~230,000, Citi's data implies that employees had an average of just one interaction with AI per day in Q1. Still, the bank is rumored to be </news/hsbc-ai-job-cuts> weighing up a round of layoffs driven by automation, so don't get too comfortable.

item.title: Balyasny has got another portfolio manager for its new Abu Dhabi office
item.description:
Would you move to the UAE now? Maybe next month? Maybe next year? If so, Balyasny Asset Management (BAM) is hiring.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
BAM opened its Abu Dhabi office in March </news/hedge-fund-balyasny-s-abu-dhabi-office-has-its-inaugural-member>, which did not seem fortuitous timing what with the war. The business there is being built out by Yash Agarwal, a former ExodusPoint equities portfolio manager who is building out a team.
The Abu Dhabi office seems to be taking shape. Agarwal didn't respond to a request to comment for this article. Nor did BAM, but someone else is arriving. That person is Mathieu Recayte, an FX options trader.
Recayte, who is French and a mathematician, has joined BAM's UAE office. He too did not respond to a request to comment for this article. Recayte spent most of his career at Nomura for 12 years before a brief and ill-fated excursion to failed hedge fund Eisler Capital while closed late last year.
Abu Dhabi is a challenging place to be now. One trader told us he was stuck in Abu Dhabi </news/abu-dhabi-war-bankers>early in the war and that it was "incredibly traumatic." Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/brevan-howard-offers-abu-dhabi-staff-option-to-temporarily-relocate-as-war-continues-6a2cd00a> reported earlier this month that Brevan Howard </news/brevan-howard-abu-dhabi-jobs> has been offering Abu Dhabi staff the opportunity to relocate elsewhere until the war is over. Recayte might want to wait in London for a while before leaving.

item.title: Everyone at JPMorgan and Citi did well in Q1, except one group
item.description:
Q1 2026 reporting season is underway. After Goldman Sachs yesterday </news/goldman-sachs-immense-quarter-was-accompanied-by-400-job-cuts> came JPMorgan <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir/quarterly-earnings> and Citi <https://www.citigroup.com/global/investors/quarterly-earnings> today, and the results are equally stellar – on the face of it, at least.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
On the investment banking </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> side, JPMorgan performed well. A huge 82% increase in M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> revenue was the main driver, but the firm also posted a 46% increase in equity capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> (ECM) revenue. Debt capital markets (DCM) revenue was down by 7%, however.
At Citi, banking revenue was generally up, although not as dramatically. M&A was up by just 19% on Q1 of 2025, ECM was up a more impressive 64%, and DCM was down around 6%. The bank did note that banking expenses were up by 20% on Q1 of last year, primarily due to “higher compensation… reflecting performance.”
The decline in DCM is rather concerning. According to Dealogic, global DCM volume increased 7% between Q1 of 2025 to Q1 of 2026, going from $2.77tn to $2.97tn. It's not a huge leap, but JP and Citi's performances were clearly below that.
It was both banks’ traders, however, that completely knocked it out of the park. Both JPMorgan’s FICC and equities traders performed well, up by 21% and 17% respectively. It was an all-time record quarter for JP’s traders overall.
The bank credited the increase in FICC revenue to strong client activity in commodities, credit, and currencies, among others. It also noted that revenues were lower in rates. Its equities results were credited to increased client activity in general.
At Citi, traders also performed well. FICC and equities trading revenue was up by 13% and 39%, respectively. The bank credited FICC performance primarily to spread products – its rates and currencies team lagged, like JP’s. The monster equities performance was put down to both cash and derivatives, as well as prime services.
Interestingly, despite traders outperforming bankers so significantly, Citi’s market team only allocated an 11% increase in expenses, credit to both higher “performance-related compensation” as well as a volume-related expenses and legal expenses.
Goldman Sachs’ Q1 results </news/goldman-sachs-immense-quarter-was-accompanied-by-400-job-cuts>, which came out yesterday, were broadly similar. M&A Q1 revenue shot up up ahead of others, but the firm’s FICC team lagged rather awkwardly behind its peers. Headcount and compensation at Goldman and JP, the only two banks that have comparable metrics, was also up broadly in line.

item.title: HSBC's Citi execution guy has finished the executing
item.description:
HSBC's reorg is all but done. Speaking to Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-14/hsbc-ceo-warns-mideast-conflict-is-denting-global-confidence?cmpid=041426_morningemea&utm_campaign=morningemea&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260414> yesterday, CEO Georges Elhedery said the “heavy lifting” of the reorganization has already occurred. All that remains now is for the bank to cut 20,000 jobs </news/hsbc-ai-job-cuts> through the deployment of AI.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Gerry Keefe will not be assisting with these future cuts. Despite being a key execution tool in last year's unexpected HSBC restructuring as ECM and M&A businesses </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts>in Europe and Asia were closed, Keefe is leaving the bank. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-14/hsbc-s-top-wall-street-europe-banker-keefe-quits-after-overhaul?srnd=homepage-europe>reports that he has quit and has something lined up. “Gerry Keefe has decided to resign from HSBC to take up a new role,” a spokesperson for HSBC told Bloomberg. “He leaves with our best wishes,” he noted.
Keefe's exit marks the departure of one of the key ex-Citi people in leading roles </news/hsbc-is-increasingly-run-by-people-who-ve-joined-from-citi> at HSBC's corporate and institutional bank. Keefe was hired from Citi by Michael Roberts in 2021. Roberts, who is CEO of corporate and institutional banking at HSBC, joined the bank in 2019 after 33 years at Citi.
At HSBC, Keefe was initially appointed head of global banking for the Americas in 2021. He then became co-head of global banking coverage in 2023 and head of banking for Europe and the Americas in January 2025, when the restructuring of Europe and the Americas was announced. As such, he is thought to have been instrumental in executing the investment bank's restructuring plan. That plan came as a shock, but Elhedery has said often that it was necessary to be "ruthless;" <https://www.ft.com/content/b18d7451-4703-49a2-a687-e56a375118d6?syn-25a6b1a6=1>there were suggestions that some senior people at HSBC had been collecting $1m pay for doing nothing much. </news/hsbc-bankers-pay>
It is not clear what Keefe will do next. He has long experience of corporate banking from Citi, including senior oversight of selling treasury solutions.
HSBC already has a Keefe replacement. Bloomberg reports that Jo Miyake will do Keefe's job on an interim basis. Miyake joined from BofA in 2021 and is currently head of banking for Asia and the Middle East. Miyake was latterly COO for commodities sales and trading at Bank of America.

item.title: Where to find the best lifestyle - and pay - in finance
item.description:
Careers in finance are known, above all, for two things: excellent compensation </banking-compensation-guide>, and abysmal work-life balance. But not everyone working in finance is a banker - or working at a bank. Do they all share the same fate?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Data from our 2026 Compensation & Lifestyle </banking-compensation-guide> report suggests not. Based on our Q1 poll, which surveyed over 2,700 professionals in the industry, suggests not.
As expected, the hardest-working people in finance are investment bankers </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> – and more specifically, the investment bankers that work in M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs>.
M&A bankers </news/what-are-ma-jobs> responding to our survey said they worked 67.3 hours per week on average. This was on par with the 67.9 hours they said they worked in 2024 and the 67.1 they said they worked in 2023. The average banker’s hours are far from the infamous 100+ hour weeks reported at the height of the pandemic.
Beyond M&A, only Debt Capital Markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> professionals averaged above 60 hours a week. Equity Capital Markets, credit sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>, and equities sales & trading professionals all reported average work weeks of around 50 hours a week, which was less than some of their colleagues in support functions such as risk, for example.
Support function professionals working in finance </news/accounting-job-finance-banking>, risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank>, operations </news/operations-jobs-banking>, and compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance> (FROC), as well as financial technology </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank>, work far more standard hours.
Respondents to our survey working in technology reported working just 43.8 hours per week in 2025. This was up on the 38.9 hours that they reported working in 2024. No other function reported working anything below 40 hours a week, ever.
Curiously, there were only two groups that reported working fewer hours in 2025 than they did in 2024: people that worked in compliance, and people that worked in equity capital markets (ECM).
Pay per hour also changed as a consequence of 2025’s bonuses. The best-paid finance professionals are still working for hedge funds, earning almost $300 on average for each hour they work. Within investment banks, sales & trading professionals were also clear, earning $189 per hour.
Significant bonus increases also meant that quants were earning an eye-watering $144 per hour of work, on par with what M&A professionals earned ($148 per hour) and ahead of both debt and equity capital markets professionals, who earned $136 and $96 per hour on average.
The poorest compensation per hour was to be found people who earned the lowest compensation in general. Professionals in FROC (Finance, Risk, Operations, and Compliance) earned $68, $94, $56, and $76 per hour worked on average – the lowest-paid four functions of the 14 we polled.

item.title: Standard Chartered hired an ex-Bank of America quant strat MD in London
item.description:
After various credit trading </news/credit-trading-jobs-ruined> desks reportedly underperformed in 2025, </news/bonuses-are-paid-and-london-high-yield-traders-are-jumping-jobs#:~:text=The%20reason%20for,to%20be%20underway.> banks are attempting to shore up their ranks this year. Standard Chartered's latest senior hire is an experienced credit quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> who, most recently, worked on a low latency credit exchange.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Geoff Morton is joining Standard Chartered as a managing director of credit trading product management. He was most recently global head of strategists </news/strats-jobs-investment-banks> at MarketAxess. He also previously co-founded Full Cycle Analytics, a bond trading intelligence startup.
Morton, an Oxford PhD </news/do-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance> physicist, spent the bulk of his career as a credit quant in banks. He spent nine years in the fixed income research and strategies (FIRST) group of BNP Paribas, working on hedging simulations for CDOs around the time of the 2008 financial crisis. He then spent nine years at Bank of America, where he became an MD and global head of electronic trading credit strats.
Banks appear eager to retain their credit traders as well as hire in new ones. Our 2026 compensation and lifestyle report </banking-compensation-guide> found that bonuses in credit sales and trading were up 25% year-on-year, </news/highest-paying-jobs-in-banking> the most of any major division. Making sure that they have the right products to use is presumably part of the retention package.

item.title: Morning Coffee: 31-year-old trader's wife ok with seeing him 30 mins a day. Bank CEOs begin rumbling on economy
item.description:
What's it like to be married to a top trader? Like not being married to a top trader: you may barely see them; even at night they are fiddling with the terminal.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
31-year-old Jeffrey Yan might not be locked in an affair with Bloomberg products, but he appears to be strong on subjugating his relationship to work. His partner might say it's worth it: Yan is the founder of Hyperliquid, a blockchain and cryptocurrency trading exchange that employs 11 people and generated $900m in profit last year. When he cancelled a dinner date she had made it was because he was going to lose $100k.
Yan has a history of working 100 hour weeks, 14 hour days. He cut his teeth in the equities algorithms team at Hudson River and trades "perps" - perpetual futures derivative contracts with no expiry dates. He also owns and runs Hyperliquid, a decentralized exchange operating on the blockchain, which he and his small team created themselves, and so it's not just trades that keep him up. Last year, there were nights he was awake at 1.30am and then again at 3am because of issues with Hyperliquid's infrastructure.
Colossus <https://colossus.com/article/beyond-the-sky-jeffrey-yan-hyperliquid/>has a profile of Yan. He is a fan of work. He says: “I think people are just a bit too soft in general. The brain is an organ. If you need to work more hours, you can train for it.” He cuts his own hair and devotes Sunday mornings only to leisure. Yan is described as 'ambitious but not arrogant.' He employs a small handful of intelligent introverts, one of whom likes plush toys and all of whom need bodyguards because of their net worth.
Yan thinks Hyperliquid can take over the world. His "aspirational goal" is for HyperLiquid to house all tradeable products, but he admits that this may take "multi-decades" and be hard. His partner may not see him for a while.
Separately, David Solomon at Goldman Sachs </news/goldman-sachs-immense-quarter-was-accompanied-by-400-job-cuts> feels gruffly enthusiastic about dealmaking in the current economic situation and yesterday said that CEOs see this moment as the moment to, "drive scale and scale creation in businesses with significant technological change." This thrust, "candidly trumps some of the geopolitical risks because they have the opportunity to do consolidating trades," he said.
Other CEOs seem to be saying something different. Georges Elhedery, who last year pivoted HSBC's investment bank towards the Middle East and Asia, told Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-14/hsbc-ceo-warns-mideast-conflict-is-denting-global-confidence?cmpid=041426_morningemea&utm_campaign=morningemea&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260414>that the "some of these uncertainties have initially started to weigh on general confidence,” and that the broader economic impact of war in the Middle East could become a broader problem. Peter Orszag at Lazard deployed a more metaphorical approach and told Semafor we are in a "Road Runner" <https://www.semafor.com/article/04/13/2026/lazard-ceo-peter-orszag-says-global-economy-hasnt-yet-felt-the-impact-of-the-middle-east-conflict> moment, implying pedalling in mid-air, a precipitous fall and then running on as if nothing happened.
Meanwhile...
HSBC's head of CEEMEA DCM is going to Bank of America to do something similar. Nour Safra is also leaving HSBC and going to Goldman in Dubai. She was head of DCM for the Middle East and North Africa. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-13/hsbc-said-to-lose-second-dubai-bond-banker-to-wall-street-rival>
Goldman Sachs shares fell after a surprise drop in fixed income trading revenues and the revelation that the deal pipeline is weaker. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-13/goldman-stock-traders-trounce-wall-street-record-by-1-billion?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260413&utm_campaign=author_21835441>
David Solomon said war impacted IPOs. “With the conflict in the Middle East, IPO activity slowed a little bit, particularly in March...The level of uncertainty is higher.” (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/goldman-sachs-gs-q1-bank-earnings-report-stock-2026-c66478dc?mod=lead_feature_below_a_pos2>
Balyasny hired Dara Falahi from Capstone in New York. (FN) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/balyasny-hires-ex-capstone-md-dara-falahi-7c706f93>
Lucien Selce and Alexis Kuperfis were jailed in Geneva for insider trading on a tip from a SocGen banker. They earned $13m and $5m from their trades respectively. Selce has 'gray hair neatly trimmed on the sides and brushed.' Kuperfis likes to wear 'a quarter-zip blue jumper and chinos.' (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-13/two-traders-convicted-in-paris-over-a-23-million-insider-coup?taid=69dce4a274610a0001d84096&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
XTX Markets CTO Joshua Leahy is going on his way. (Finance Magnates) <https://www.financemagnates.com/executives/after-more-than-a-decade-at-xtx-markets-cto-joshua-leahy-steps-down-also-as-director/>
Parents are paying $30k+ for their children to be coached into finance jobs. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-13/parents-of-college-students-are-paying-thousands-for-early-career-coaching?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=linkedin&sref=DK3y4h9m>
Luis von Ahn, who runs Duolingo, gets taxi drivers to interview prospective job candidates on their way to the office. "Part of the interview for us is how they treated their driver. Our belief is if they are gonna be mean to their driver they are probably gonna be to other people, particularly under them, so we did not want that.” (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/business/companies-markets/article/duolingo-tax-driver-test-job-applications-hires-r5s6wrfcj>

item.title: Morning Coffee: 31-year-old trader's wife ok with seeing him 30 mins a day. Bank CEOs begin rumbling on economy
item.description:

item.title: Jane Street engineers need to be "high-fidelity" communicators
item.description:
The importance of communication in financial services jobs cannot be overstated. Banks </news/goldman-sachs-wealth-management-jobs#:~:text=Some%20things%20never,is%20incredibly%20important.%22>and hedge funds </news/2023/10/citadel-goldman-sachs-114> often wax lyrical about its importance, as do electronic trading firms. A production engineer at trading firm Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> has espoused the importance of "high-fidelity" communication among its tech staff.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
In a video <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zR9PpXWsKFQ>published by Jane Street over the weekend, engineer Mark Doss said that Jane Street engineers need to be able to "speak really effectively in a high fidelity way." You need to not only understand trading terminology, but the "pretty nuanced" way in which its traders speak in order to pinpoint an issue they're complaining about.
For example, you need to have deep business context to understand terminology used by traders so as not to create any friction. If a trader says that "spoos market data looks slow", he said that you should instinctively know to check order engines in Aurora, Illinois. (Spoos refer to indexed futures of the S&P 500 which trades on CME, based in Aurora).
A benefit of Jane Street's culture </news/working-for-jane-street> is that you're given license to speak in a "sort of no-BS way." Doss said that he doesn't have to "couch his language" or worry about "reputational impact" when pointing out an error in its systems, allowing tech staff to more easily address problems.
Knowing when not to talk is just as important as communication itself. Doss said that Jane Street technologists "obsess over signal-to-noise ratio" provided to traders, who optimize their screens down to the last pixel </news/jane-street-s-traders-on-1-4m-have-fighter-pilot-eyes-high-pain-thresholds>to fit in as much relevant information as possible. "Everyone deeply understands that noisy alerts are worse than useless," Doss said.

item.title: Goldman Sachs' immense quarter was achieved with 400 fewer people
item.description:
Goldman Sachs had an exceptional 1st quarter. The firm's Q1 results <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/current/pdfs/2026-q1-earnings-results-presentation.pdf>, released today, show it generating the second highest net revenues ever. The standouts were the firm's M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma>bankers, whose revenues were up 89% year-on-year, and its equities capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> bankers, whose revenues were up 45% year-on-year, and its equities salespeople and traders, </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> whose revenues were up 27% over the same period.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
These were good times. But good times do not always mean good hiring. In the first quarter, Goldman instead shaved its headcount lower.
The firm ended Q1 with 47,000 people, down from the 47,400 it began the quarter with. The net headcount reduction came after Goldman said </news/hsbc-ai-job-cuts> it has begun to look at workstreams susceptible to automation by AI. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/world-at-work/goldman-sachs-make-small-job-cuts-performance-april-source-says-2026-03-19/#:~:text=Goldman%20Sachs%20to%20make%20performance%2Dbased%20job%20cuts,with%20the%20matter%20told%20Reuters%20on%20Thursday.> reported in March that the firm planned to make performance related cuts in April, with a focus on underperformers.
Cutting headcount in the first quarter is nothing unusual for Goldman Sachs. As we noted last year, </news/goldman-sachs-london-hiring>in the past decade Goldman made Q1 cuts half of the time. However, those cuts were more frequently in the 200 person range. At no point were they above 300 people.
Will Goldman hire more talent as the year progresses? This remains to be seen. The firm said today <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/current/pdfs/2026-q1-earnings-results-presentation.pdf>that its M&A deals backlog declined in the first quarter, suggesting that war in the Middle East might be discouraging activity and may yet dampen enthusiasm for recruiting. However, speaking on today's investor call, Goldman Sachs' CEO David Solomon said corporates are "incredibly engaged" and willing to do deals because "they operate in the long term" and "believe they have an opportunity to drive scale and innovation."
In equities, the most dramatic revenue growth was in prime financing, suggesting churn in largely automated hedge fund business. In fixed income trading, revenues fell across everything from rates to credit to mortgages and only revenues from commodities and FX trading grew.
More headcount reductions may be coming. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-layoffs-cuts-underperformers-sra-spring-summer-2026-3> reported in March that Goldman is adopting a different approach to headcount cuts this year. Instead of making one or two rounds of large cuts, the firm reportedly plans to make rolling rounds of cuts for poor performers. Insiders at the bank tell us the firm had hoped for a wave of voluntary resignations after bonuses were paid, but that this didn't happen.
The good news is that Goldman's falling headcount was accompanied by higher spending on compensation. Pay per head rose by 10% in the first quarter as a result.

item.title: Electronic trading firm G-Research is hiring engineers and salespeople
item.description:
Growing a quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> firm takes more than just hiring quants. Secretive British trading firm G-Research </news/g-research-pay> seems to be in agreement, as it has been hiring senior figures for both its engineering </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank> and sales </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> functions.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Kevin Rands joined G-Research in London this month as its head of edge trading technology. The role appears to be an application of edge computing, </news/2020/09/guide-to-edge-computing> when computations are moved closer to a source of data to minimize latency.
Rands spent the last eight years at asset manager T. Rowe Price. He held various tech leadership roles, but was most recently head of fixed income tech, head of investment risk tech and head of front office architecture and engineering. Rands also worked on equity derivatives infrastructure for both JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley, holding an executive director role in both banks.
On the sales side, G-Research hired Julia Ly to work on strategic data partnerships. She spent the last four years at S&P, where she was EMEA head of hedge funds within the chief client office.
Last month, it also hired James Narramore, an SVP and co-head of equity alpha capture at London hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Winton. Narramore, a data specialist, doesn't disclose his new role at G-Research, but he has a background in sales and business development.

item.title: The highest paying jobs in banking are not for bankers
item.description:
Bonus season is over for most of the world’s financial services professionals, and it’s time for a time honoured tradition: the eFinancialCareers 2025-2026 Compensation and Lifestyle Report </banking-compensation-guide>, to benchmark how your bonus changed compared to your peers.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
While data from our report showed that the global average bonus was up 5%, there was a lot of nuance in the numbers. Salaries were broadly down. And bonuses were up strongly in some sectors – and down quite badly in others.
In our absolute terms, the highest bonus increases were to be found among hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> professionals and investment bankers </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process>, who saw their bonuses increase by around 22% to 20%, respectively.
Within investment banking, defined as M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> and both debt and equity capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> (DCM and ECM, respectively), the biggest increases were for DCM professionals, whose bonuses increased by 20% on average. Equities capital markets professionals reported that their bonuses increased by 13.3%, and M&A bankers reported that their bonuses increased by 15.8%.
The only group of professionals who saw their bonuses decline were those in support functions (Finance </news/accounting-job-finance-banking>, Risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank>, Operations </news/operations-jobs-banking>, and Compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance> – FROC). In this group, the biggest fall was for compliance professionals, whose average bonuses declined by 11.6%. Bonuses also fell significantly for operations professionals, where the decline was 5.3%. Interestingly, professionals from these two functions also reported substantial salary decreases, of 13.7% and 8.6%, respectively.
Quantitative finance </news/quant-jobs-finance> professionals (quants) also achieved 32% increases in their bonuses. Interestingly, despite the impressive bonus increases, quants in our survey did not think they were particularly well-paid; just 32% of those in our survey thought so, compared to 37% of finance professionals, who received a net bonus decrease.
The biggest drop in compensation was for people that sold and traded </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> commodities. Bonus for these professionals fell by 37%, and salaries also declined by 4.5%. Overall, compensation among commodities traders was down by 16.8%. Just 19% of commodities professionals responding to our survey thought they were paid fairly, compared to 33% and 37% of their colleagues in equities and macro trading, respectively.
Our 2025-2026 Compensation & Lifestyle Report can be downloaded here </banking-compensation-guide>

item.title: Balyasny hired a Brevan Howard PM in Singapore as it looks to grow in Asia
item.description:
Balyasny </news/balyasny-hedge-fund>has plans for growth in Asia, after revenue in the region increased 82% over the last two years <https://www.businesstimes.com.sg/wealth/wealth-investing/balyasny-asia-revenue-jumps-82-record-during-expansion-drive>. Its Singaporean </news/singapore-s-finance-jobs-vs-hong-kong>office, home to almost half of the hedge fund's ~130 Asian investment staff, is welcoming a quant </news/quant-jobs-finance>PM with a French background.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Trung Nguyen announced via LinkedIn that he's joining Balyasny as a portfolio manager in Singapore. He spent the last year and a half as a PM at Brevan Howard, and almost six years before that as a quant researcher for Cubist, the quant arm of hedge fund Point72.
Nguyen spent the bulk of his career, however, working for a French hedge fund in Paris. He spent seven and a half years with quant fund Capital Fund Management </news/capital-fund-management-cfm-hedge-fund> (CFM), joining after graduating with a PhD from the Grandes Écoles. CFM is known for having very few portfolio managers but Nguyen was one of them, working on directional strategies.
Balyasny has ~60 investment staff in each of its Hong Kong and Singapore offices but, as of August, employed 21 PMs </news/where-do-portfolio-managers-work-balyasny> split evenly across the two countries. Its Tokyo base is smaller, housing just three PMs as of August, but The Business Times reported in January that the hedge fund is tripling its Japanese office space.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Morning Coffee: Stressful existence of the ex-Goldman Sachs trader who is never fired. Hedge fund manager capitulates in face of 15 women
item.description:
If you leave Goldman Sachs and work for a multistrategy </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-hiring>pod shop hedge fund, your life may be fraught. Risk appetite at such funds is low; stop losses are non-negotiable. Commodities trading houses can be more tolerant, but do not expect a smooth ride there either.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Ex-Goldman Sachs trader Yaoyao Liu has not had a smooth ride at Vitol. When a youthful Liu left Goldman Sachs in London after three years in 2012, he joined the world's largest independent oil trader. This year has been harsh. The Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/business/energy-oil/wrong-way-bets-on-oil-had-a-star-trader-hundreds-of-millions-in-the-hole-b4feff18?mod=panda_wsj_author_alert> reports that the oil trading team lead by Liu at Vitol lost several hundred million dollars at the start of the latest war in the Middle East.
It's not the first time that Liu has been bucked by markets. At the start of the COVID pandemic in 2020, the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/a8616e60-7748-4b61-b7e4-caa6917f1711?syn-25a6b1a6=1>reported that Liu and his team made a large loss of an unquantified magnitude. The team was betting that oil prices would rebound; they didn't. Vitol's net income reportedly fell by 70% in the first quarter of 2020, for reasons that were unclear.
If Liu and his team were at a multistrategy hedge fund, this might have been the end of it. They would likely join the procession of traders rotating between major funds. Instead, they're still there at Vitol, where they seem to enjoy a high degree of protection.
The WSJ says that Liu - who is based in Dubai (or at least was until recently), runs a kind of internal hedge fund at Vitol. He makes huge bets on commodity derivatives and he benefits from the market visibility afforded by Vitol's physical trading business. Vitol reportedly keeps Liu's positions secret internally: colleagues do not know what they are. Unconfirmed speculation suggests that his team's recent losses might be related to bad bets that diesel would rise relative to jet fuel, or that Dubai crude might fall versus benchmark Brent.
Vitol's willingness to stick with Liu through the big downs is almost certainly related to his big ups. In 2022, Liu reportedly made $2bn in trading gains amidst Russia's invasion of Ukraine. This was less than Citadel's $8bn of commodities trading gains for the same year, but it was not inconsequential.
Liu's big profits mean that big hedge funds want to hire him. Liu presumably doesn't want to move and be spat out for breaching funds' tight risk limits.
The WSJ reports that Liu has already made back "some of the [recent] loss" and that Vitol as a whole is up for the year. Nonetheless, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-11/energy-trader-vitol-reorganizes-derivatives-team-after-losses?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260411&utm_campaign=author_22490032>reports that Vitol has been "reorganizing" its London derivatives team and that some traders might be moving on as a result. It sounds stressful. Liu, who graduated with a first class degree in quantum engineering and quantum chemistry from Cambridge University, is possibly inured to it.
Separately, Crispin Odey has also had a very stressful time and seems to have decide that enough is enough.
The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/69666b46-8452-4807-add9-712a0394a53c?sharetype=blocked&syn-25a6b1a6=1> reports that Odey has decided not to pursue his £79m libel case against it. Odey's lawyers reportedly said he had been “forced to accept" that he would probably not succeed on a public interest basis. However, had the case gone ahead, Odey thought that he would have been successful, "in demonstrating that he is not the violent predator he was presented as..."
The FT notes that it had 15 women willing to come to court and testify in favour of its series of articles reporting sexual misconduct allegations against Odey. Odey's lawyers did not respond to a request to comment for this article.
Meanwhile...
Commodities trading houses lost billions of dollars in the early days of the Gulf War. "For most participants the situation was a surprise. Before the war started, there was a strong conviction in the market that prices would fall, and because of the war, they spiked.” (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/3baeeff3-8c7e-4fcd-b704-3ddde3abdbcc?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
JPMorgan Chase, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Citigroup and Bank of America report Q1 results this week and are expected to unveil the highest combined quarterly revenues from their trading businesses for at least 12 years. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/7dfb2398-f414-4c82-927c-5db4d34cb5da?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Private capital firm Partners Group will also be inhibiting investor withdrawals if things get too intense. “If we see redemptions in these amounts above the gates, we will gate." (Bloomberg) <https://www.ft.com/content/04be1b18-53db-48c2-97bd-3731851f6d65?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Private equity investments are now typically held for seven years, he pointed out, double the previous norm. Assets held for more than six years are considered potentially stranded, meaning they’re at risk of write downs due to declining performance or a lack of new funds. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-04-10/jpmorgan-s-jamie-dimon-is-surprised-more-private-equity-firms-aren-t-using-ipos>
Bank CEOs are rushing to profess their love of the UAE. “Our support is unwavering...Our clients’ ambitions across the region haven’t changed,” says David Solomon at Goldman Sachs. Goldman Sachs is expanding its partnership with the Qatar Investment Authority, which may commit $25bn to its asset management arm. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-10/finance-titans-bet-mideast-resilience-will-outweigh-war-fallout>
It's not easy living in the UAE and being woken up bombs every night. "The previous night was a rough one: alert, projectile, boom, alert, projectile, boom and so on. Morning life is normal again, as if nothing happened." (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/c9029475-b7cc-419c-823b-4caf78247ecf>
Anthropic's Mythos model has been released to a few organisations. This includes banks like JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs and Citi. There have been suggestions that Mythos might be used to hack banks' own sites. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-10/wall-street-banks-try-out-anthropic-s-mythos-as-us-urges-testing?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260410&utm_campaign=author_21835441>
What does AI do to junior hedge fund jobs? At smaller funds, roles might disappear: "One or two people on an investment team will be able to cover more stocks with more rigor with these tools...." At larger funds: "I think what you will see with junior analysts is that the junior analyst resource will be shifted more towards a primary research role." (YouTube) <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lgDC4200x0U>
The exciting new reality for managers at EY: "They're increasingly responsible for cultivating a psychologically safe environment where people can experiment, fail forward and learn from AI; coaching, developing others; and leading teams comprised of both humans and agents." (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/ey-talent-chief-ai-changing-employee-recruitment-evaluations-promotions-careers-2026-4>
How to handle job cuts in banks. "Survivors of enough cycles know the drill. They don’t broadcast. They observe, document, and quietly assess their own position. The clearest reading on the barometer belongs to those who treat every flutter of the flag as another layer added to their wealth of experience and knowledge, the steady accrual of sharper instincts, refined protocols, and unshakeable resilience." (Substack) < https://substack.com/home/post/p-193143691>
The job market is bleak, unless you are willing to train AI models. Patrick Ciriello designs software for banks but he lost his job in 2023. He now works 40 hours a week earning $20 an hour. (Guardian) <https://www.theguardian.com/technology/ng-interactive/2026/apr/07/ai-training-work-jobs>

item.title: How to leave Bank of America, live in Bali, then work from home
item.description:
It's Friy-ay and if it seems you might want to leave banking, live in Bali, and return to London to work in finance adjacent roles from home, we would like to let you that this is all a possibility.
Meena Sadeghi has done it.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Sadeghi spent five years working in investment banking for Bank of America in London before leaving in 2021. She briefly got a job working on Amazon roll-ups in the e-commerce space before being made redundant. She went to Bali for seven months, came back to London, and has been working on a contract basis for private equity firms, corporates and start-ups ever since.
Sadeghi now describes herself as "Nomad Advisory" and she is self-employed. It sounds a fair alternative to the corporate grind. "I’m more focused on how I work - I do my best work in high-trust environments where I’m judged on output," says Sadeghi. "Most of my roles over the last few years have been remote-first."
Sadeghi's new modus operandi wasn't a foregone conclusion. When she first lost her job at the Amazon consolidator, she got a private equity job offer and considered studying an MBA. But she turned down the private equity job offer because she wanted to make a more considered move instead of "just following what comes next." And she forewent the MBA because it would cost $250k, when what she really wanted was "a break from finance and a change of environment." - "I realised I didn’t need an MBA for that," Sadeghi says on LinkedIn. She booked the one way ticket to Bali instead.
Speaking to us, Sadeghi says she mostly needed space to think. "I’d already done investment banking and the roles post-MBA were the same ones I could already access.” She adds that it's becoming acceptable to take time out to recalibrate: “The idea that you need to move straight from one role to the next is starting to break - more people are questioning the default path.”
Sadeghi says time on the beach has become more acceptable. “It used to be about your CV - now it’s about how you think and how you operate. People are looking for judgment, initiative, and what you’ve done outside structured roles, not just which bank you worked at.”
Writing on LinkedIn, she also claims to heard a KKR partner saying that simply working at Goldman Sachs won't get you a job in private equity now. You need to do "something different" and to have "stories that nobody else has."
If you want to leave banking now and maybe work in private equity in the future, seven months in Bali may therefore still be a good thing. Presuming you can get there amidst fuel shortages due to the war.

item.title: Banking interns must make 100 applications per job. Unless they are diverse
item.description:
It’s pretty common knowledge that summer internships </news/internship-investment-bank> in finance are very difficult to get into. Historical acceptance rates have hovered anywhere from single digits to decimals – Goldman’s acceptance rate </news/goldman-sachs-internship-acceptance-rate#:~:text=If%20you%20thought%20getting%20into,in%202022%20it%20accepted%201.27%25.>, for example, dipped from around 2% in 2013 to around 0.72% in 2025. But is that true across the industry?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Data from student application tracker Trackr, <https://the-trackr.com/> formerly known as the Bristol Tracker, shed some light on the situation. The answer was pretty nasty: the average number of applications that aspiring financier students had to make, per offer, shot right up this year.
In 2024, students made an average of 68 applications to get an offer. In 2025, they made an average of 75. And in 2026, they made a whopping 101 applications per offer. Those are year-of-year increases of 10% and 35%, respectively. This was also the first year that the average student needed to make over 100 applications to get an offer, Trackr noted.
With such a hill to climb, students will want to maximise their odds. And there are two things that students can, err, do to improve their odds: be female, or be black.
Women only had to make 79.1 applications to get an offer on average, compared to the 111.1 applications that men had to make on average. Being a woman means you must make 29% fewer applications than a man to get an offer.
Students of black heritage, meanwhile, only had to make 49.8 applications to get an offer, compared to 105.8 application per offer for white students. Being black means you must make 58% fewer applications than a white person to get an offer.
Another thing that can help your applications, of course, is getting a spring internship. Without a spring week, students need to make a rather daunting 137.6 applications, on average, to get an offer. Those that got a spring week only had to make 73.3 applications. And those that convert their spring internships only had to make 39.3 applications to get an offer for a summer internship.
It was “definitely the toughest recruitment cycle yet,” one student told Trackr. “Summers [internships] have become ridiculously competitive.” It makes sense; more than half of students who applied for internship did not get any offer at all, Trackr noted. But – the bright side, if there is one – is that 46% of students did receive some sort of summer internship offer at the end of the day. Not bad.

item.title: Goldman Sachs' rare female London partner quietly left for BlackRock
item.description:
Goldman Sachs doesn't have many female partners </news/2021/10/goldman-sachs-female-partners> in London and now it has one fewer: Hilary Lopez, head of Goldman's EMEA third party wealth business has left. It's thought that she's joining BlackRock as head of EMEA wealth.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Lopez has worked for BlackRock before. She was last seen there in 2008. Then she was at BNY Mellon for over a decade. And at Goldman Sachs from 2021. Now she's back at BlackRock, and observers suggest it is a "huge win" for the fund management firm.
BlackRock declined to comment on its win for this article. Lopez didn't respond to a request to comment.
BlackRock's last head of EMEA wealth was Ivan Pascual Ferrer, a formerly cherubic Spaniard who was there for 15 years before leaving for HarbourVest in January. Lopez seems to be sitting in Pascual Ferrer's warm seat.
Goldman's third party wealth business provides investment solutions to third, parties like private banking firms. These solutions include things like white label technology and ETFs. Goldman is keen to grow in the area after, for example, buying <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/2025/goldman-sachs-to-acquire-innovator-capital-management>Innovator Capital Management and sprinkling a shower of 13 ETFs into the London market last year.
While Lopez is joining BlackRock from Goldman, Goldman recently hired Brett Olson, formerly of BlackRock. Olson was last at BlackRock in 2023 and he joined Goldman this month as head of EMEA ETFs in London.
Various senior women have left Goldman in the past year, including, for example Anne Marie Darling. </news/anne-marie-darling-goldman-sachs-partner-barclays-coo>Goldman's global third party wealth business is, however, run by a woman (Padideh Raphael) and by a man (Byron Lake). Raphael says she's been on maternity leave three times during her 26 year career at Goldman. Lake joined from JPMorgan in 2024 and became her co-head in May last year.

item.title: UBS alternative capital group chief reappeared at probably better place to be
item.description:
Getting restructured out of a bank you’ve worked at for 12 years, three months after a promotion, might not be the ideal way to end your time at a firm. The upsides, however, can be quite spectacular.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Simona Maellare joined Blackstone as its EMEA head of private wealth. She joined the firm from UBS, where she spent 12 years and was most recently global (co-)head of the firm’s alternative capital group, and global head of financial sponsors coverage. She was also made head of EMEA for OneUBS some three months before departing the firm.
Maellare’s departure from UBS and arrival at Blackstone came after some rigmarole </news/simona-maellare-ubs>. Bloomberg reported <https://www.blackstone.com/news/press/blackstone-names-rashmi-madan-as-global-head-of-portfolio-solutions-for-private-wealth-and-simona-maellare-as-head-of-emea-for-private-wealth/> last June that UBS was restructuring, and that the alternative capital group would be dismantled – with Maellare as a victim of that reshuffling. Her fellow alternative capital co-head, Terry Sullivan, stayed on at UBS as chairman of its FIG team.
At Blackstone, Maellare replaced Rashmi Madan in her role as EMEA head of private wealth. Madan, a Blackstone veteran of more than 15 years, moved into a new role as global head of portfolio solutions at the firm, which the firm called <https://www.blackstone.com/news/press/blackstone-names-rashmi-madan-as-global-head-of-portfolio-solutions-for-private-wealth-and-simona-maellare-as-head-of-emea-for-private-wealth/> a promotion.
Private wealth is a major lynchpin in Blackstone’s strategy. With the industry struggling for exits <https://www.spglobal.com/market-intelligence/en/news-insights/articles/2026/1/private-equity-exits-rise-returns-fall-in-2025-96929032>, private wealth investors offer the unique opportunity for firms to invest in evergreen funds – one without exit deadlines, capital calls, drawdowns, and the likes. They also have a lower threshold for huge carried interest </news/what-is-carried-interest> payouts – 15%, versus the standard 20% at close-ended funds, according to private markets advisory firm Hamilton Lane <http://explore.hamiltonlane.com/2025-market-overview/evergreen-funds>. Lower carried interest thresholds, as well as no due date on investments, means that eight figures carried interest payments might be within sight for some.

item.title: Barclays' leaky rates trading team also leaked in Hong Kong
item.description:
Last year, Barclays' rates trading team lost at least five </news/deutsche-bank-pinched-barclays-head-of-european-rates-sales#:~:text=20%20Aug%202025,desk%20left%20earlier%20this%20year.> senior people in Europe. It also lost at least two senior people in Hong Kong; now that has increased to three.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Barclays isn't commenting, but we understand that Minyi Yang, a senior rates options trader who had worked at Barclays for his entire adult life since graduating from the London School of Economics with a first class degree in 2008, is not there any more.
Yang did not respond to a request to comment on his intentions. He may intend to join a hedge fund.
Predecessors who left Barclays' Hong Kong rates options team include Shumei Kameyam, who went to Dymon Asia last April last April and Jiujiu Xiong, the bank's former head of APAC rates option trading, who is now with the "35-year-old-killers" </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience> at Jain Global.
In London, Barclays' exits last year were centred on the European swaps desk. </news/barclays-head-of-usd-swaps-trading-left-for-a-hedge-fund>
Sources say that rates options traders are becoming more sought after as trading opportunities increase in line with Central Bank policy uncertainty. This is particularly the case in Asia, as the Bank of Japan normalizes rates while Chiense rates remain at historic lows.
, macro flow rates and FX traders used to be more popular with hedge fund due to the liquidity of their products. However, rates options traders seem to be in increasing demand in recent years due to improved liquidity and more trading opportunities around central bank policy uncertainties. This seems particularly true in Asia with BoJ gradually normalizing their policy rates while China rates and rates vol are stuck at historical low due to their current economic situation.
One Barclays trader said the departures are "perfectly normal" and that the bank is a good place to work. "There is definitely no bad blood," agrees a Hong Kong headhunter. Barclays has hired replacement traders in London in the form of Laura Brown and Lucile Flight. </news/barclay-keeps-hiring-the-hottest-female-macro-traders-in-london>Further hires are expected.

item.title: Morning Coffee: When executive assistants steal from bankers and buy handbags. The joy of being Apollo
item.description:
If you're an executive assistant (EA) working with senior bankers or hedge fund managers, you are exposed to temptation. A good EA might make $250k. But this is not enough to shop regularly at Louis Vuitton and Gucci. Or to buy your husband a speedboat.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This is probably why, every now and then, an EA accesses her boss's bank account and buys a selection of prestigious items before eventually being found out and prosecuted.
The latest to go down this road is Catalina Corona, who was working for Richard Schmeelk, a former partner at Salomon Brothers. Corona reportedly <https://nypost.com/2026/04/09/business/personal-assistant-pleads-guilty-to-stealing-10m-from-elderly-salomon-bros-exec-to-buy-louis-vuitton-gucci/?utm_source=twitter&utm_campaign=nypost&utm_medium=social> stole $30m from the now deceased Schmeelk and spent it on handbags, Apple products and flights. She pled guilty to charges of theft this week and now faces decades in prison.
Corona was not the first EA to extract money from Schmeelk. In 1999, a former EA - Bebe Fazia Laljie <https://caselaw.findlaw.com/court/us-2nd-circuit/1461332.html>- also stole $800k from him to pay her credit card bills and was sentenced to 46 months in prison.
Nor was Schmeelk the only banker with an EA who liked handbags. In 2004, Joyti De Laurey <https://www.standard.co.uk/hp/front/freed-early-the-pa-who-stole-ps4-3m-and-broke-prison-rules-6608744.html>was sentenced to seven years in prison after stealing £4.3m from her bosses at Goldman Sachs. She bought handbags, jewellery, watches and a speedboat for her husband, who later said he thought she was earning big bonuses. <https://www.standard.co.uk/hp/front/goldman-wife-a-liar-says-husband-6965535.html>
DeLaurey's ex-Goldman boss - Scott Mead - has since become a fine art photographer. Schmeelk was enjoying his retirement. Ironically, he seems to have been parsimonious during his career. At Salomon, Schmeelk would reportedly <https://financialpost.com/news/fp-street/meet-wall-streets-mr-canada-richard-j-schmeelk> fly from New York to Toronto and back in a day to save on hotel rooms.
If there's a lesson in the repeat infractions, it's that senior bankers probably shouldn't give EAs access to their bank accounts or trust them to pay bills. Bankers work hard, but so do EA's. - DeLaurey's husband said she barely came home when she worked for Goldman. "I was lucky if I saw her for an hour a day. Sometimes I would not even see her for a few days." It's not clear whether she was shopping during this time, though.
Separately, it's great to be Apollo. The private capital group spotted <https://www.ft.com/content/2459bd2e-d0bb-43e8-a5e4-ca6bc9a44d3e?syn-25a6b1a6=1> the danger of investing in First Brands before it crystallised and said in February that it also had the foresight to avoid investing <https://www.ft.com/content/7302e0ba-43a5-4e46-8d23-c98bb7ced025?syn-25a6b1a6=1> in the sorts of software companies that are now threatened by AI.
But even if Apollo had invested in software companies, all might have been ok. A new Substack post <https://substack.com/@shittysituationinvesting> analysing Apollo's investment in a collapsed European real estate firm (Hemingway Real Estate (Lux) SCSp) suggests that even when investments lose money, private capital firms can use accounting mechanisms and multiple entities to mitigate the pain.
Meanwhile...
Andrew Bailey at the UK Financial Stability board thinks the war might have unforseen consequences. “Private credit is a relatively opaque world and it’s not yet, of course, because of its relative newness, actually come under stress, and we may be seeing that now.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-09/fsb-s-bailey-warns-iran-war-is-compounding-private-credit-stress?taid=69d771c161963600015522dc&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Investors attempted to pull more than $20bn from private credit funds in the first quarter. Apollo Global Management, Ares Management, Blackstone, Blue Owl and KKR were all affected. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/3513f9df-18dd-4ea4-ae20-3523988c106c?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Where is Sonny McNess, the ex-JPMorgan trader who joined Mercuria and built such massive aluminium positions that the London Metals Exchange had to change its rules? Not at Mercuria, it seems. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-09/mercuria-trader-who-built-huge-lme-aluminum-bets-has-left?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260409&utm_campaign=author_22490032>
BDO is cutting 31 UK partners so that younger people can come forward instead. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/ccf7209c-e66a-4e1c-8371-6b0701ddcdf6?accessToken=zwAAAZ1zec7WkdPM9yCc5mpOHNODcWsHAd3N9g.MEYCIQDznTW1cYr_5syg618gX000Hmjrlp1g0CZmsoHvVFQyDAIhAMcIUEgoLCNFnUETE9xUeykzInuHBoAUKfCFTCBVJRXa&sharetype=gift&token=d5518aa8-d847-426d-b941-6765406087aa&syn-25a6b1a6=1>
AI cannot be used for investment decisions because it is “absolutely crap at judgment.” Tools are “sycophantic” and agree with users' hypotheses. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/72c20f77-e85d-49cb-84ef-4b676244d1c5?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
What is BlackRock? “BlackRock has historically been viewed as a diversified asset manager that’s really good at making ETFs. But the interesting thing that piqued my attention in the last 12 months was that BlackRock is also one of the best data and software companies in the industry.” (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/why-blackrock-is-weathering-the-private-credit-storm-better-than-peers-2b9d2ca7?mod=djemHeardEUH>
Chris Rokos is up 4.7% this year. Brevan Howard is down 1.09%. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-09/macro-trader-chris-rokos-made-money-in-march-as-peers-got-stung?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260409&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
Bank of America thinks the sports betting market is worth $1.1 trillion. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-09/bofa-sees-a-1-1-trillion-market-for-sports-event-betting-in-us?taid=69d7b3615fcb6f0001a6e7f6&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter&sref=DK3y4h9m>
The White House told staff not to bet on prediction markets. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/politics/policy/white-house-warns-staff-not-to-place-bets-on-prediction-markets-amid-iran-war-3780668f?st=WEkQas>
The Iranians making Lego videos about the war are open to storylines. “We’ve committed ourselves to learning more every day about American people and culture. In this process, Americans themselves have been helping us—and that support and guidance continues. They share impactful tips and ideas with us.” (Wired) <https://www.wired.com/story/inside-the-pro-iran-meme-machine-trolling-trump-with-ai-lego-cartoons/>

item.title: Ex-Credit Suisse banker who escaped before its collapse just left Moelis
item.description:
Getting to a boutique bank </news/boutique-investment-banks> like Moelis & Co is the peak of most M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> bankers’ careers. And getting to a boutique straight after leaving Credit Suisse just a few months before its implosion seems such a great stroke of luck that one is permanently grateful. But some people, apparently, just can’t be satisfied.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Johan Dubbeldeman joined JPMorgan this month, as a managing director (MD) in its investment banking team based in Amsterdam. He joined JP from Moelis, where he spent three and a half years as one of two MDs in its Amsterdam office, covering Benelux clients.
Before Moelis, Dubbeldeman was at Credit Suisse and Rothschild. His times at both were pretty active, being credited with numerous deals across 2021 <https://www.dealmaker.nl/dealmaker/johan-dubbeldeman> especially, including the $2bn acquisition of Intertrust Group by CSC Global.
Dubbeldeman left Credit Suisse in September 2022 after just a year at the firm, and just six months before the firm began to collapse </news/2022/05/credit-suisse-job-cuts>. That collapse – and its subsequent takeover by UBS – led to substantial job cuts </news/2023/03/ubs-credit-suisse-job-cuts> that created piles of refugees from the firm. They’ve ended up some interesting places, from private equity firms </news/2023/06/private-equity-miami> to rival banks </news/2023/02/vivek-nahar-credit-suisse-deutsche-bank> to the public sector </news/2023/10/moving-from-banking-to-government-fund>. Getting to a boutique bank before the collapse was a bit of a coup for Dubbeldeman, in hindsight.

item.title: One of HSBC's most senior women is leaving and her job is being fused with another
item.description:
Wan Sze Loh is leaving HSBC. HSBC is declining to comment, but we understand that Loh has decided to step down and off, and that the bank has rolled her job up into another one.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Loh didn't respond to a request to comment for this article. She joined HSBC from Morgan Stanley in 2017 and became the bank's head of institutional sales for APAC in 2022. One insider at the bank says she was running "everything." - "Credit, equities, rates and FX," sales in the region. She was based in Hong Kong.
With Loh moving on, HSBC already someone else in mind. It is Patrick Boumalham, the bank's global head of wealth sales, who is currently based in Singapore.
Subject to regulatory approvals, Boumalham will soon become head of institutional sales Asia and global head of internal wealth sales. This sounds like a substantial undertaking, but maybe internal sales simply involves touring HSBC offices.*
Boumalham, who is Lebanese, has worked for HSBC since 2005. He was with the bank in Switzerland and London before moving to Singapore in 2022, the same year that Loh was promoted.
*We understand this to involve offering Markets & Securities Services (MSS) products such as structured products to clients via HSBC's Wealth business.

item.title: French bankers and traders have little knowledge of these fine lunch breaks
item.description:
If you're moving to Paris in the hope of participating in the sort of luncheon experience alluded to by ABN AMRO chief executive Marguerite Bérard in her recent interview with the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/2d33bf77-f3c0-47c6-b918-a96b0de0b32c?syn-25a6b1a6=1>, then we have something to share.
These lunches are no longer standard, unless you are a senior executive at BNP Paribas.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bérard was a member of the executive committee at BNP Paribas from 2019 to 2025, when she left to become CEO of ABN AMRO. At BNP Paribas, she seems to have enjoyed some culinary experiences. "French meals can be long," Bérard told the FT. It's different at ABN AMRO, where Bérard now eats lunch 'early and at her desk.' - "This has been one of my adjustments,” she reflected. Sandwiches are Bérard's new norm in weekly meetings with Dutch colleagues.
We asked several senior bankers and traders in Paris whether they were familiar with the Bérard's previous long lunches. Most of them had no idea what we were talking about.
"We grab lunch on a takeaway basis and eat on the desk," said one senior trader at BNP. "We usually just grab a takeaway like in London," said a managing director at a European bank. BNP Paribas didn't respond on whether its executive directors are an exception to the prevailing Anglo Saxon protocol.
One Paris hedge fund manager did claim a difference in French eating habits, however. "In London I don’t think I really ever have lunch outside the office unless meeting with clients," he reflected. "But in Paris, people have lunch around a table either at the canteen or at restaurant outside the office. This is a massive difference."
If you're in Paris and you don't have access to the BNP Paribas executive dining room, where should you go instead? One managing director suggests Jaïs <https://www.le-jais.fr/en/>, a bistro offering sweetbreads in the 7th arrondissement. If you're at JPMorgan, Hôtel Costes <https://www.hotelcostes.com/en> is worth checking out. Relais de l’Entrecôte is also a favourite (and is to be found in London). Failing that, French hedge fund professionals can be found eating in the Rue Marbeuf or at the likes of Loulou, Gigi, or Coco, which are not dog names.

item.title: Ex-Morgan Stanley analyst: "I worked in Hong Kong because I couldn't get a job in New York"
item.description:
Getting an investment banking internship </news/internship-investment-bank> or graduate </news/investment-banks-may-hire-record-graduates-in-2026-getting-a-job-could-be-harder-than-ever> job in New York requires a lot of forward planning. If you're unlucky or just late to the party, you could try for an off-cycle internship or take a chance on opportunities abroad. An ex-Morgan Stanley analyst who did the latter revealed on a podcast <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zbnjbeqWhok> this week that it can be a very bumpy road.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Hannah Zhang worked in Morgan Stanley's Hong Kong </news/hong-kong-bankers-want-to-live-in-singapore> office from 2016 to 2019, first joining as an intern. Speaking to ex-JPMorgan analyst Ben Chon (aka Rareliquid), she said she worked in Asia "because I couldn't get a job in New York... by the time I realized I wanted to go into investment banking, it was already junior year and a lot of the recruiting pipelines </news/why-a-target-school-really-matters-when-applying-to-banking-internships> were filled up." Recruiting for US internships generally starts in the first year of university </news/the-best-time-to-apply-for-a-banking-job> but Europe and APAC recruitment starts a year later, so roles in Hong Kong, Singapore or London would all be available.
Zhang initially joined in sales and trading, but said that the sector "pigeon holes" you more than IB because "you don't have quite as many transferable skills [or] exit opportunities." </news/do-you-still-need-an-investment-banking-graduate-job-to-get-hired-by-blackstone> Chon said that Asia is colloquially known as the "slaughterhouse" for investment banking jobs, which Zhang said "resonates a ton."
Zhang said she kept a list of all the reasons why she wanted to quit. In her worst ever day(s), she said she worked 25 hours overnight to deliver a pitch deck only for the meeting to be pushed to next week the morning after. Zhang said she had to "decipher [her] MD's chicken scratch handwriting in Chinese on a piece of paper" at 3:00am, and turn comments left in WeChat voice notes. Some of this no doubt resonates with New York analysts, but Zhang said that banking jobs in Asia are more hierarchical, with more "unspoken rules and etiquette" as well as the need to have a "bilingual version of everything."
Today, Zhang works in fintech at blockchain infrastructure firm Allium. While she still works a lot of hours, she said that the amount of work was not really the problem: "the work just wasn't impacting anything... that's what got me at the end of the day." Indeed, ex-bankers who leave for fintech frequently tell us their jobs are even more intense post-banking, but they seem happier.
Zhang has been out of banking for a while. In that time jobs got easier, then even more intense. Dealmaking in Hong Kong slumped in the early 2020s, <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-11-21/hong-kong-bankers-have-lots-of-free-time-anxiety-as-deals-slump> as did working hours, but last year it saw a massive increase in activity that has spilled into 2026. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/morgan-stanley-hires-contract-staff-hong-kong-deals-surge-sources-say-2026-03-11/> reported last month that Morgan Stanley made the unusual move of bringing in contract investment banking staff in Hong Kong to help it deal with that workload.

item.title: Jeff Bezos' secretive new AI lab likes hiring from hedge funds and trading firms too
item.description:
The AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> industry has matured a lot in recent years as the major labs expand and new rivals emerge. A recent entrant is the overweeningly named 'Project Prometheus,' a lab quietly launched by Amazon founder Jeff Bezos last year. It recently made headlines for poaching xAI co-founder Kyle Kosic, but has also adopted OpenAI </news/openai-culture>'s habit of looking to financial services for talent.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This week, Andrei Margeloiu announced he was joining Prometheus in London. He spent the last year and a half working on FX quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> research and development </news/quant-developer-jobs-in-banking>for hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Citadel, which he joined after completing a PhD </news/do-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance> from Cambridge. His PhD thesis focused on using neural networks to analyze data.
It appears to be the first direct hire from a financial services firm, but Prometheus has also pursued multiple people that had already left finance for AI. Late last year, it hired San Francisco-based Shiyuan Guo; he spent two and a half years as a FICC/equities quant at Citadel Securities, but left for Claude developer Anthropic </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking>last March. In February it hired London-based Jamie Watson. He'd spent the last seven years at Niantic (the video game development </news/2023/03/video-game-software-developer-hedge-fund-high-frequency-trading>/AR firm behind Pokemon Go) but previously worked as a structured credit trader for Morgan Stanley, whom he joined as a PhD graduate.
There may be more finance alums, but Prometheus employees are extremely secretive. The FT <https://www.ft.com/content/e03c235d-8637-41e5-9e63-a872e398897a?syn-25a6b1a6=1> reported this week that it has hundreds of staff, but less than 80 people on LinkedIn say they're associated with the project.
Other AI labs have been hiring finance alums too in 2026. xAI hired London-based Daniel Pfeifer from electronic trading firm Optiver (which is launching its own AI lab </news/optiver-ai-lab>) last month. In January, it hired New York-based Depei Li who spent the last two years as a quant trader at Jane Street </news/2023/03/video-game-software-developer-hedge-fund-high-frequency-trading>spinoff Five Rings Capital </news/a-quant-trading-firm-paying-806k-in-london-works-from-a-flexible-workspace>. LinkedIn suggests that over 150 people from hedge fund/trading firms have joined Anthropic or OpenAI within the past year alone.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share?
