item.title: Morning Coffee: Deutsche Bank says it wasn't paying enough to attract good people. Charge $2k an hour, work 17 hour days
item.description:
Deutsche Bank does not pay its special people badly. It has increased bonuses for its material risk takers (MRTs) by 60% in two years, </news/deutsche-bank-bonuses>such that last year they each received €1.8m ($2.1m). But pay is a relative issue and while Deutsche Bank's MRTs might be cool with their compensation, the people overseeing them have not been so fresh.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Deutsche Bank explained yesterday that it has increased pay for its supervisory board members because it was being too cheap to attract able people. In a media release on the topic, Deutsche declared <https://www.db.com/news/detail/20260408-deutsche-bank-s-2026-agm-to-be-held-in-person-again-for-the-first-time-since-2019-dividend-again-increased-for-shareholders?language_id=1> that the compensation it had been offering was “no longer competitive in attracting and retaining highly qualified supervisory board members.” It has therefore increased regular pay for supervisory board members by 17% to €350k and pay for its deputy chairman by 16% to €550k.
This is not bad, considering that supervisory board is part-time affair, but Deutsche notes that sitting on its board involves "demanding and multifaceted requirements" in a "complex regulatory environment."
This may not be the last time that Deutsche Bank has to hike pay. Its previous paucity reportedly <https://www.marketscreener.com/news/deutsche-bank-chairman-s-pay-set-to-rise-to-1-4-million-euros-ce7e50dbd98df326> became apparent only when Deutsche Bank tried to find some new people board members, only to find that no one good wanted to do it.
Given the choice, Deutsche Bank would have increased pay even more heftily. Last December it was reportedly making moves <https://www.ft.com/content/ed08c254-fead-4adf-b577-384babf105c3?syn-25a6b1a6=1> to hike compensation for chair Alexander Wynaendts by more than 40%. Yesterday, it revealed that Wynaendts received a mere 21% pay rise, to $1.4m. Wynaendts already earns more than any other chairperson at Germany’s blue-chip Dax companies.
Separately, if you want to work all day and all night while charging thousands of dollars an hour, law is the career for you.
The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/6843726f-2459-462e-b0a1-a33ed10c1c66?syn-25a6b1a6=1> reports that Alberto Safra, son of deceased billionaire banker Joseph Safra, was charged $35m by lawyers working on a dispute involving his dead father's estate.
The $35m bill was reportedly the result of senior lawyers at US firm WilmerHale charging themselves out at up to $2.1k an hour and working 12-17 hour days. On one day alone, they charged $162k. $11k business class flights and hotels were also bundled in.
The London high court has granted Alberto a review of the fees. This should be welcome to other clients of US lawyers. Safra engaged his expensive lawyers in 2022. The going rate has reportedly since increased to $3k an hour.
Meanwhile...
After the tenuous ceasefire, the short squeeze. John Flood, Goldman’s head of Americas equities execution services and partner declared: “This is the offramp the market has been waiting for...“Asset managers and sovereign wealth funds have remained on the sidelines since the start of the war. This likely encourages them to start playing offense again.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-08/hedge-funds-closing-stock-short-bets-at-fastest-pace-since-2020?taid=69d676785d64bf000157578c&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
The Wells Fargo employee who was supposed to receive $175m for blowing the whistle on fake accounts being set up to meet sales targets will now only receive $53m. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/b18fff7e-0797-4dba-bec6-4f8843cffd13?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Nick Rodolakis left Jefferies and joined NatWest as head of funds and sponsor coverage within its corporate and institutional division. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/natwest-hires-jefferies-rodolakis-to-head-up-financial-sponsors-52c6c314?mod=homepage>
Wells Fargo hired Neil Ghosh from Truist as head of chemicals investment banking. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-07/wells-fargo-hires-ghosh-to-lead-chemicals-investment-banking>
Bank of Nova Scotia hired Chris Adams from RBC for utilities banking. (Bloomberg) < https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-31/scotiabank-hires-royal-bank-s-chris-adams-to-lead-utilities-push>
Bank of America is closing its Elma operations centre and 170 people there will lose their jobs. (Yahoo) <https://www.yahoo.com/news/articles/bank-america-close-elma-office-173101279.html?guccounter=1&guce_referrer=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ29vZ2xlLmNvbS8&guce_referrer_sig=AQAAANjjQrmRuZ6eXZw6fdgRyQ7x9SmQOJ1VPFxR0ysAlBZE3Nk82z-KuBxh7lXuzpIdv9eYgADW2ealVF5pmFBtxXaiJrSRiA6seqR_KbVPr1vPlIdBBoOzvZD0-MgvFqoZzI96S-MNT1ZRwQSAKNKb9jh_IV2mfj2QNTS1G8Z3uL46>
A tech company took 120 employees to a tropical offsite in Honduras and it resulted in intestinal issues, a rodent like porcupine in a shower and crawling along the beach in 100 degrees. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/workplace/corporate-retreat-gone-wrong-07754741?mod=djemCareers>

item.title: "At my bank, you have to drink with the boss to matter at all"
item.description:
I work at a major French bank in Hong Kong. But I am not French, and it is absolutely killing my career being here. There is very much a cool kids’ club, and I’m not part of it. I will never be part of it.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
My boss’ inner circle are basically his drinking buddies. They not only go out together in the evening, but also cluster together at every single corporate event – tucked away in a corner, ranks closed around the boss. They make him physically inaccessible.
No matter how good you are at your job, you will struggle to get face time unless you’re in that inner circle. They completely monopolise his time and attention.
A lot of informal conversations happen after hours. That’s where relationships are built, especially in Kong Hong. But the French drinking culture – socializing and bonding late into the night – push out people who are not familiar or comfortable with it. And yes, some people don’t drink, and others have families or different lifestyles. Others aren’t French, for Christ’s sake.
Of course, no one says you must drink. But you really do. Because, if you don’t, you miss the moments where decisions, conversations, and connections happen.
You need to learn to adapt. You need to learn to build connections with other teams. You look for mentors there. But it’s not fair. Sometimes you just want a normal conversation with your boss during the day. Not at midnight over a bottle of wine.
Performance should always speak for itself. But in reality, sometimes it feels like the bar matters more than the office.
Sebastian Wu is a pseudonym.

item.title: Citi paid Vis Raghavan $25m to hire from JPMorgan. JPMorgan paid Doug Petno $23m to hire from Citi
item.description:
Doug Petno is a funny guy <https://ERFORMANCE SUMMARY: Assessment of results against long-term strategic priorities and qualitative considerations 2025 Business Results & Progress Against our Strategic Framework • CIB achieved net income of $27.8 billion on revenue1 of $78.5 billion, with an ROE of 18% • IB fees of $9.7 billion, up 7%; Payments revenue of $19.3 billion, up 7%; Fixed Income revenue of $22.5 billion, up 12%; Equities revenue of $13.3 billion, up 33%; and Securities Services revenue of $5.6 billion, up 10% • Ranked #1 in global IB fees for 17 consecutive years with wallet share of 8.4%2 in 2025 • Participated in 5 of the top 10 fee paying deals3 • Ranked #1 in Markets revenue with 11.8%4 wallet share (#1 in Fixed Income; Co-#1 in Equities) • Record revenue across Markets, Payments, and Securities Services • Continued to advance CIB's industry-leading positions and focus on integration, collaboration and modernization across the business to provide clients with high quality products and services, and deliver strong performance Client/Customer/Stakeholder • Continued focus on and investment in technologies, efficiency and modernization, implementing optimized client-focused solutions to meet their needs and gain market share globally, in addition to driving AI product maturity and adoption • Maintained a commitment to our institutional, corporate, and sponsor clients across all regions and products through the participation in numerous client events throughout the year • Ranked #1 for overall institutional client service quality for the 7th consecutive year in Global Markets, per Coalition Greenwich Institutional Voice of Client 2025 Markets Study • Ranked #1 Global Research, #1 Global Fixed Income Research and #1 Global Equity Research by Extel for the 4th time in 5 years • Awarded Best Global Corporate Bank consistently, per Coalition Greenwich Institutional Voice of Client 2025 Global Corporate Banking Study Risk, Controls & Conduct • Maintained a satisfactory risk and controls environment, with continued focus on the anticipation of risks and proactive reviews of operational resilience • Demonstrated focus on driving accountability and the highest standards of culture and conduct consistent with the Firm’s Business Principles • Drove significant progress on regulatory matters specific to the CIB, while continuing to lead the integration of controls and governance across heritage Corporate & Investment Bank and Commercial Banking Teamwork & Leadership • Drove succession planning, retention and development of top talent through key appointments, focused training, and hiring efforts • Prioritized strengthening connectivity and a culture of collaboration across an integrated CIB, driving accountability and delivery on key strategies to align client experience and enhance productivity • Supported the Firm’s culture, connecting with and engaging broadly across the CIB organization to provide leadership and communicate strategic priorities>. But maybe he will lose some zing when he apprehends that Vis Raghavan, </news/vis-raghavan-pay-citi>with whom he previously worked at JPMorgan, is now earning more than him.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Petno and Raghavan's pay for 2025 was disclosed in Citi <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/Annual_Report/2025/2026-citi-proxy-statement.pdf>and JPMorgan's <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/content/dam/jpmc/jpmorgan-chase-and-co/investor-relations/documents/proxy-statement2026.pdf>recently released proxy statements. Petno was paid $23m. Raghavan was paid $25m.
Ironically, both men seem to have been paid in part for their success at pinching each other's staff.
Raghavan's Citi report card for 2025 notes that he "successfully recruited key talent" alongside many other achievements, such as "driving global resource delivery across nearly 100 markets," making "exemplary contributions" as a "change agent," and rendering himself, "instrumental in gaining market share in key areas like M&A and debt capital markets."
Petno's JPMorgan report card for 2025 notes that he too was busy hiring. As well as retaining existing staff, Petno is praised for his "development of top talent through key appointments," and his 'focused hiring efforts.' Petno additionally displayed prowess in areas like "driving AI product maturity and adoption," and foreseeing risks.
Anyone who still remembers 2025 will recall that it was a year in which Citi and JPMorgan exchanged many senior people. Vis Raghavan hired well over ten former colleagues from JPMorgan </news/vis-raghavan-hire-from-jpmorgan-citi> into key Citi roles and claimed to be "cheap" </news/vis-raghavan-hire-from-jpmorgan-citi> in the process. JPMorgan hired people like star sponsors banker Anthony Diamandakis </news/anthony-diamandakis> and Suneel Hargunani from Citi </news/jp-morgan-s-latest-big-citi-hire-might-be-joined-by-more-after-bonuses>as they moved the other way following Ragahavan's arrival.
Swapping talent clearly wasn't the only element in each man's pay package. But it seems to have helped. Petno and Raghavan may want to work more closely on this in 2026.

item.title: Globe-trotting banking technologist appears to have settled down, in London
item.description:
What’s the best place for a career on the technology side </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank> of banking? Is it in a major financial hub like London or New York? Is it in an off-piste but still mature market like Japan? Or is it in an offshoring hub like Poland? Well… Why not all?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Jamie Pizzorno joined Deutsche Bank in London last week, as a managing director and head of data management and transformation. He joined Deutsche from Standard Chartered, where he was head of cybersecurity change, based in Warsaw.
Pizzorno has been on quite a journey throughout his career. From an internship during his masters at Goldman Sachs in Tokyo, he started his career in New York at NTT DOCOMO, a Japanese telecommunications firm. He then spent 18 years at Goldman, across Japan, New York, and London, where he was before leaving for Singapore for Standard Chartered </news/2022/04/jamie-pizzorno-goldman-sachs-to-standard-chartered>, before later leaving for Warsaw.
Pizzorno “writes” many of his LinkedIn posts with emojis and “it’s not X, it’s Y” sentence structures. Some of his posts are even rallying against the ethics of AI. Pizzorno is a significant advocate of diversity causes, especially LGBT ones. He was a shortlisted Inspirational Role Model of the Year at the European Diversity Awards <https://www.europeandiversityawards.com/shortlist-2024> and once claimed to suffer from Long Covid.

item.title: Citadel's most senior equities recruiter in London left, but it's ok
item.description:
If you work in Citadel's London equities team, you will know Drew Gillanders. </news/citadel-hedge-fund-jobs-london>You will also know Eleanor Sharkey. Sharkey, who has been Citadel's head of business development for international equities since 2022, probably hired you. She will not be hiring anyone else to Citadel's equities team. She has quit.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citadel isn't commenting on Sharkey's whereabouts and nor is Sharkey herself. It's thought that she might soon reappear at a rival fund. Sharkey has been replaced by Freya Maynard, a former Morgan Stanley prime brokerage associate who joined Citadel in 2023.
As we have reported previously </news/as-hedge-funds-churn-talent-their-super-recruiters-are-more-important-than-ever>, business developers in hedge funds are a big deal. They are not standard recruiters, but recruiters charged with managing the flow of portfolio managers who deploy a fund's capital. They must therefore be cognisant both of the portfolio manager talent available the market and of the firm's appetite to deploy capital to different strategies.
Sharkey is not the only recent departure from Citadel's business developer rack. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/citadel-recruiting-exec-laura-sterner-resigns-leaving-company-2025-11> reported in November that Laura Sterner also left as head of business development in the global equities business. Sterner only worked at the fund for two months and joined to replace Alex Topkins, the head of business development for global equities, who left in April and reappeared at Soros.
Citadel has also made business development hires. Five new external recruits joined in US fundamental equities in the past two months. Sapna Vir joins from Verition as head of business development for global credit and convertibles, starting in December.
It's post bonus season in hedge funds. Coupled with a difficult March, this is expected to result in high levels of portfolio manager exits, making business developers across the market more important than ever.
People who left Citadel in 2024 and are coming off non-competes are surfacing elsewhere. Burak Guner, a quant partner has gone to Millennium. Christopher Panek, the head of volatility at Citadel global quantitative strategies, is trying his luck at Two Sigma.

item.title: Electronic trading giant Optiver is building out a new AI Lab
item.description:
Over the past few years, AI labs </news/the-difference-between-working-as-a-quant-in-finance-and-as-a-researcher-in-an-ai-lab> like OpenAI </news/openai-culture> and Anthropic have been unabashed in their pursuit of top young talent from electronic trading and HFT firms. </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> Optiver, an Amsterdam/Chicago based trading giant, has decided two can play at that game.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Earlier in the year, it launched an AI </news/financial-services-is-using-ai-more-than-other-industries-but-seeing-a-fraction-of-the-benefits> Lab in Shanghai. It's not clear how large the team is right now, nor when exactly it was founded, but one of its members is Zhou Fang, who joined in February of this year. He's a PhD </news/do-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance> mathematician who had worked as a deep learning quant for rival trading firm IMC in Chicago for five months after graduating. He describes himself as a founding member of the lab but is understood not to be in charge.
The Shanghai lab has job openings for both a machine learning </news/trading-jobs-in-finance-should-you-study-machine-learning-or-economics> engineer and an AI researcher; listings call the AI Lab a "newly established team [which] will operate independently from our core trading operations" to apply "advanced AI and machine learning technologies to complex problems." Optiver is looking for people with "startup mindset but Big Tech rigor."
There's precedent for spinning an AI lab out of a trading firm in China. Deepseek </news/liang-wenfeng>was founded by Liang Wenfeng, founder of Chinese quant fund High-Flyer </news/2023/03/chinese-quant-funds>, who used the fund's profits to finance his AI research. Optiver should have plenty of money to fund the lab after its primary business saw a lot of success in 2025; its annual review <https://optiver.com/optiver-reports-robust-financial-results-for-2025/> published last week said that net trading revenue was up by over €1bn ($1.17bn).
Seemingly happy with the results so far, Optiver is building out a team for its AI lab in the US. This week, Andrew Arnold announced he was joining the firm as its head of research for the AI lab in New York. He's an adjunct machine learning professor at NYU and was most recently a principal applied machine learning engineer for eCommerce firm Shopify.
Arnold was also previously a machine learning researcher at Google. He was also previously a portfolio manager at Cubist, the systematic arm of hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Point72, and was the chief technology officer of hedge fund Trexquant.
Optiver did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Almost everyone got a 2025 bonus at Stripe in London. Almost no one got a bonus at Starling Bank
item.description:
Many people who join fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech> do so in the hope of earning stock </news/joining-a-fintech-startup-for-the-stock-pay-is-a-dead-end> in their firm that will balloon in value as it grows. If you want to earn that stock pay, or indeed any other kind of bonus, you'll have to choose your fintech carefully.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The UK's government mandated gender pay gap disclosure filings require fintechs </news/best-place-to-work-fintech> to break down the gender-split of their organization, and say how many men and women each received bonuses last year (in either stock or cash). From that, we can extrapolate how many people across the organization received bonuses that year.
Starling Bank </news/starling-bank-fca-fine> is a glaringly poor performer: just 6.9% of employees received a 2025/26 bonus. It's down from ~8.5% in 2024/25, but there have been even worse years for the bank; in its 2023/24 report, the first filing after the departure of founder Anne Boden, <https://www.bankingdive.com/news/starling-ceo-anne-boden-exit-investor-fallout-report-neobank/652918/> just 2% of staff received any sort of bonus.
One of the best places to work for bonuses is Atom Bank, which has given every employee a bonus for the last three years. In its most recent accounts (for the year ending March 2025) it paid an average of £52.4k ($70.4k) in 'salaries and wages'. That's on the lower end as far as UK fintechs go but it makes up for that by also offering remote working and a four-day week. </news/2022/11/uk-british-fintech-good-workplace>
The other best option, which has a reputation for much higher pay, is payments giant Stripe. </news/stripe-high-performance-engineers> It regularly reports that over 90% of staff earn bonuses and, last year, 96% of people were given one. While we don't have accounts for 2025 from Stripe yet, previous accounts have separated salaries from other compensation; in 2024, 96% of UK staff (~277 people) shared £26.6m in bonuses, meaning they earned roughly £96k per head.
Revolut </news/revolut-the-fintech-hiring-0-15-of-applicants-raised-pay> offers bonuses to the majority of its employees, but men earn an egregious amount more than women. Its gender pay disclosure revealed that the mean bonus for women was over 85% lower than the mean bonus for men. </news/gender-pay-gap-banking-finance#:~:text=The%20Gender%20Pay%20Gap%20in%20Fintech>

item.title: Citi's global head of equities quants left in NY and now it would like a new one in London
item.description:
How would you feel about becoming the global head of equities quant analysis at Citi? If you would feel very good about it, you may like to know that Citi is hiring. The bank has begun advertising the job </jobs-UK-London-Global_Head_of_Equities_Quantitative_Analysis.id24073698> here in London.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citi isn't commenting but insiders observe that the job being advertised seems very similar to the job only recently vacated </news/one-of-citi-s-top-equities-quants-resigned-maybe-her-team-will-follow> by Anissa Dhouibi in New York. Dhouibi, who spent six years at Citi after joining from JPMorgan, resigned last month. Her whereabouts are unknown and she is not responding to enquiries. Hopefully she is on a beach.
Dhouibi was popular at Citi and her replacement has large shoes to fill. Citi says it would like a "visionary" managing director with a PhD or Masters and 15 years' experience to lead its team.
It's not clear whether Dhouibi's former colleagues in New York might be inclined to move to London for the role. Alternative, the bank may decide it has an internal candidate in the form of Holmi Atig, the London-based global head of equities prime and delta one quant analysis.

item.title: A complete guide to the gender pay gap in banking and finance
item.description:
Financial services has had a decades-long </news/2007/07/asset-managers-struggling-to-shake-off-macho-image>(well-earned) reputation as a hypermasculine industry. As it looks to bring in top young talent, </news/internship-investment-bank>it has marketed itself as open to anyone, including women </news/women-in-finance>. That doesn't mean high earning females are abundant...
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
We've looked at government mandated gender pay gap disclosures in the UK for 40 different major financial services firms across investment banking, </news/2023/05/whats-an-investment-bank> private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity>, fintech, </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech> electronic trading </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> and hedge funds. </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Nobody's perfect but some sectors, and some firms in particular, really outperform the rest.
The Gender Pay Gap in Investment Banking
On average, women in British banking jobs earn £0.73 for every £1 a man earns in salary but just £0.53 per pound a man earns via bonus. </news/everything-we-know-about-2026-bonuses-by-bank>
Some of the most generous employers for women are UBS and Barclays. The former has the highest mean hourly pay ratio (amount earned by women in salary per £1 earned by men), as well as the highest bonus ratio. Barclays, meanwhile, has the largest number of women in the top 25% of earners of any company analyzed both in and outside of banking. The most generous US bank appears to be Bank of America; its mean bonus ratio (amount earned by women in bonus per £1 earned by men), is almost double that of American rivals Citi and Goldman Sachs.
Indeed Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan, which can be viewed as the two most prestigious banks on the list, are two of the worst performing </news/gender-pay-gap-investment-banking-goldman-sachs-jpmorgan> for several metrics. The average woman at each of the two banks earns roughly 60% of what the average man earns. JPMorgan appears to pay higher bonuses to women than Goldman, but the latter bank has more women in its top quartile of earners.
One of the most peculiar cases is boutique </news/boutique-investment-banks>investment bank Lazard. There, average salary is neither great nor terrible, but the median bonus for women is more than 80% lower than the median bonus for men. While that might appear to be a bad thing, the fact that more than 70% of people in its lowest paying quartile are women might imply the bank has actually ramped up hiring for female analysts and associates. Mean bonuses at Lazard fare much better for women, suggesting there are some female high earners in its ranks.
The Gender Pay Gap in Private Equity
On average, women in private equity earn £0.60 for every £1 a man earns in salary and a measly £0.48 per pound a man earns via bonus.
Blackstone </news/do-you-still-need-an-investment-banking-graduate-job-to-get-hired-by-blackstone> has made a huge jump in closing its gender pay gap... for salaries at least. While the firm historically paid £0.70-£0.80 for each £1 a man earned according to prior filings, the gap this year closed to 97p. Bonuses, which presumably includes the coveted carried interest that brings people to PE in the first place, saw much more disparity; women at Blackstone have been stuck earning £0.50-£0.60 for each £1 a man earns in bonuses for each of the last few years.
European PE firms aren't nearly as generous, it seems. The lowest mean salary and bonus are both at CVC Capital Partners, and HG Capital pays just marginally better in both regards.
Private equity also appears to be pushing much harder than banking to hire junior women. More than 70% of the bottom quartile of earners in each of the five firms are women, with Apollo seeming to have the most female juniors. This is reflected in the notably low median bonus ratio of just £0.20
The Gender Pay Gap in Fintech
On average, women in fintech earn £0.82 for every £1 a man earns in salary but just £0.59 per pound a man earns via bonus.
Fintech, which you might think is more progressive, is a sector with a lot of variation, particularly in regard to bonuses. At Starling Bank, for example, bonuses were almost equal between men and women (median bonuses were identical), but at Revolut </news/revolut-the-fintech-hiring-0-15-of-applicants-raised-pay>, the mean bonus for women was 86% lower than the bonus for men. Revolut's median bonus ratio is £0.67, which implies that there are plenty of women earning decent bonuses, but a few men at the top earning astronomical amounts.
More than any other sector analyzed, women are most likely to be in the top quartile of earners at a fintech. They occupy 27% of that quartile on average, with Monzo having the most female high-earners.
The Gender Pay Gap in Hedge Funds and Trading Firms
On average, women in hedge funds and electronic trading firms earn £0.77 for every £1 a man earns in salary but just £0.47 per pound a man earns via bonus.
More so than any other sector of finance, hedge funds and trading firms have a severe lack of high earning women. The top paying quartile of the average firm is made up of just 13.6% women, with many firms having less than 8% in their top quartile. An egregious example is Jump Trading, where just 1.4% of its top quartile are women.
The most generous firm for women isn't a hedge fund: it's secretive London-based trading firm G-Research </news/g-research-pay>. There, women earn £0.91 for every £1 a man earns in salary and £0.70 for every £1 a man earns in bonus. Women also make up 35.8% of its top paying quartile; the next best performer is Brevan Howard where 23.5% of its highest earners are women.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Bankers nervously trying to keep their roles in the biggest deal so far. Goodbye once more to big bonuses in London
item.description:
Equity capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm>bankers are famously some of the most highly strung people in the industry, and they have good reason to be. The fees on initial public offerings are often huge, but the workload is intense and the scope for making mistakes is very small indeed. And although an IPO might be just one more deal for the bankers, it is one of the most important moments in a company’s history. So the clients tend to be quite demanding. If the client is Elon Musk – not the easiest person to deal with at the best of times, the stress must be off the charts. <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/blockbuster-spacex-listing-could-suck-oxygen-out-fragile-ipo-market-2026-04-07/>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The SpaceX IPO <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/spacex-lays-out-ipo-details-targets-early-june-roadshow-sources-say-2026-04-07/>, for which the prospectus is expected to be filed next month, is the biggest in history – aiming to raise $75bn at a valuation of $2trn. That’s a lot of money even by the standards of the US equity market, and it seems that SpaceX might be sucking the oxygen out of the room; overall IPOs are down 37.5% for the year to date, partly as investors and issuers wait for this deal to get out of the way.
So it’s incredibly important to the bankers to be in the deal, and the syndicate is pretty huge, with 21 banks involved <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/01/spacex-lines-up-21-banks-for-mega-ipo-code-named-project-apex.html>. In order to stay in Elon’s good books, they have apparently had to agree to subscribe to Grok AI <https://www.ft.com/content/a8e02654-b29a-4e6a-b2f8-f458b5dbfe5c>. (As well as a defence contractor and satellite network, SpaceX is an artificial intelligence company and owns the social network which used to be called Twitter. Good luck to the analysts trying to come up with a sum-of-the-parts valuation).
And the inconvenience doesn’t stop there. As well as trying to get along with an irreverent chatbot, the bankers need to keep track of a retail allocation <https://www.theguardian.com/science/2026/apr/07/spacex-2tn-valuation-retail-investor-ipo-elon-musk> which is going to be about three times the size of a normal IPO. Reports suggest that institutional investors will need to be persuaded to agree to a significantly longer <https://www.wsj.com/finance/spacex-ipo-elon-musk-prepare-1367846a> “lock up” period than normal, and the marketing period will involve everyone having to make trips to Austin to meet management and see the factories rather than a conventional roadshow.
This is all going to be pretty exhausting. The way that IPO bankers manage their stress is to keep everything working along standard timelines and processes. Any change to the execution plan means a lot of work for everyone, to make sure that no detail is missed which might cause problems later. A client who is determined to keep ordering off-menu is a nightmare. And this is only the beginning; there haven’t been any in-jokes, tantrums or funny numbers <https://observer.com/2024/04/elon-musk-number-420-tesla-twitter/> yet.
It all feels like an even more difficult version of the Aramco </news/2019/11/bankers-saudi-aramco-deal> IPO, which bruised a number of banker egos on the way to the market. We shouldn’t feel too sorry for capital markets bankers – they are very well paid. But sometimes, they really do have to work for their money.
Elsewhere, London bankers might have celebrated a bit when the cap on bonuses </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> was removed a couple of years ago. But in order to really benefit from it, you’ve got to actually make money for your employer. And it seems that might be a bit more difficult than anticipated <https://www.ft.com/content/728bf79f-b6c8-47b2-b97e-2ba0074a4744?syn-25a6b1a6=1>.
Under new European regulations, it is going to be significantly more difficult for branches of non-EU banks to provide core services to European clients, and non-EU institutions are also likely to be shut out of all the deals taking part under the “Safe” defence spending initiative.
All of which means that, despite some signs of a return to profitability in the British domestic market, global firms are likely to maintain their trend of increasing the size and staffing of their Paris and other EU offices, rather than trying to run things out of London. So ambitious global bankers will still be thinking about role-based allowances and higher base salaries, rather than trying to max out the variable compensation.
Meanwhile …
“Down 5%” is the new “killing it” for global macro funds, with a number of big names like Brevan Howard, Taula and Jupiter doing somewhat worse than that in March. The only players that have been able to cope with the chaotic environment of war and rumours seem to have been the tightly managed quant funds, with DE Shaw and Capital both up last month. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-07/macro-traders-slump-most-in-march-as-war-squeezes-hedge-funds?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260407&utm_campaign=author_22884492>)
Although there are plenty of organisations which work in teams, it always seems to be the Navy SEALs that CEOs use as the example, even though the SEALs hardly ever write powerpoint slides. Jamie Dimon is the latest, in his letter to investors where he says that the secret of competitive success is to delegate jobs to small groups of highly motivated people. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/jamie-dimon-small-navy-seal-teams-shareholder-letter-2026-4>)
If you have had as much fun as you think the UAE can provide, and if you are properly rich (rather than just “influencer rich”), the latest destination is apparently Milan. It’s not a cheap place to live, but the Italian tax regime is quite favourable. (Guardian <https://www.theguardian.com/business/2026/apr/07/milan-dubai-super-wealthy-italy-rich>)
“As our private bank’s performance has strengthened, expectations for colleagues have risen accordingly”. Citi’s wealth managers are reminded that the reward for a job well done is sometimes that last year’s superperformance becomes this year’s baseline expectation. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/citis-wealth-division-ups-performance-targets-64b504ae?mod=topic_news>)
If you have been really good at guessing reality TV results and predicting Donald Trump’s tweets, you now have a problem. Nobody in the USA seems to know whether prediction market profits should be recorded on your tax return as short term derivatives trading, gambling winnings or something else. (WIRED <https://www.wired.com/story/nobody-knows-how-to-file-taxes-on-prediction-market-wins-and-losses/>)
Unfortunately, one of the worst jobs in accountancy is also the least likely to be replaced by AI. Part of auditing a company is checking its inventory levels. That’s bad enough when you have to count thousands of nuts and bolts, but if the company has inventories of frozen fish, or pigs it can become uncomfortable or disgusting. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/accountants-audit-inventory-tax-day-59b1b71f?mod=mhp>)

item.title: Steven Hunter left JPMorgan aged 23. Now he's 34 and his company is worth $1.3bn
item.description:
It's been a while since we spoke to ex-JPMorgan analyst Steven Hunter at 9Fin. </news/2020/05/ex-jpmorgan-analyst-still-hiring-for-stealthy-fintech>The intervening years have gone very, very well for him.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
In the midst of the pandemic six years ago, Hunter told us that he felt thankful for having raised seed funding for his London-based fintech before the COVID-time began. 9Fin then employed 12 people and was hiring, but not wildly. "We had some really aggressive expansion plans but we decided to take a breather and see where the world ends up," Hunter told us in May 2020.
That hiring breather didn't last and although the world has seemingly not ended up in a great place <https://www.ft.com/content/e49f0110-f07b-4480-9ea4-2b37aab2c906?syn-25a6b1a6=1>, 9Fin is doing fine. The company now employs 400 people. Last week, it announced <https://9fin.com/insights/9fin-raises-170m-to-lead-ai-transformation-of-debt-markets?utm_source=personal&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=q126_global_seriesc> the completion of a $170m series C fundraising round at a valuation of $1.3bn.
War aside, 9Fin is helpfully positioned. It's at the nexus of current obsessions. It's about credit market valuations; it's about AI. "9fin is the intelligence layer for debt markets — combining proprietary data, real-time analysis, and AI-driven workflows into a single platform," goes the tagline.
Hunter's 400 employees work across engineering, AI, data, editorial, and debt markets. Along with Hunter himself, those who've been there from the early days are presumably on track to become very wealthy. Accounts filed with Companies House in the UK show that 9Fin operates a share option scheme for employees with a four year vesting programme.
Hunter spent two years at JPMorgan in London after graduating with a first class degree in law from the University of Bristol in 2013. He spent another year at Babson Capital Management working on high yield investments. Then he founded 9Fin in 2016.
If you're a JPMorgan analyst with a business idea, Hunter cautions that entrepreneurship is very different to banking. "The same playbook doesn’t translate directly."
"In banking, there’s a well-defined process, whether it’s M&A, debt, or equity. These are transactions that have been executed many times before, with clear steps and resources. If you want to move faster, you can typically add more people or capital," he says.
By comparison, Hunter says running a business involves uncertainty, urgency, and operating "without a clear playbook." He brushes aside comparisons with Revolut's Nik Storonksy, who once worked as a trader at Credit Suisse, and whose net worth is now over $10bn.
Storonsky has been described as "obsessed with details and data" by one employee at Revolut. <https://www.reuters.com/markets/europe/revoluts-storonsky-takes-big-step-forward-battle-with-banks-2024-08-22/#:~:text=When%20in%20London%2C%20he%20works,underperforming%20departments%2C%20the%20person%20said.> Hunter seems different. He likes high trust and self-sufficiency. "I aim to give our executive team a high degree of autonomy and ownership. Wherever possible, I set clear, high-level objectives and then let the team execute against them."
If you want to join Hunter at 9Fin, he will be hiring. Headcount will be increased "meaningfully" he tells us. "The focus will primarily be on AI, product, and engineering as we continue to invest in the core platform. We also expect to expand our go-to-market teams, particularly in the US, where we’re seeing the fastest growth.”
What are the habits that have got him this far? Hunter declined to divulge an elite morning routine but says he's "very deliberate" in how he spends his time. "I use two simple filters. First, does this impact the company’s top three priorities? Second, am I uniquely suited to do it compared to anyone else on the team? If the answer to either is no, I probably shouldn’t be doing it."

item.title: Bankers at UBS & beyond express sorrow at death of the funniest man in the markets
item.description:
Richard Kersley </news/the-tragic-tale-of-a-top-equity-researcher-spending-20k-a-month-to-stay-alive>, the former Credit Suisse head of product research management, who was diagnosed with a brain tumour three years ago, has died.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Kersley's wife, Nix Kersley, wrote on LinkedIn that he died peacefully at home on Saturday evening.
As we reported last year, Richard Kersley had been spending £28k a month to fight his cancer. He opted to retire instead of joining UBS when Credit Suisse collapsed, but then blacked out at Goodwood a year into his retirement. Kersley was subsequently diagnosed with Glioblastoma Multiforma (GBM), a brain tumour that kills most sufferers within a year. He opted for treatment with a device called Optune Gio, which increases survival rates using "Tumor Treating Fields" emitted by patches placed on the head. But the device, plus immunotherapy drugs, weren't covered by Kersley's health insurer, AXA.
"Richard has not had a single operation or ailment in his life," said his wife last year. "He's never had to claim for an operation through AXA and the rejection of this claim seems ridiculous. You expect to get the best option on the market with health insurance, but it's just standard care."
Kersley spent 37 years at Credit Suisse. Former colleagues, who are now working at UBS and elsewhere, described him as "full of humour", "very funny" and one of the "smartest, kindest and funniest people in the market."
"The world is a less glorious place without him," said Matt Hall, vice chairman of UK investment banking at UBS. Speaking off the record, another former colleague says Kersley was always the person to go to when "other managers had done something crappy."
Kersley, who was in his early 60s, had intended to spend time with his family after retiring. "It was supposed to be family time," his wife told us last year. "When Richard was working, he was up at 5.30am and dealing with calls in the evenings and at weekends."
Writing at the weekend to announce Kersley's death, she said: "Don’t waste a moment of time as it is so damn precious and can be taken away in a heartbeat. No one imagines a life where a 5minute walk to the local coffee stop is so utterly beyond your reality. Don’t take anything for granted."

item.title: Morgan Stanley's new fintech banking head is from Banking's most nomadic team
item.description:
When Silicon Valley Bank </news/2023/03/where-do-silicon-valley-bank-employees-go> collapsed in 2023, its top teams were pounced upon by various banks. HSBC bought its UK division for £1 </news/2023/03/svb-to-hsbc>and turned it into HSBC Innovation Banking, while boutique </news/boutique-investment-banks>Moelis & Co took a small cabal of technology investment bankers. One of the MDs in the latter team has just left to take a big new role at Morgan Stanley.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Georgi Balinov joined Morgan Stanley as its global co-head of fintech investment banking, based in New York. He spent the last three years at Moelis, and a year before that at SVB. He's one of three global co-heads alongside Jigar Patel and Kaivan Shakib.
At Moelis, Balinov's team was led by Jason Auerbach, former global co-head of TMT banking at UBS. It was an unusual group in that Auerbach was consistently followed by those loyal to him. Many of the team at SVB were from UBS and the first seven bankers hired by Moelis had worked with Auerbach across both firms. That kind of nomadic movement between firms is more common among pod shop hedge funds </news/2023/04/team-moves-ma> than investment banking teams.
Balinov, who had never worked at UBS, was an exception. He was previously an MD at both Merrill Lynch and, latterly, Barclays. He served as the British bank's head of global technology payments banking for two years.
Morgan Stanley may be reorganizing its fintech ranks in anticipation of a bumper year for fintech. IPO candidates include Monzo, which laid off its entire US operation <https://techfundingnews.com/monzo-shuts-down-us-operations-europe-ipo/> last week in order to prepare for a European public launch.

item.title: JPMorgan’s footprint in Texas has grown, and it will grow still
item.description:
As part of JPMorgan’s 2025 annual report <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir/annual-report>, which was published yesterday, the firm published a letter to shareholders courtesy of longtime CEO Jamie Dimon. The letter is very, very long, some 50 pages in total. Dimon, who has led the firm since 2006, had a lot to say – including about where JPMorgan’s 320,000-strong workforce will be migrating to in the next few years.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
“Sometimes you see companies leaving states, but migration also shows up in shifts of employees out of certain states,” Dimon explained. Although New York City (NYC) is still JPMorgan’s “global headquarters, we have shrunk our headcount in the city… And increased our headcount in Texas.”
Worrinyngly for fans of NYC, this is a trend that will “likely continue”. JPMorgan’s footprint in NYC decreased from 30,000 a decade ago to 24,000 today, according to Dimon. Its footprint in Texas, meanwhile, increased from 26,000 to 32,000 in the same time period.
Dimon blamed a variety of factors on the switch. “New York City has much going for it, particularly for financial companies,” Dimon said, but “it also has the highest city and state corporate taxes and the highest individual income and state taxes” in the United States. “higher taxes mean lower returns on capital and less competitiveness by their nature.” As such, the firm was voting with its feet.
There are plenty of well-paying jobs in Texas, despite the implications of what Dimon said. The firm employed a statistically robust 2,762 people on H1b visas in the United States, with a median salary of $156k. The 490 based in New York had a median salary of $175k, and the 641 based in Plano, Texas had a median salary of $150k. Not as great, but still pretty great.
Still, it’s unlikely that the bank will abandon New York City completely any time soon. It just opened <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/newsroom/stories/jpmc-celebrates-new-global-hq-at-270-park-ave> a brand new headquarters in the city, which Dimon called “a beautiful physical manifestation” of JPMorgan. It’s built on the same space as its old headquarters, and can house up to 10,000 employees. It shows “how you can deconstruct something and rebuild it in a powerful way,” Dimon said. Whatever that means.
JPMorgan has a huge office in Plano, built on a former horse farm, but its biggest Texan presence is in Dallas Fort Worth. The firm spent some $14bn on technology last year, and invested over $40m in Texas specifically from 2019 to 2024 on education, workforce, scholarships, and mentorships, per a What Works report <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/content/dam/jpmorganchase/documents/newsroom/expanding-high-quality.pdf> from the firm it published in May 2025.
Texas is popular with other banks, too. Goldman Sachs also has a huge footprint in Dallas – it’s building a $500m campus in the city, with a plan to increase its headcount there from 4,600 to 5,000 by 2028. It also has another well-known presence in Salt Lake City </news/finance/would-you-move-to-salt-lake-city-for-a-job-at-goldman-sachs>, where it has another 3,000 employees. What both cities have in common is that they are much, much cheaper than NYC.

item.title: Pay at XTX Markets rose modestly for most employees despite massive revenue boost.
item.description:
London based electronic market maker </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay#:~:text=Electronic%20Market%20Making,hedge%20their%20risk.>XTX had a fine 2025. Revenues across its three primary entities rose by roughly a third <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-02/gerko-s-xtx-markets-earnings-rise-33-on-global-trading-surge?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260402&utm_campaign=author_19908303> to a total of £3.93bn ($5.21bn). Pay for staff also rose, but not by nearly as much.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Of three entities that released accounts earlier this month, only 'XTX Markets Technologies Limited' released headcount and compensation figures. The entity, which defines its principal activity as protecting and enhancing XTX Markets' intellectual property, increased headcount from 113 in 2024 to 127 in 2025.
Overall spending on 'wages and salaries' was up ~18% last year but, due to the increased headcount, the rise in compensation per head was more marginal. XTX paid staff £457k ($606k) in 2025, up ~5% from the £436k per head it paid in 2024.
These figures do not include XTX's limited partnership entity, 'XTX Research LLP,' which releases accounts months after its other entities. In 2024, that entity paid 30 partners an average of almost £20m </news/lucrative-electronic-market-maker-xtx-pays-19-9m-per-head-to-its-30-partners>. That entity also had 19 employees who were paid £555k in 'salaries and wages'. Perhaps they benefitted more from the firm's success last year than XTX Technologies' employees.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: WhatsApp: http://wa.me/442079977910 <http://wa.me/442079977910> (+44 20 7997 7910), Telegram: @AlexMcMurray, Signal: @AlexMcMurrayEFC.88 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.
item.author: alex.mcmurray@efinancialcareers.com (Alex McMurray)

item.title: Hybrid-loving bank Standard Chartered hired a new head of wellbeing and flexibility
item.description:
As major banks called staff back to the office </news/banking-work-from-home> last year and ranted about the woes of work-from-home Friday, </news/jamie-dimon-work-from-home>Standard Chartered was one of the few banks that stood firm with its hybrid policy. This year, it seems to be doubling down on flexibility.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Standard Chartered hired Anna Seely in London late last month as its head of wellbeing and flexibility. She spent the last six years at tech consultancy Accenture, where she was a managing director, and has a background in psychology.
Seely takes over from Rachel Plaistowe who was global head of wellbeing until November of last year. Plaistowe said on her LinkedIn that the role entailed "embedding wellbeing </news/2023/05/resilience-banking> into the flow of work, building wellbeing-related skills, and reframing wellbeing as an enabler of sustainable high performance" for over 80,000 employees.
The flexibility element appears to be an extension of the role, but is in line with Standard Chartered's broader culture. CEO Bill Winters (who also works hybrid) famously said last year: “We work with adults. <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-07-31/stanchart-ceo-resists-office-mandates-we-work-with-adults?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=250731&utm_campaign=author_20887312>The adults can have an adult conversation with other adults and decide how they’re going to best manage their team."
Employees are generally offered 2 to 3 days at home per week </news/the-happiest-people-in-standard-chartered-spend-two-days-in-the-office>, but that doesn't mean they take them. When we surveyed bankers on time spent in the office late last year </news/days-in-the-office-by-bank>, Standard Chartered staff said they spent 4.2 days per week away from home.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Morning Coffee: War is coming for banking jobs, but maybe not in the way that you thought. How hedge fund star Hamza Lemssouguer makes money
item.description:
Jamie Dimon at JPMorgan thinks something is up. It's not Donald Trump's threat to destroy Iran "in one night" <https://www.ft.com/content/35346eaf-0869-4706-8ebb-2d171e15266f?syn-25a6b1a6=1>unless the Strait of Hormuz is opened by 8pm Eastern time today. It's not the tone of Trump's tweets <https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump>. It's the war's impact on inflation and thus interest rates. “The skunk at the party—and it could happen in 2026—would be inflation slowly going up,” Dimon opined in his Investor Letter <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir/annual-report/2025/ar-ceo-letters> accompanying the bank's newly released 2025 annual report.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Skunks are making themselves felt even without Trump's shaking of the nuclear sceptre. "All roads" <https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/war-middle-east-will-lead-slower-growth-higher-inflation-imf-chief-tells-reuters-2026-04-06/> lead to higher prices, declared the IMF's managing director yesterday. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/hedge-funds-face-worst-monthly-drawdown-over-four-years-goldman-sachs-tells-2026-04-01/> says hedge funds have been "hammered." Citadel's global fixed income fund was down 8.2% in March. Blue Owl stock has fallen to a lower low <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-06/blue-owl-stock-closes-at-a-record-low-amid-private-credit-exodus?cmpid=eveus&utm_campaign=eveus&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260406> than before. Tiger Global is being declawed. <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-06/tiger-cubs-viking-maverick-pummeled-last-month-amid-war-in-iran?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260406&utm_campaign=author_17940573>
The war is also changing the nature of financial services jobs. Homeworking is back in Paris, where the Financial Times <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir/annual-report/2025/ar-ceo-letters>reports that both Goldman Sachs and Citi are allowing staff to avoid the office after an Iran-linked group called for the bombing of Jewish targets, including the Bank of America office, which was subject to a bomb attempt </news/citi-traders> last week. In Saudi Arabia, Semafor <https://www.semafor.com/article/04/06/2026/riyadh-evacuates-main-business-hub-over-iran-threat> notes that Riyadh evacuated its main financial zone in the King Abdullah Financial District (KAFD) on Thursday. Goldman Sachs, HSBC, BNP Paribas and others are based there.
It's not just evacuations. As jet fuel prices rise and flights are cancelled, in-person client meetings will stall. But the intrepid few who keep travelling and keep meeting clients, will reap dividends.
Dividends of intrepidness are already on display. Fresh from February's note </news/alap-shah-citrini-research>predicting a meltdown in middle class mortgages, Citrini Research despatched an unnamed researcher to the Strait of Hormuz <https://www.citriniresearch.com/p/strait-of-hormuz-a-citrini-field/comments?utm_source=substack%2Csubstack&publication_id=836125&post_id=193267829&utm_medium=email%2Cemail&isFreemail=true&comments=true&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&r=qrn8>for a field trip. We have not read the resulting note but the WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/iran-war-latest-news-updates-2026/card/wall-street-analyst-goes-on-hormuz-field-trip-with-15k-in-cash-cuban-cigars-cSF1DVfjbkCysCvExGTQ>says the researcher went equipped with Cuban cigars and $15k in cash. CNBC <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/06/wall-street-firm-sends-analyst-to-the-strait-of-hormuz-heres-what-they-found-out.html>says he interviewed "fishermen, smugglers and regional officials." Citrini's man discovered that the Strait is not in fact closed and that crude oil shipping volumes are higher than previously thought. It's just that ships are not being tracked. They are turning off their transponders to pass through a "functional checkpoint" imposed by Iran.
The new Citrini report is already thought to have led to a softening in the oil price. Banks and hedge funds that were relying on Automation Identification (AIS) system data from transponders for oil price predictions are wrongfooted. Intrepid analysts willing to go into warzones with cigars are the new top skillset.
Separately, Hamza Lemssouguer </news/hamza-lemssouguer-hedge-fund>, the 35-year-old former Credit Suisse trading star turned hedge fund manager, has enough personal wealth to own 160 rare parrots and a 400-year-old Tudor Estate in the English Home Counties.
The Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/hamza-lemssouguer-arini-capital-hedge-fund-firm-99c961d5> explains how Lemssouguer got here. He doesn't drink. He doesn't drive. He grew up in modest circumstances in Morroco and studied at France's École Polytechnique before joining Credit Suisse and reportedly </news/2021/07/credit-suisse-top-traders-leaving> generating $220m of PnL in a single year in 2020.
Now Hamza runs his own fund, Arini, which has $20bn in AUM. He employs a mix of analysts and traders from the likes of Afghanistan, Turkey and Ukraine, but has just 20 analysts in total. The WSJ says Arini is about often supersized trades whic often involve heavily indebted companies. It makes loans to some. It shorts the bonds of others.
Hamza likes to go big. And he likes to be different. “Being young and also being an outsider and unconventional allows [me] to not be arrogant towards the market,” he tells the WSJ.
At Credit Suisse, Hamza became the bank's top bond trader ten years ago, when he predicted that Jaguar Land Rover was headed for a fall. While everyone else in the market thought the stock would rise, Lemssouguer called-up Jaguar Land Rover dealerships directly and discovered their inventory was rising. “I took the contrarian view,” he says. This is where the edge is - in war and beyond.
Meanwhile...
Goldman Sachs placed its Paris office under police surveillance. (euronews) <https://www.euronews.com/business/2026/04/03/us-bank-goldman-sachs-placed-under-police-surveillance-in-paris>
Brevan Howard says its Abu Dhabi staff can go somewhere else. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/brevan-howard-offers-abu-dhabi-staff-option-to-temporarily-relocate-as-war-continues-6a2cd00a?mod=homepage>
XTX Markets' revenues were up 33% last year. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-02/gerko-s-xtx-markets-earnings-rise-33-on-global-trading-surge?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260402&utm_campaign=author_19908303>
Redemption requests at Blue Owl's top fund have surged to 40% of its value. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/f4320148-3d81-4bd0-9ab6-053a5bade188?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Redemption requests at Goldman Sachs' private credit fund are only 5%. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-06/goldman-sachs-private-credit-fund-dodges-exodus-with-4-999--redemptions>
Barclays still likes leveraged finance and hired Nick Fall from Goldman Sachs. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-06/barclays-hires-goldman-s-fall-for-us-leveraged-finance-role>
JPMorgan hired Tom Rees from Eisler to start an FX systematic trading unit. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/jpmorgan-hires-former-eisler-capital-portfolio-manager-to-start-trading-unit-f7a8fd02>
AI is decimating crypto jobs, particular when they relate to data and marketing. (plexusrs) <https://plexusrs.com/ai-induced-layoffs-in-crypto-are-rewriting-the-org-chart/>
Andy Sieg is no longer one of the five highest paid executives at Citi. (AdvisorHub) <https://www.advisorhub.com/citi-wealth-chief-andy-sieg-fell-off-list-top-paid-execs-for-2025/>
Bill Ackman has written a very long post revealing that he employed his nephew who upset someone at his family office, who is now bringing a discrimination claim. (X) <https://x.com/BillAckman/status/2040547553675194539>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.
item.author: sbutcher@efinancialcareers.com (Sarah Butcher)

item.title: Must-Knows for Hiring in the Singapore Job Market in 2026
item.description:
Are you hoping to hire fresh new talent for your business in Singapore this year? Despite economic uncertainty, regulatory complexity and rapid digital transformation, the Singapore job market grew significantly in Q4 2025 <https://www.hcamag.com/asia/specialisation/recruitment/singapore-labour-market-strong-but-set-to-ease-in-2026/559892>.
In fact, some estimates report <https://vulcanpost.com/907760/singapore-job-market-companies-hiring-2026/> that nearly a third of businesses in Singapore will be hiring this quarter. But every one of those roles will be under the microscope for fast and effective return on investment, shared Ken Ong <https://www.linkedin.com/in/kenongsingu/>, Managing Director of Morgan McKinley Singapore in our recent 2026 Singapore Salary Guide <https://www.morganmckinley.com/sg/salary-guide>:
“Permanent hiring has stabilised but remains selective. Businesses are scrutinising each role to ensure it contributes directly to strategic growth. At the same time, demand for contract and project-based talent has risen sharply as companies look for agility and cost efficiency without compromising capability”.
So, what’s the best way to stay competitive, with that in mind? “Success will hinge on how effectively companies use talent as a strategic differentiator. The most forward-thinking employers are reimagining their workforce models, combining permanent and flexible talent and prioritising skills over credentials”, says Ken.
Here’s his advice for how to do exactly that:
1. Prioritise Hiring for Niche Skill Sets
The era of hiring based on a prestigious degree or a long list of previous job titles is rapidly fading in favour of a "skills-first" methodology <https://www.morganmckinley.com/sg/article/why-skills-based-hiring-future-recruitment>. In 2026, Singapore’s most successful organisations are those that identify and target "plug-and-play" talent. These are individuals who possess the specific, high-level technical capabilities required to hit the ground running once they arrive in the door.
This shift is particularly evident in sectors like Technology and Legal, Risk & Compliance, where regulatory shifts and rapid digital adoption create immediate voids that only specialists can fill. By prioritising these niche skill sets, businesses ensure they aren’t just filling a seat, but are actually acquiring a strategic asset. This will be what differentiates a high-value hire from a standard replacement.
2. Gain Agility By Using Vetted Contractors
The "contractor economy" has evolved from a backup plan into a core pillar of strategic workforce planning. Forward-thinking companies in Singapore are now intentionally blending lean, permanent core teams with highly skilled contingent specialists to drive time-bound transformation projects. This allows businesses to access elite expertise without committing to long-term overhead costs.
By leveraging contractors, organisations can pivot their strategies at speed, scale up for critical product launches or regulatory deadlines and scale back once the objective is met. In 2026, the competitive edge will go to those who treat their contingent workforce as a strategic asset, ensuring they have the "elasticity" to innovate and deliver results without being slowed down by hiring constraints.
Want to explore contracting hiring? Find out here <https://www.morganmckinley.com/sg/employers/recruitment-solutions/contract-recruitment> how our team can help you take the first steps to finding top contractors.
3. Ensure You Have the Right Tech Talent
We are seeing a significant "scarcity premium" for experts in cloud architecture, cybersecurity, and MLOps, with some niche roles commanding salary uplifts of up to 30%. For businesses, this means that "good enough" technology talent is no longer sufficient. Technical resilience now depends on securing the high-level architects who can design secure, scalable, and AI-ready infrastructures.
Attracting this talent requires an approach that goes beyond just compensation. Because these specialists are in such high demand, they are looking for environments where they can work on cutting-edge tech stacks and have a genuine influence on product delivery. To stay competitive, organisations must highlight autonomy and the opportunity to lead transformative projects.
4. Transform Support Teams Into Strategic Teams
The traditional view of HR, Finance, and Supply Chain as support functions is being dismantled as automation and offshoring absorb basic transactional tasks. These departments need to transition from administrative oversight to strategic business partnering. These high-value individuals need to interpret data analytics to drive ROI, navigate complex international ESG regulations, and act as consultative partners to the C-suite.
As basic operational roles are phased out, the demand for talent with "business acumen" has surged. Organisations are now looking for finance leads who understand digital transformation and HR partners who can lead people analytics. By elevating these roles into strategic positions, companies ensure that every department is contributing directly to business growth.
5. Learn How to Impress the Discerning Candidate
The candidate pool in Singapore is more data-literate and value-driven than ever before. In 2026, professionals are no longer guessing their worth. They are using tools like the Singapore Salary Calculator <https://www.morganmckinley.com/sg/salary-guide/calculator> to benchmark their pay against the broader market. Beyond fair compensation, these discerning candidates are prioritising roles that offer genuine flexibility, a clear sense of purpose, and meaningful pathways for progression.
For employers, this means that a "one-size-fits-all" approach to recruitment is no longer effective. Success in 2026 requires an employer brand that can clearly articulate its "purpose-led" culture and commitment to employee wellbeing. Organisations that offer hybrid work models will naturally win talent over those that remain rigid. Understanding and meeting these evolving expectations is now a prerequisite for attracting the high-calibre professionals who drive innovation.
6. Use Hiring Speed as Your Competitive Advantage
In an employer-driven market, hiring processes can stagnate due to excessive layers of approval and budget scrutiny. However, by streamlining their hiring (such as empowering business heads to make faster decisions), leading companies are closing roles for critical talent in as little as two weeks. This prevents losing top candidates to more agile competitors who are ready to move.
Speed is especially crucial when dealing with niche contractors and high-demand specialists who often have multiple offers on the table. A prolonged interview cycle is often interpreted as a sign of a bureaucratic or indecisive culture. By defining must-have skill sets early and shortening the time-to-offer, organisations not only secure the best talent but demonstrate an operational efficiency that appeals to high-performing professionals.
7. Upskill Staff as a Means to Fix Skills Gaps
Relying solely on the external market to fill skill gaps can sometimes be a high-risk and high-cost strategy. The most resilient organisations are using upskilling and reskilling as a defensive play to future-proof their existing workforce. By investing in training focused on digital risk, sustainability, and AI literacy, companies are building internal talent that can step into evolving roles as needed.
Beyond the technical benefits, a robust upskilling strategy is one of the most effective retention tools available. When employees see a clear investment in their professional growth, their loyalty increases, reducing the cost and disruption of turnover. By prioritising development, businesses create a more adaptable, future-ready workforce that is less susceptible to the volatility of the market.
Want to learn more about the trends defining the Singaporean jobs market? Download Morgan McKinley’s new salary guide – or speak to one of their consultants <https://www.morganmckinley.com/sg/find-staff> about your hiring.
Get the free guide <https://www.morganmckinley.com/sg/salary-guide>
item.author: editor@efinancialcareers.com (Morgan McKinley)

item.title: Multistrategy hedge funds' 2025 hiring (and firing) laid bare in ADV filings
item.description:
Multistrategy hedge funds like Citadel, Millennium and Balyasny do not file annual reports. They do, however, make annual filings with the SEC known as ADV forms. Those forms have just been filed.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
As shown below, the latest filings confirm Izzy Englander's Millennium as the multistrat Goliath. With 6.670 employees, Millennium is nearly twice as big as Steve Cohen's Point72 and more than twice as big as Ken Griffin's Citadel.
In total, the biggest hedge funds added nearly 2,000 people last year, with Millennium alone adding 530 of them and Point72 adding 394. Citadel was more circumspect, with only 131 extra employees.
In percentage terms, though, the biggest hirers were not the biggest funds. Headcount at Jain Global </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience>was up 73% as the firm presumably added "35 year-old killers" and other such talents following its launch in 2024. Verition increased headcount by 20%; Balyasny by 18% or 371 people.
The table below shows how many of multistrategy firms' employees are actual investment-focused as opposed to support and and other non-investment staff. Jain Global, Balyasny and Verition were again the big hirers. By comparison, Schonfeld and Walleye reduced investment headcount in the past year.
The ADV filings also reveal discrepancies between the balance of investment and non-investment staff at major funds. While only 36% of people at Walleye are investment professionals, 56% of people at Jain Global are, for example.
A high proportion of investment professionals means a low proportion of non-investment professionals and by implication comparatively few people like technologists and executive assistants. Bobby Jain in February said that his fund built its infrastructure with the assistance of AI and therefore didn't have to hire "hundreds of technology people."

item.title: Investment banking at Rothschild will make you rich, but not rich rich
item.description:
Being a top investment banker </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> at Rothschild & Co. seems like something hard to beat. Few names in the industry have the prestige of Rothschild – and the (financial) allure. However, reality is quite different for senior investment bankers currently employed at the firm.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Rothschild doesn’t have many such senior investment bankers, who are referred to as material risk takers (MRTs). These are a regulatory term that refers to employees whose “professional activities have a material impact on the risk profile of the firm,” as well as all those that earn over £660k (€756k/$871k)
More worryingly, the ones that it does have are not very well-paid. Rothschild had 51 investment banking MRTs in 2025, which it paid an average of €237k ($273k) in fixed compensation and €239k (€276k) in variable compensation, for a total of €476k ($549k) on average. That’s not a lot. In fact, it’s roughly what a 20-something year old investment banking VP in London earns </news/2023/10/london-investment-bank-pay>, for someone apparently at the peak of their career.
The situation hasn’t improved much for Rothschild’s banking MRTs in recent years. In fact, it’s declined – Rothschild banking MRTs earned €464k ($535k) on average in 2024, and €561k ($647k) on average in 2023. The fall since then has been driven by declining salaries.
If not investment banking, then how do you get rich at Rothschild? Asset management </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-asset-management>, apparently. Asset management MRTs employed at Rothschild have been the highest-paid group of people since 2022, and in both 2024 and 2025 averaged over €1m ($1.2m) a year in pay.
In 2025 specifically, the average asset management MRT at Rothschild earned fixed compensation of €459k ($532k) and variable compensation of €606k ($703k) for total compensation of €1.1m ($1.2m). Those salaries were almost double what the average investment banker earned, and more than double the bonus.
Rothschild doesn’t break down pay bands for high earners to the same degree that bigger banks do, but it had 17 employees earning more than €1m, six earning over €2m, and four earning over €5m. That’s not so bad, considering that it is quite a small bank.
For reference, Barclays employs around 1,500 MRTs </news/barclays-bonuses-2026> in its investment bank and pays them an average of £1.5m ($2m) on average. It also has around 700 people earning over €1m.
