item.title: Citadel Securities' secret sauce and the $2m pay premium
item.description:
If you're leaving the Goldman Sachs trading floor now, there's a well-trodden path to take. It doesn't lead to a hedge fund </news/finance/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>, but to Citadel Securities, the electronic trading firm </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms>. Traders and salespeople </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> from Goldman Sachs and other leading banks are congregating there. This is why.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citadel Securities declined to comment for this article, but publicly available information helps explain its appeal. Citadel Securities is growing fast and it's highly profitable. Sales and trading businesses in banks are being squeezed by the cost of technology investments and by margin compression. Multistrategy hedge funds </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds> are also now struggling in the wake of recent losses in their macro businesses. </news/macro-hedge-fund-managers>Citadel Securities' rival electronic trading </news/hft-pay-london> firms have a broader focus.
Figures released <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-24/citadel-securities-nets-record-12-billion-trading-haul-in-2025> this week showed Citadel Securities making $6.5bn in EBITDA on revenues of $12.2bn last year. This kind of profitability enabled the firm to pay its 1,800 people a total of $3.5bn for 11 months, implying an average of $2.1m each a year. </news/citadel-securities-pay-2m>Top traders can make this at Goldman Sachs; average traders, maybe not.
Speaking to Risk Magazine <https://www.risk.net/awards/7962597/flow-market-maker-of-the-year-citadel-securities> in December, Citadel Securities' ex-Goldman Sachs president Jim Esposito </news/jim-esposito-citadel-securities-president> and chief operating officer Matt Culek, explained what differentiates the firm from banks.
Banks' trading systems typically evolved for financing activities, said Esposito. These financing activities tend to involve long dated trades and to be capital intensive, he added. As a result, banks' trading platforms are less able to quickly redeploy capital as needed. Combine this with the fact that banks' trading operations are often siloed and managing their risk independently, and it can be hard for banks to understand risk positions and to offer liquidity during times of high volatility.
Citadel Securities is different, said Esposito. The firm uses its own in-house technology and is able to calculate exposure across multiple businesses and products to capture netting effects. Combined with a buffer that's retained at the "top of house" for times of spiking volumes or volatility, this means capital at the firm can be very swiftly reallocated to maintain liquidity for clients.
It's not clear how Citadel Securities has fared since the US and Israel launched strikes in the Middle East on 28 February, but during the volatility created by Trump's tariffs in April 2025, the firm said it recorded daily average Treasury volumes worth $70m in per-basis-point sensitivity, with hit ratios of 45%. "They provide an incredible amount of liquidity. Basically, the banks don’t want to enter into that kind of business,” one client informed Risk.
Citadel Securities has competitors beyond banks, though. Jane Street, IMC, Hudson River Trading (HRT) and others, are also in the liquidity-provision space. Jane Street might even pay more on average than Citadel Securities. </news/jane-street-versus-citadel-securities-pay>
However, market insiders say that comparing the electronic trading firms is not comparing like with like. While Citadel Securities has focused on being a pure market maker (“We like to think we are the Amazon of finance. We trade more equity flow than anyone on the planet,” says Esposito), rivals are more heavily focused on investing their own money. Jane Street, for example, is thought <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-04/jane-street-s-trading-haul-juiced-by-surging-bet-on-anthropic>to have made about $830m, or 12% of its third quarter revenues, last year from investing in Anthropic. HRT has units like Prism, which focus on the kinds of mid-frequency longer duration trades <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2025-12-01/high-speed-trader-hudson-river-steps-up-battle-with-citadel-securities-jane-str>that more commonly found in hedge funds.
This helps explain why, as Citadel Securities boosts its institutional business, market makers from banks keep climbing aboard. Nikhil Choraria, one of Goldman's profitable traders and the former head of the European rates desk, </news/nikhil-choraria> is due to join in about six months' time as head of fixed income alongside ex-BofA trader Shyam Rajan. Citadel Securities can make markets even in the most volatile situations, and can pay accordingly. In these times, Choraria is unlikely to be the last top trader to succumb to its appeal.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: European banks are going to struggle in London. This is why
item.description:
The European Union bonus cap </news/bonus-cap> has been officially scrapped in London, and the world has changed. Some banks in the city - mostly British and American ones - also scrapped their caps very quickly. Some banks in the city - mostly European and Japanese ones - have kept them. The former group is well placed for the future... And the latter is not.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Adrian Crawford, employment partner at law firm Kingsley Napley, explained why. “There is currently [a] divergence in the market,” between the two bonus approaches, Crawford said. This could lead to "a difference in culture between European and US banks" in the long run.
The divergence is partly related to inertia - changing European banking culture is very complicated. “European banks have probably been slower to make any change because doing so will mean treating UK staff differently from those in Europe,” Crawford explained. The EU itself is also involved in the process: “making changes which are now permitted under the UK rules might raise issues with other regulators outside the UK to which banks are subject.” If you're a European bank that's passported into London and is regulated in Europe, scrapping the cap is probably not an option.
This is difficult, because bankers will always be drawn to bonus potential. “Any bank which does not offer an attractive bonus opportunity is likely to lose staff and become less competitive,” Crawford said. “Bankers consider themselves to be strong performers,” whether they are or not, and they seek the opportunity to prove themselves.
“Lower performers may prefer the security of higher fixed pay and less bonus opportunity,” Crawford said, and “star performers can therefore be confident of being well-rewarded in either environment”. Top performers want the chance to prove themselves, and earn bonuses.
And so those that remain in European banks will have a few things in common. They will be people who prefer the security of high fixed compensation (salaries) more than the potential of variable compensation. And they will be a generally lower-performing group of individuals.
The benefits for the banks that choose to keep the cap will be limited. A blog post on Bank Underground, written by dissenting and anonymous Bank of England staff, said back in 2022 <https://bankunderground.co.uk/2022/12/19/how-does-remuneration-regulation-affect-bankers-pay/> that under the bonus cap, high-performing bankers simply had their salaries increase as well as their bonuses. There was no real impact from the bonus cap on how total compensation grew, just its composition.
In reality, the bonus cap just means that, due to ever-increasing salaries, bankers face limited consequences and banks have less flexibility in down years. When pickings are slim, American banks will be able to pay their most expensive employees less. European banks will be saddled with heavy salaries. Weirdly, bankers overwhelmingly prefer, and will seek out, the former over the latter.
“A bank which retains role-based allowances and a lower bonus cap has higher fixed costs and less ability to reward star performers with high bonuses,” Crawford said.

item.title: Electronic trading firm IMC hired 450+ people as it grows in London, New York and Hong Kong
item.description:
Amsterdam-based electronic trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> IMC had a big year in 2025, as revenues rose 40% to $3.12bn <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/market-maker-imc-s-trading-revenue-rises-40-on-volatility-surge>. It had a lot of new faces helping it out; IMC said in its 2025 annual report that it made over 450 new hires in 2025, reaching a headcount of 1,900.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The firm didn't break down how many employees it had in each office, as it did in its previous report, but it noted that both London </news/hft-pay-london> and Hong Kong </news/as-jane-street-and-other-prop-trading-firms-expand-in-asia-these-are-the-new-faces-and-new-teams> were "growing." Those teams had ~20 and ~5 employees, respectively, in IMC's 2024 report. IMC officially announced its Hong Kong expansion in October, and is hiring for quants, traders and engineers <https://www.imc.com/us/corporate-news/imc-expands-hong-kong-office#:~:text=We%20are%20actively%20seeking%20researchers%2C%20traders%20and%20engineers%20to%20join%20our%20expanding%20Hong%20Kong%20office%2C>in the region.
IMC said that "expanding [its] New York office... will be a key focus for IMC over the coming years." IMC has also grown in both India and Denmark, </news/algorithmic-trading-firm-imc-adding-commodities-traders-to-new-london-office> moving to a larger office in the former, and opening a brand new office in the latter.
IMC marginally expanded its internship class too. It hired over 220 in 2025, up from 200 the year prior. 55 of those are thought to have been in the US, where 29 were allegedly brought back full-time on up to $425k pay packages. </news/a-high-frequency-trading-firm-is-offering-its-interns-425k-to-come-back-as-open-ai-looms>
It wasn't just expensive traders being hired. IMC said it also "continued to expand [its] risk and compliance function in line with IMC's growing headcount."
IMC has seemingly struggled to hold onto some employees as well. Headcount in its 2024 report was 1,600, growing just 300, suggesting 150 people left.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Mysteriously rich Asian Masters student only wants to work in banking. JPMorgan developers must facilitate the possible elimination of their own jobs
item.description:
If you don't get a junior banking job straight out of university, </news/target-universities-finance-uk> you can always study a Masters in Finance </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking> and try getting your junior banking job after that instead. However, Masters in Finance courses are expensive. At Warwick Business School, for example, UK students pay £37k and international students pay £45k in fees. Not everyone can afford this.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The sort of person that can afford a Masters in Finance is a mysterious student with links to Singapore, who is known as 'GKC'. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/da09ffdc-e334-4715-80a2-7ae9d1bc4666?syn-25a6b1a6=1> says GKC owns two UK apartments worth a combined £4m, and has £800k in bank accounts. When she finishes the course, she wants to work in banking.
As contemporary students in the UK will testify, there are many GKC-like students to be found on British Masters in Finance courses. They might not all have circa £5m ($6.7m) in net assets, but they often have plenty of cash.
British nationals are discouraged from pursuing Masters courses because when they graduate they will have to pay an additional 6% in income tax on everything they earn above £21k as they repay their student loans. UK students already have to pay 9% in additional tax on loan repayments for their bachelors courses, so the combined additional tax payments are 1,500 basis points. The structure of the British tax system means that British Masters students who go on to earn over £100k pay a punitive marginal tax rate of 77% on their earnings <https://www.economicsobservatory.com/why-do-some-people-in-the-uk-face-marginal-tax-rates-of-over-60#:~:text=Student%20loans%20can%20further%20reduce,jumps%20even%20higher%20to%2077%25.> between £100k and £120k. It's just not worth it.
If you're the mysterious GKC, though, your wealthy benefactor has likely paid your fees and you probably don't have any student debt anyway. It's not easy to get a banking job in London </news/i-m-a-chinese-student-trying-to-get-a-banking-job-in-london-it-s-not-easy>following changes to student visa rules, but you can potentially get a job in Hong Kong, where things are currently booming.
This is fortunate for GKC, who despite being worth nearly $6.7m, is having a spot of bother. The UK's National Crime Agency has imposed an "unexplained wealth order" upon her, demanding that she explain how she came by all that money given that doesn't seem to have worked for years. The suspicion is that it's related to a money laundering case in Singapore.
It all sounds very stressful, particularly given that GKC has been at boarding school in this country since the age of 16 and likely needs money to stay. Banks interviewing a stressed Singaporean Masters student could take pity and at least offer a short internship.
Separately, corporate-speak is strong in banks and nowhere is it stronger than when it comes to AI. On one hand, the spiel goes, that AI will increase productivity and liberate humans to do interesting work appropriate to multicellular mammals. On the other, banks want to cut costs, and replacing developers with AI is a way of doing so.
JPMorgan's developers are therefore in a spot. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/jpmorgan-objectives-software-engineers-developers-boost-productivity-ai-2026-3>reports that the bank is commanding the 65,000 people in its technology division to "drive excellence" by using AI, or face the consequences.
JPMorgan's developers have reportedly been ordered by the HR team to: "Demonstrate measurable improvement in code quality, speed and productivity through regular use of approved AI coding assist tools, contributing to the team's overall efficiency targets". And to: "Engage in identifying, implementing and optimizing AI-driven automation opportunities within technology lifecycle management (TLM) processes to drive efficiency and support capacity unlock initiatives, ensuring all enhancements leverage current technology assets before considering new solutions."
Failure to assimilate these commands will be problematic. Developers at JPMorgan are anxious. Someone's seen a management dashboard categorising them as "light", "heavy" and "non" users of AI tools. JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon has spoken publicly <https://qz.com/jpmorgan-chase-hiring-ai-jamie-dimon-1851781327>about over-hiring during the pandemic and using AI to negate the need for new hires. </news/jpmorgan-hiring-2026>Developers who don't leverage the optimization for efficiencies may be in trouble.
Meanwhile...
Oil traders are exhausted. Only high frequency strategies used by banks and electronic market makers seem to be trading. “It’s changing so fast that you can be long one minute, and then next thing you know, something comes out and you’re short.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/oil-traders-fatigued-by-wild-price-swings-throttle-back-flows?taid=69c575d0a8829a00016ec965&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Deutsche Bank created a "pressure index" showing when Trump is likely to make a conciliatory statement causing oil prices to fall. “If the index moves up, a probability of strategic adjustment by the US administration is more likely. If all four pain points hurt, the incentive to adjust is very high.” The pain points=weighted 20-day change in S&P 500, 10-year US Treasury yield, Trump approval rate and 1-year forward inflation. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/bc6037d6-d6cf-4117-9eb0-311956ea9dd1?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Lloyd Blankfein says difficult times may be ahead. “The analogy I like to give is you accumulate tinder on the floor of the forest and eventually a spark will come. But the longer between intervals where there’s a spark that sets it on fire, the more that accumulates.” (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-25/ex-goldman-ceo-blankfein-warns-of-fire-risk-in-private-markets?taid=69c46a1e6d936300016cc8c5&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>)
“The market isn’t being erratic, this is what an efficient market looks like in the face of radical uncertainty. People are shifting gears incredibly fast or they’ve backed away. The market’s confusion is entirely rational.” (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/6fc9a9f5-ce8f-471a-a4d2-2166d63e3bd8?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Millennium gave cash to Engineer's Gate but now they've mutually agreed that the cash will be withdrawn. Engineer's Gate wanted to the cash in the account to be subject to quarterly withdrawals in normal markets with the possibility of investor-level gates in stress periods. It also calculated the allocation at $1.5bn, while Millennium saw it as $3.bn including leverage. (Bloomberg) < https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/millennium-and-engineers-gate-wind-down-hedge-fund-partnership>
IMC's annual report says it made $3bn in trading revenue and $968m in profit. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/market-maker-imc-s-trading-revenue-rises-40-on-volatility-surge?srnd=phx-industries-finance>
BNP Paribas is rolling out an AI companion for all its staff. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/bnp-paribas-set-to-roll-out-ai-companion-for-all-staff?sref=DK3y4h9m>
It's going to be great to work in Hong Kong. Profits from a wide range of investments may soon be be eligible for tax treatment as carried interest, including earnings in hedge funds. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/b018d802-a7d1-4853-9acd-d3007a61cf72?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Dentists can earn $400k but they also have huge tuition fees and loan repayments after buying dental practices. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/personal-finance/the-complicated-financial-lives-of-dentists-the-millionaires-next-door-60abc67c?mod=hp_lead_pos9>
If you work for the PiF now you won't just be investing in Saudi Arabia. “The Saudi macroeconomic and physical position remains strong, stable and resilient. We measure our returns not in quarters but in decades, and PIF remains committed to its investments around the world.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/saudi-wealth-fund-commits-to-global-investments-despite-iran-war?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260326&utm_campaign=author_24138202>

item.title: "I work for a bank that is systematically sexist"
item.description:
I regret to write this, but I work for a bank that is systematically sexist. I joined this institution because I thought it was a great place to work as a woman. It is not.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Since joining here four years ago, I have been perpetually surprised by what I have witnessed.
These invents include, but are not limited to:
- Working with an alcoholic contractor (hired through nepotism) who spent his time harassing multiple women, inside and outside the office, for months while management did nothing.
- A male senior VP sexually harassing a junior employee. Despite multiple complaints, no action was taken, and the junior left. The SVP remains in the same role.
- A female director (another nepotism hire) who often claimed illness but was seen out of work and encouraging younger women to firt in order to advance their careers.
- Senior staff involved in relationships with juniors that influenced promotions and career progression.
- Teams pushed to breaking point by a director who screamed at employees publicly or on Zoom without providing additional resources.
- A culture of gossip and favoritism controlled by a small inner circle of Senior MDs dictating who succeeds.
I am not mentioning the name of the bank, but I find this all astonishing. change. People warned me against joining and I now regret dismissing the warnings I received. If someone cautions you against joining a particular bank, I strongly suggest you listen.
Sabrina Barbet is a pseudonym

item.title: The best places for women to work in financial services may not be banks
item.description:
It’s tough to be a woman in finance </news/female-bankers>. But if you’re flexible on where exactly in finance you work – away from those blasted investment banks, for example – then you have a lot more options for your career progression.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
A new report from <https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/69c3eac493cc6e8b87a6f65f/HMT_WIFC_Review_2025.pdf> the UK treasury, in partnership with think tank New Financial, shed some light on the issue. While investment banks have been struggling to field female representation at their highest levels, there are two types of employers that have had much less of a problem doing so: insurance companies and UK-based banks.
Insurers are the heroes of the hour. As a group, they became the most female-representative of the four company types tracked by the treasury back in 2023, but last year met an important milestone – being the first company type to reach 40% of female representation among its senior management.
Investment banks, by some margin, are the worst place for women in senior roles. There were a few reasons for this. BNP Paribas' London investment banking was noted to have “low turnover amongst senior management roles” and “a limited female candidate pool particularly in front office, STEM and specialist roles”, as well as “a narrow pipeline of female talent internally.” Its relatively low target of 30% female senior management was extended from 2025 to 2030.
Another poor sector – although nowhere near as bad as investment banking – was investment management. AXA Investment Managers, which had a 35% target that it dropped to 33%, blamed “natural attrition” for its changing goal, of all things. It also blamed “organizational change”, namely, its acquisition. It is unclear why this would disproportionately impact female managers.
Still, BNP and AXA are both significant employers of women, obviously. BNP’s country head for the UK is a woman - Emmanuelle Bury-Lucas. And so is AXA’s former head of alts, Isabelle Scemama, who is now BNP’s global head of alts. There are plenty of women at the top.
Some organisations have gone even further with their ambitions, however. Three firms, according to the Treasury, have targets - that they've achieved - to have 100% female representation at senior levels: Beaufort Group Consulting, Campbell & Fletcher, and Magenta Financial Planning. All are small businesses that appear to be founded and run by women (according to their companies houses filings). There is no room for senior men there.
Overall, parity with men is a generationally distant dream for the financial services industry as a whole. UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves, in the foreword to the report, noted that “at the current rate, we will not reach parity for another two decades.” The old boys club </news/senior-finance-discrimination> continues – for now, at least.

item.title: What next for the macro hedge fund managers in the mire?
item.description:
The tide has turned for the macro hedge fund manager. </news/2021/05/how-to-get-a-job-in-a-global-macro-fund>Having spent the past few years in a snazzy wetsuit rubbing "Sex Wax" <https://www.sexwax.com/>into his board, a big wave has come and knocked him (sometimes her) off his feet. The future will not be like the past.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bloomberg <https://x.com/nishantkumar07/status/2036861219760468118?s=20> reported yesterday that, as suspected </news/hedge-fund-job-fears-after-tempestuous-week>, macro hedge funds are hurting. Macro veteran Caxton Associates was down 15% between the start of March and March 20th. Taula Capital was down 9.6% over the same period. Brevan Howard's </news/brevan-howard-technology> Master Fund was down 6%.
Painful for the firms, these figures are excruciating for individual portfolio managers who not so long ago were surfing from job to job on huge pay packages. </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-pay-millions>Two thirds of portfolio managers (PMs) at some funds are likely to be 9% down on their high water marks in the estimation of Mark Pacitti, a former Citadel and Goldman Sachs quant. "Some of these pods are done. Not struggling. Done," Pacitti says.
What next, if so? Maybe a return to a trading seat in bank? One top macro headhunter says there's still demand for macro traders on the sell-side and that a loss in this market will not be career ending. "Everyone has lost money," he says. "Every element of the market has been distressed at some point in the last three weeks and the normal hedges didn’t work. It's not that 30% have lost; 100% have." War in the Middle East has been worse for macro hedge funds than last April's tariffs and maybe even COVID, he adds. At least, during COVID, markets weren't reliant on Trump's tweets.
However, macro traders who seek shelter in banks may find the view there displeasing. "The linear business in banks has lost money to hedge funds as the bid-offer is tight," says one senior rates trader at a bank in London. "Banks use a softer risk limit model than hedge funds do now, and people find it hard to come back to the sell-side. They've enjoyed the lifestyle on the beach too much," he adds.
Another senior trader at a bank in London says the macro hiring market is not happening. "There are a lot of people looking for jobs and too few openings for them," he tells us.
So what do you do if you're a macro hedge fund manager sitting on a large loss? Maybe nothing. As we noted earlier this week, portfolio managers who moved recently on large guarantees </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-pay-millions> are fine: they get paid no matter what. But even portfolio managers without big guarantees can sit it out on salaries.
"A lot of people have lost money, but they haven't done anything stupid," says the headhunter. "Everyone knows that this is an unusual situation." He observes that hedge funds that might usually be inclined to cut people with losses are being unusually tolerant: "Hardly anyone has lost their job. Most people are being asked to close positions and wait until the dust settles."
One senior macro trader with experience in both banks and hedge funds says much depends on individuals' track record and the circumstances preceding the loss. "A lot of people have reached their stops, but it all depends how they were reached and the communication with risk managers," he says. "If you've been irresponsible they will move to terminate you. But if you're an established portfolio manager who communicated well, they will tell you to de-risk your positions and see what happens when things normalise."
Longer term, though, the trader predicts a more aggressive shakeout. "Right now, the senior management of these funds isn't reacting because they're occupied with the stability of the fund overall and the risk of drawdowns rather than individuals' pnl. - It will be three months or more before you see individual PMs leaving."
The people who are most exposed have recently moved into hedge funds from banks, says the trader. "They have no track record, the fund doesn't know you well, and you just lost money quickly." It's still not the end. "They will be slowly recycled back into the industry when things get better," he suggests.

item.title: New York City’s investment banks are shrinking, while paying more
item.description:
Much has been said about New York mayor Zohran Mamdani </news/bankers-mamdani> and his impact on the city’s bankers </news/zohran-mamdani-tax-bankers>. While it’s still too early into his tenure as mayor to call time on the industry’s future, one thing is for sure: there are fewer bankers in New York City than there have been for years.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
The office of New York State Comptroller, Thomas DiNapoli, estimated that 198,200 people worked in New York City’s securities industry in 2025. That was a 2% decline on the 201,500 employed in 2024, and even a decline on the 198,400 employed in 2023.
However, the decline in jobs is not an indictment of how important finance is to New York, but rather how important New York is to finance. DiNapoli noted that, in 2024, just 17.9% of American securities roles were based in New York City, compared to around 33% back in 1990.
The securities industry might be expanding, but its role in New York City is still paramount. NYC accounts for 22.5% of all private sector wages paid in the city, as well as 4.9% of private sector employment in the city.
Even by New York standards, Wall Street is very well paid – and 2025 was a very good period on the pay front, even if it wasn’t on the employment one. The average salary in the industry was $506k in 2025, according to the comptroller’s office, and the average bonus was $247k – meaning total compensation of $753k. Average salaries increased by 7.3% on 2024, average bonuses increased by 6%, and average (total) compensation increased by 6.9%.

item.title: Hedge fund Two Sigma's spacious new office in London makes it Jane Street's neighbour
item.description:
Quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Two Sigma has had its share of controversy in recent years, particularly regarding the squabbles between co-founders John Overdeck and David Siegel. </news/two-sigma-is-still-the-fun-hedge-fund-despite-its-founders-squabbles> Despite this, the fund posted its best returns of the decade <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-02-05/how-two-sigma-founder-s-divorce-fight-hangs-over-hedge-fund-s-future#:~:text=Its%20biggest%20funds%20have%20posted%20solid%20returns%20in%20recent%20years> so far in 2025. As it looks to improve further, the fund has found itself a new office in London. </news/best-paying-hedge-funds>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Companies House filings show Two Sigma has changed offices to the fifth floor of 7 Devonshire Square, located near Liverpool Street. Devonshire Square's website shows that the fund occupies the floor by itself <https://www.dsq.london/buildings/office/dsq-7/#:~:text=FLOOR%205,TWO%20SIGMA>. It's understood that it will also occupy the sixth floor after undergoing refurbishments.
Two Sigma should recognize one of it's new neighbours, Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street>, which has an office on the same estate, occupying the entirety of 212 Devonshire Square. This might be a fleeting arrangement, as Jane Street is reportedly looking to expand its office footprint in London and is exploring moves elsewhere. <https://www.ft.com/content/9b6953ba-9106-4926-b71b-527b866c28b8>
Two Sigma used to live in City Tower on Basinghall Street occupying three floors. However, the new office is thought to have a larger square footage of around 20,000 square feet (including the second floor once completed). The office will be able to house roughly 200 people; Companies House accounts for its UK entities showed it had 107 UK employees in 2024 (predominantly engineers), and its website states it still has over 100 people in London.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs head of M&A does not seem to be getting ready for job cuts. Macro hedge fund pain
item.description:
Earlier in the year, we were told that 2026 was going to be like 2025, but better – more consistent deal volume and less geopolitical drama. So far … it’s early days. Global investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> revenue is tracking about 10% up on last year (remember, 2025 had quite a slow start) and M&A is up 25%. But credit losses have begun to show and the geopolitical drama has not quite gone away. So there’s a lot to play for, and a real two way debate between optimists and pessimists.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Stephan Feldgoise, Goldman Sachs’ </news/morgan-stanley-goldman-sachs-job-cuts-2026> head of global M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma> is definitely among the optimists <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-25/goldman-m-a-head-sees-massive-capital-pools-driving-m-a?taid=69c41818f133a40001e9c029&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>. In an interview with Bloomberg, he reminds us of that slow start (and the “Liberation Day” tariff shock) which worried everyone last year, but which quickly “turned into one of the most active if not the most active” second halves of the year on record.
When you look at Feldgoise’s reasons for being bullish, you might worry, though, because some of them are quite long in the tooth. He talks about the existence of “massive pools of capital … an incredible amount of capital sitting with investors … looking to invest in transactions into companies”. And these pools do exist, but the “dry powder <https://www.ft.com/content/6b64d81c-92ba-4d93-8241-154b1bb3b354>” thesis has been a regular feature of investment banker discourse for several years now.
His other main point, about the strategic rationale for consolidation, is also well made. But although “If there is something that you think you should do to position you for the next, not 10 weeks or 10 months but 10 years or 20 years or 30 years, you should seriously think about doing it” is a good reason for doing deals, is it a good reason for doing deals right now? You always need to aim-off for optimism when you hear someone this senior talking, because they have to be that way to get where they are – glum bankers don’t win mandates.
The rule is always – don’t listen to what senior bankers say, watch what they do. And the other rule is that targets are an opinion, forecasts are an opinion but headcount is a fact. So what are bankers actually doing?
Well, across the Street, Bank of America has set up a new “Private Capital M&A” team <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-25/bofa-starts-private-capital-m-a-team-to-focus-on-pe-exits> to concentrate on doing deals involving exactly those “massive pools of capital”. A couple of months ago, JPMorgan also set up a private capital team <https://www.thebanker.com/content/8dd7629f-fd7b-4a12-a206-636582f0240e>. Goldman doesn’t seem to have had a specific team launch, but last year it made Chris Convey and Haidee Lee <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-22/goldman-sachs-names-colin-convey-to-co-run-global-sponsor-m-a> the global co-heads of “Financial Sponsors M&A” alongside their other coverage responsibilities.
So, it looks like the bulge bracket top management genuinely believe that the pools of cash on the sidelines are about to be put to work. Or at least, they think it’s a real enough possibility that you don’t want to be caught napping when the next wave of deals begins. That seems like good news given that Goldman is embarking upon a new exercise in rolling job cuts. </news/morgan-stanley-goldman-sachs-job-cuts-2026> Feldgoise seems willing to see through the empty months without removing heads.
Elsewhere, it was quite public a couple of weeks ago that Caxton Associates <https://www.ft.com/content/f47e1478-7b4b-4dd3-8037-6643549ac737?syn-25a6b1a6=1> had been caught on the wrong side of the trade when bond and commodities prices reacted to the news from the Gulf. Since then, things seem to have got worse <https://www.ft.com/content/21191b60-c5fc-4c49-be1f-87c0beafb016?syn-25a6b1a6=1>. The drawdown has grown from $600m to $1.3bn; the flagship macro fund is down 15% and the total loss is getting on for a tenth of the total assets under management.
That’s the kind of territory which makes everyone nervous. Caxton has a long track record and a stable investor base, so it’s less at risk than a smaller or younger hedge fund. It was even able to tighten its liquidity terms <https://www.forbes.com/sites/jacobwolinsky/2022/11/28/institutions-are-dumping-their-hedge-fund-allocations-on-a-massive-scale/> in 2022, and the current drawdown comes after a very strong year last year </news/london-caxton-kirkoswald-pay>, during which the employees seem to have fully shared the upside.
But none the less, it’s no fun to look at your “high water mark” and realise that you’re several fathoms further down. Caxton might claim it's not alone - Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/bridgewater-flagship-avoided-significant-pain-march-macro-performance-2026-3> notes that Brevan Howard and Taula have also lost money. But it's not at all pretty.
Meanwhile …
Jefferies results are often prophetic for the sector, and they seem to show the pattern of strong year-on-year performance in the actual business, weighted down by the credit consequences of questionable deals from previous years. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-25/jefferies-investment-banking-windfall-eroded-by-credit-woes?srnd=phx-industries-finance>)
If AI is a gold rush, and datacentre companies are the ones selling pickaxes, then KKR has worked out how to make money by selling axe handles in a pickaxe rush. One of its best investments ever has turned out to be a specialist computer-cooling company, whose employees have apparently gained about $240,000 each as the buyout returns to market. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/business/deals/kkr-scores-one-of-its-best-bets-with-sale-of-data-center-cooling-business-6c017f92?st=PeyNj6&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink>)
Hedge funds have decided to make it clear that they’re not running scared from the Middle East. After stories of staff relocating, Millennium have reiterated their commitment to Dubai. Verition have extended their office lease there by five years, and Hudson Bay Capital have also opened up an office in Abu Dhabi. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-25/hedge-funds-shed-usual-reticence-to-vouch-for-dubai-abu-dhabi?srnd=phx-industries-finance>)
The SpaceX IPO prospectus is apparently planning to part from industry custom by listing all the syndicate banks in alphabetical order, rather than in order of importance. So there will be no “top left” position to covet, and league table credit will be hard to apportion. (The Information <https://www.theinformation.com/briefings/spacex-ipo-bankers-will-forgo-coveted-lead-left-position>)
This might or might not be related to the bankers’ willingness to keep it ambiguous whose deal this is, but the SpaceX IPO might also dispense with the usual “lockups” for its current shareholders, and has raised the valuation so that it will be the largest IPO ever. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/37a14f36-77f6-413e-a4d5-b05cefbddfb1?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Apparently government chiefs of staff aren’t subject to the same rules as bankers when it comes to backing up messages on their work phone. So after one theft, quite a lot of evidence requested by a parliamentary inquiry is gone forever. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/01e56a86-8dee-437a-b422-7db8c6231567>)
Brewdog beer is going to be served in the Hamptons this year (NY Post <https://nypost.com/2026/03/24/business/craft-beer-brewdog-to-launch-in-hamptons-after-tilray-ceo-buys-troubled-brewer/>)

item.title: The ex-Citi VP at the heart of GPU trading: "We're growing 200% every six months"
item.description:
Carmen Li has had an atypical trading career. Instead of starting out at a bank and moving to a hedge fund, she began her career at an electronic trading firm (DRW), moved to a bank (Citi) and then founded a start-up. Now she's chief executive of both that start-up (Silicon Data) and of Compute Exchange, the new exchange for used and refurbished GPUs (Graphics Processing Units).
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Compute Exchange launched in January 2025. It was co-founded by Don Wilson, the former Chicago pit trader who co-founded DRW back in 1992. In November 2024, Wilson informed the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/4001099b-12a9-4a3f-bf21-7333e88b536d?syn-25a6b1a6=1> that he thought GPUs and "compute" would become the biggest commodity in the world. Last September, Wilson told Bloomberg <https://www.listennotes.com/podcasts/odd-lots/the-king-of-chicago-trading-YCcwlovOu87/?srsltid=AfmBOor-sj4ucl5qfxXd9l-XiQCw5i6egplOx844PuWbykuwwnoDQ3wQ> that as the GPU market develops, it will become possible to create GPU-related futures contracts. "I envision that these will be traded kind of like electricity futures, where there's one for every month, and if you want to hedge the next three years, you sell thirty six of them and now you've locked in your pricing," Wilson added.
This is where Silicon Data comes in. Silicon Data creates publicly available indices based on 280,000 datapoints that can be used to price GPU futures. Li was already involved in GPU index creation, and so when Wilson wanted a CEO for Compute Exchange, she was a natural choice. "I already knew him from my time at DRW," Li explains.
Compute Exchange doesn't own GPUs, but is the equivalent of an electronic market maker in the units, which underpin AI computing capacity. "We list the inventories that are available and when a request comes in we will dynamically ping the providers. They will give a price and the client will get a full specification back," says Li. "It's not just about price but actual performance and things like thermal latency degradation."
None of the GPUs listed are new, but Li says this doesn't matter. GPUs last longer than people think and as the software improves they will last longer still. Refurbished GPUS are also available at shorter notice than new ones. "You can wait six months for a B300 [Nvidia's new chip]," she says. Refurbished chips are available almost immediately.
Li was a senior VP in working capital products at Citi, but left the bank in 2022. She claims to be having a lot of fun in her new existence. Notional trading volume is growing 200% every six months at Compute Exchange and Li says she's at the heart of something special: "I love working on these products. We are trying to bring transparency to the whole GPU ecosystem."
Other people in financial services are also looking for a slice of this fun. Li says both Silicon Data and Compute Exchange employ engineers with backgrounds from Millennium and elsewhere. She's hiring human data engineers even though agentic agents are dealing with a lot of the flow. "We want to make sure it's all done properly," Li explains.
What Li won't be hiring are sales or marketing professionals. She doesn't need them. "There are zero salespeople at Silicon Data," she explains. "We have no sales people, zero paid ads and no marketing dollars. We just pride ourselves on being the best for compute, and if you google anything about GPU pricing you will see us and our benchmarks." This seems to be a good approach: revenues have increased 5x in the past two months.

item.title: "I grew up on a remote mountain and got into banking. Junior bankers don't know how lucky they are"
item.description:
I work in investment banking </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>for one of the largest banks in the world. My route in was not easy; junior bankers joining through summer analyst </news/internship-investment-bank>schemes fail to realize how lucky and privileged they are.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
I grew up in some of the most remote mountains of an emerging market country. By 18, I had never lived in a city and had never owned a television, phone, or had access to the internet. I left to pursue higher education in the west, and the first few years were the toughest of my life. It took me more than a decade to get into banking after I got my degree.
I spent two years working in consulting in my home country before securing a place on a business banking graduate scheme. I started in customer service but quickly moved up to mid-market corporate banking. I spent seven years there and acquired so many qualifications on the side that people would judge me. I was finally offered a temporary role in healthcare M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs>(as an analyst), which my manager prevented me from taking. I left to pursue an MBA </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking>, and finally received an investment banking offer in the final month of the course.
In banking, my salary </news/salaries-and-bonuses-goldman-sachs-jpmorgan-citi-baml-morgan-stanley> increased by more than five times. The hours, pace, technical precision, awareness, and energy required were immense. I didn't realize at the time, however, how privileged I was; I hadn't built anything or brought any transformational ideas to the table. All the deals I worked on were handed to me on a plate by senior MDs in their 50s. My previous experience was more emotionally complex and relationship driven.
When I became a director in my 30s, I felt nine years behind people my age who joined banking as a graduate, and had to work three times as hard just to stay level. In interviews, </news/70-top-banking-interview-questions-and-how-to-answer-them>I'd hear terms like 'plug-and-play', 'team fit', 'linear success', and 'deep experience' which are often camouflage for ageism, especially against women. Ageist awards like 30 under 30 </news/up-and-coming-28-year-olds-jpmorgan>, and the arrogance that accompanies their winners, don't help.
Success in investment banking is as much a function of opportunity, privilege, market timing, geography, and mobility as it is of talent and effort. I am simply more employable when I remove my graduation date.
Rubi Illescas is a psuedonym

item.title: Barclays hired some new securitization professionals, now it's having second thoughts (again)
item.description:
Barclays' asset backed securitization (ABS) aspirations have a complicated history. In 2015, the British bank scaled back <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-07-28/barclays-said-to-cut-european-abs-sales-trading-research-heads> its ABS business in Europe and cut the team heads. Since then, it's been building things back up. Now it's getting cold feet again.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-25/barclays-pulls-back-on-asset-based-lending-after-mfs-tricolor?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260325&utm_campaign=author_21061455> reported today that Barclays is pulling back on asset backed loans after losing up to £500m on loans to UK mortgage provider Market Financial Solutions </news/barclays-mfs> and £110m+ on loans to US subprime auto parts provider Tricolor. Both loans were underwritten by collateral, but in both cases there are suggestions that this collateral may have been double pledged.
Barclays declined to comment for this article. Bloomberg said today that the bank is scaling back loans to small corporates and focusing on loans and securitizations for larger corporates instead. The bank may reportedly change tack (again) if its risk profile changes.
Barclays' current cold feet come after the bank said it was interested in growing its securitization business when it unveiled a new strategy </news/barclays-2bn-cost-cutting-program-will-hit-people-in-the-investment-bank-hardest> back in February 2024. At the time, Barclays said it hoped to earn an additional £500m annually from a combination of additional revenues from securitization, European rates trading and equity derivatives. It was not intending to lose money instead.
Barclays has since made some big hires in the securitization space. In February 2026, it appointed Chetan Vohra from Cerberus Capital Management as global head of securitized products. Vohra replaced veteran securitization banker Scott Eichel </news/scott-eichel-barclays>, who joined in 2018 after a career at RBS and who was also supposed to grow the business. Eichel hired people like David Garner, Barclays' global head of securitized products bond trading, who joined in 2024. Last October, Barclays appointed existing bankers Benjamin Fernandez and Jonathan Wu as co-heads of ABS and residential mortgage backed securitization globally, based in New York.
As Barclays withdraws from securitized lending to small corporates, it's not clear whether its people involved in this occupation will remain occupied. It's not entirely surprising that the bank is having second thoughts, though. £610m ($815m) of potential losses are not negligible and the bank is keen to increase returns in the investment bank while cutting costs and minimizing risk weight assets allocated to the business.
Speaking in February, Barclays' CFO Anna Cross said revenues in the investment bank were already expected to grow more slowly this year, as both the rates "tailwind" dissipates and market share growth in investment banking increases more slowly. The bank can't afford more big losses in ABS. Earlier this month, Barclays' CEO CS Venkatakrishnan </news/venkat-Barclays-CEO-strategy> said he was sorry that the existing losses occurred. This was probably a sign that a cold wind was coming.

item.title: French bank in New York hires top private equity banker from Japanese rival
item.description:
The private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> industry sniffed some wins in 2025. Consulting firms such as Bain & Co. <https://www.bain.com/insights/outlook-gaining-traction-global-private-equity-report-2026/> believe that 2026 will be even more promising – and some bankers are even coming out of retirement for the opportunity.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
John Stroll joined Crédit Agricole earlier this month as a managing director (MD) </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> in the French bank’s private capital group for the Americas. Stroll is based in New York for Crédit Agricole, as he was for his previous employer, Mizuho.
Stroll left Mizuho at the end of 2024, after six years at the firm, which made him an MD in its financial sponsors and strategic solutions team.
It isn’t clear what Stroll did exactly in 2025, but given his social media posts requesting various materials related to owning and operating small businesses, it seems possible that he tried to strike it out on his own. He wasn’t registered anywhere on FINRA, either, suggesting that he worked away from Wall Street. Stroll declined a request to comment.
Despite the name, Crédit Agricole’s private capital group does not involve itself in private credit </news/private-credit> transactions. Its website claims that it supports “private equity transactions, as well as infrastructure and real estate investors”, meaning it has avoided much of the carnage in recent years that has led to firms such as Ares and Apollo beginning to limit investor redemptions <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/25/private-credit-defaults-loan-quality-debt-risk-systemic-ai-disruption.html>. Lucky for some.

item.title: Must you take a graduate job at Goldman Sachs to found a successful fintech startup?
item.description:
When looking at careers in finance, you might like the concept of joining a fintech startup </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech> over a more corporate career, or even starting your own. Having a flashy name on your CV can go a long way when trying to become a founder, both for attracting talent and investors, but how necessary is it that you pursue a graduate job at the likes of Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>before striking out on your own?
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Alexis Augier, </news/alexis-augier-vega> founder of asset management infrastructure firm Vega, did just that; he spent seven years working across banking and private equity (PE) at firms including Goldman Sachs and KKR before becoming a founder. In an ongoing AMA on The Bubble, our new anonymous forum, he said that he wouldn't have done much to change his career path except "perhaps working for a year or two at a high-growth startup."
Augier followed the path trodden by some of the biggest founders in fintech. Nik Storonsky of Revolut </news/revolut-the-fintech-hiring-0-15-of-applicants-raised-pay> was once a trader at Lehman Brothers, where he claims that he earned almost half a million dollars as a first year bonus. </news/revolut-career-harvester-cv#:~:text=He%20claimed%20on%20the%20podcast%20that%20his%20first%20bonus%20as%20a%20trader%20was%20%22definitely%20close%20to%20half%20a%20million%2C%22%20almost%20ten%20times%20the%20average%20bonus%20of%20a%20bulge%20bracket%20analyst%20in%202025.%20Evidently%2C%20not%20all%20bank%20employees%20are%20equal.>David Velez of $68bn fintech Nubank started out as an investment banking </news/2023/05/whats-an-investment-bank> analyst at Goldman Sachs in New York. Kalshi </news/electronic-market-makers-trading-on-kalshi-are-small-fish-in-a-big-pond> founder Tarek Mansour </news/tarek-mansour-kalshi-sam-bankman-fried-ftx> interned as an equity derivatives analyst at Goldman Sachs before stints at Palantir and Citadel. Some, like Coinbase's Brian Armstrong and Plaid's Zach Perret, came from Consulting </news/graduate-consulting-cv-template> instead.
There are also, however, many successful founders with unconventional backgrounds. The Collison brothers </news/stripe-remote-work-patrick-collison> of Stripe </news/stripe-high-performance-engineers>didn't even finish university, dropping out of Harvard and MIT a year before founding Stripe. Klarna </news/klarna-ceo-pay-sebastian-siemiatkowski> founder Sebastian Siemiatkowski met one of his co-founders while working at Burger King.
There's a reason the background of successful founders isn't ubiquitous; Augier said that corporate experience can be "helpful for things like fundraising, but finance won’t teach you the day-to-day reality of building a company." Other ex-Goldman founders we've spoken to that have said that life as a founder is completely different and involves working even longer hours </news/goldman-sachs-analyst-fintech-working-hours-gen-ai> across multiple disciplines (Augier said that "if you start your company you will think about it 24/7").
The issue with a corporate route for banking and PE roles in particular is that you won't be doing too much technical or engineering work (beyond Excel). This can present issues when creating a technology firm but Augier said "the key is to be able to hire people who complement your skillset and who are rockstars in their field." Surrounding yourself with these subject matter experts is "a big part of the job."
If you want to ask Augier more questions about his time in banking, private equity or fintech, you can sign up to The Bubble and comment for free here. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/t/ama-i-m-the-founder-and-ceo-of-vega-ask-me-anything-about-finance-careers-or-working-at-vega/5219/5> His week-long AMA ends tomorrow, so act fast.

item.title: 35-year-old hedge fund star invests in "the wonders of nature," as well as credit
item.description:
Hamza Lemssouguer </news/hamza-lemssouguer-arini> is the person to know in London if you're interested in credit investing. He is also the person to know if you're interested in saving and breeding parrots. </news/hamza-lemssouguer>And now, he is the person to know if you run an educational or wildlife conservation project in South America or Africa.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Lemssouguer and his wife Aline have launched the A&H Foundation. <https://www.ah-foundation.org/>The foundation describes itself as working "collaboratively with local partners and communities to deliver lasting, measurable impact to advance education and preserve the wonders of nature together," and says it's rooted in the Lemssouguers' "passion for education and conservation." It aims to work with, "local communities, scientists, governments, NGOs, philanthropists, and the private sector to educate children and protect endangered species while honoring local cultures and tradition."
Hamza was a star trader at Credit in his 20s </news/2023/10/hamza-lemssouguer> before founding hedge fund, Arini Capital Management, in 2022. Aline Lemssouguer was a vice president in credit sales at Credit Suisse between 2018 and 2023. Ken Costa, a former chair of investment banking at UBS is on A&H's board along with other London-based bankers and investors.
Lemssouguer is not the only financial services professional supporting the natural world. XTX founder Alex Gerko </news/alex-gerko>is investing in nature restoration. Quadrature Capital </news/quadrature-capital> runs a climate foundation encouraging reductions in carbon emissions. James Davies, the former head of Deutsche Bank in America, has bought a house and forest </news/deutsche-bank-s-ex-head-of-its-americas-investment-bank-wants-you-in-the-wild>in Western Massachusetts and is running natural retreats for executives in need of spiritual renewal. "If you bring your executive committee here, I will run a weekend for free,"Davies told us last year.

item.title: BNP Paribas hired way more interns last year, but fewer in France, weirdly
item.description:
In a world in which AI has been discussed </news/jpmorgan-ai> as potentially cutting intern classes </news/internship-applications-banks> at investment banks by up to two-thirds, it can be reassuring to know that banks are still hiring normal-sized intern classes – banks including BNP Paribas.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
BNP released its annual report <https://invest.bnpparibas/en/document/universal-registration-document-annual-financial-report-2025-pdf> earlier this month and detailed the number of interns it hired. Overall, the number of interns at the French bank was up, from 1,674 in 2024 to 1,834 in 2025, an increase of almost 10%. The 2025 figure was even up on the 1,759 interns that BNP recruited in 2023.
Strangely, however, the number of graduates in France specifically declined, from 490ish in 2023 and 2024 to just 387 in 2025. That's a substantial drop– around 21% – and it’s not entirely clear why. The claim made in August last year that the bank recruited an “unusually small number of summer interns” might be related.
While the data doesn’t go far back, it’s still a positive upward trend. At Deutsche Bank </news/deutsche-bank>, by contrast, the number of graduates has decline steadily since 2023, from 1,177 to 1,025, a decline of 13%. It’s also declined steadily at Goldman Sachs </news/how-many-interns-goldman>, from 2,900 to 2,600, a decline of around 10%.
There were some other interesting tidbits buried in BNP’s report. For example, the fact that 5% of BNP’s female workforce in EMEA identified as disabled in 2025, compared to 0.2% of female employees in Asia Pacific, or 1.7% of female employees in the Americas. Or the fact that men have a slightly higher voluntary turnover (attrition) rate – and less training hours on average – than women.

item.title: Exits from HSBC's DCM team in London, post bonuses
item.description:
HSBC has paid its bonuses for 2025 </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k> and the sands are shifting as the money is banked. We understand that at least one senior person in the London DCM team has gone on her way, possibly accompanied by another.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Elena Farrell, a managing director (MD) in SSA origination at the bank is understood to have resigned for personal reasons. Farrell spent a decade at the bank after joining from RBC Capital Markets. Her last day was yesterday.
It's understood that Dennis Surgrue, an MD in the London capital solutions team has also tended his resignation after joining HSBC from S&P Global just over two years ago.
HSBC declined to comment.
Farrell is thought to have returned from maternity leave in recent months. Last year, HSBC cut two members of the DCM team </news/upset-as-hsbc-s-cuts-include-a-woman-on-maternity-leave-and-her-replacement> in March, including Farnam Bidgoli, its head of global ESG Solutions, who was on maternity leave at the time. Bidgoli is now on the FCA's sustainable finance committee.
Hugo Moore, </news/hsbc-s-dcm-people-are-slipping-away-before-bonuses-are-paid> a veteran HSBC FIG DCM banker left shortly before bonuses. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-19/hsbc-cuts-10-of-us-debt-capital-markets-team-amid-overhaul> reported that HSBC cut 10% of its US DCM team in early February, which was shortly before bonuses were announced.
HSBC paid its material risk takers an average of $1.4m each </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k> last year. This generosity came as a pleasant surprise </news/hey-hsbc-s-us-bonuses-were-really-great-actually> to some.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Citadel Securities did indeed seem to pay its people $2m each on average last year. Dubai hedge fund managers face the risk of having to endure the rain
item.description:
In return for the mild inconvenience of saying “no, the trading firm not the hedge fund” often at parties, Citadel Securities </news/2022/10/how-to-get-a-job-at-citadel> seems to treat its employees very well indeed. The latest numbers reported by Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-24/citadel-securities-nets-record-12-billion-trading-haul-in-2025> seem to confirm that not only has it had a record year, but that our predictions from last year </news/citadel-securities-pay-2025> were correct; the average compensation per head at Citadel Securities is not only into seven figures, but no longer has a one at the start of it.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Revenues there grew from $9.7bn in 2024 to $12.2bn in 2025 , and compensation expenses grew a bit faster in percentage terms. The data only covers the first eleven months, but the bill for that period was apparently $3.5bn. That would translate to $1.94m each for 1,800 employees for the eleven month period; unless something very strange was going on, that means the full year figure is well above $2m. (A simple annualization would reach $2.1m per head, and if the final month includes any element of “trueing up” of bonus accruals, it could be higher).
Averages always conceal as much as they reveal, of course, but Citadel Securities doesn’t have very many junior employees and admin people to bring the average down; even the interns </news/citadel-securities-teenage-prodigies> are on the pro rata equivalent of $200k for the summer program. So it’s very likely that if you met someone at a party the other week who said “no, not the hedge fund”, then you were talking to someone really quite rich. Particularly if they were a maths genius </news/2023/08/citadel-olympiad-hiring> or very avuncular </news/jim-esposito-citadel-securities>.
Citadel Securities is likely to be fine with this. The non-personnel costs of running a modern trading business are considerable, but $2.1m a head is very affordable when your revenues are $12.5bn and $6.5bn of this is profit. And whether Citadel Securities likes it or not, it will probably have to pay up, because right now </news/citadel-securities-staff-in-london-are-appreciated-by-banks-and-trading-firms>, it seems like it’s a very strong name to have on your resume in terms of being hired by other trading operations.
So, although the question of “bragging rights” doesn’t really arise (the compensation structures are completely different, and you can’t compare the revenues of a trading business to a set of investment returns, Citadel Securities is by no means a poor relation to the more famous multistrategy fund that shares its name. Who knows, maybe there will be a barbecue somewhere in the Hamptons this year where somebody utters the phrase “oh no, not the trading firm, the hedge fund”.
Elsewhere, although the climate and nightlife are famous, and the cultural offerings arguably overrated, it would be ridiculous not to admit that a major reason why hedge fund employees were so keen to move to Dubai wasn’t tax. And now, after a sudden and sharp reassessment of the lifestyle factors, some of Millennium Management’s expat staff <https://www.ft.com/content/3a3dfe47-4afa-4956-9244-893e5899453b?syn-25a6b1a6=1> have decided that although they have left the Gulf and don’t want to return, that doesn’t mean that they are completely willing to give up on the dream of keeping their gross income all for themselves.
And so one of the options that Millennium, along with a few other hedge funds, is apparently exploring is an expansion of its Jersey office. British, Irish and EU citizens with UK settled status don’t need to get any new work permits to go there, and while it’s not completely tax free, income tax is only 20% and there’s no capital gains tax.
Jersey also has quite a lot of amenities for the rich, and if you like boiled new potatoes <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jersey_Royal>, there’s nowhere on earth to compare. It’s also considerably more convenient for London (perhaps dangerously so, if the ease of taking a helicopter makes you lose count of the days needed to establish your tax residency). But it has to be admitted, although Jersey is the sunniest place in the British Isles <https://business.jersey.com/news/jersey-officially-still-the-sunniest-spot-in-the-british-isles/>, that’s not up against demanding competition; unlike Dubai, it often goes months without having two consecutive days <https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/crm848gxly0o> without rain.
The clock is ticking, to a certain extent. The Gulf States are being generous about allowing expats to stay away while retaining their residency, but any British or European citizens who have stayed home may find their home countries less resident. And there’s the danger of one’s employer remembering about another island with good tax arrangements, very reliable internet connections, but out in the rainiest bit of the Irish Sea </news/2019/07/accounting-jobs-isle-of-man>.
Meanwhile …
Carlyle Group are lucky enough to have gone into an extremely tense geopolitical situation with a vice-chairman who is better placed than most to analyse developments, because he’s the former supreme allied commander of NATO. Admiral James Stavridis is pretty eyes-open about the “tiny flickers of good news”, and surprised that investors are not more focused on downstream consequences of oil shortages. But he notes that post-war reconstruction has often delivered some huge economic success stories; it’s not impossible that Iran and Cuba might be the hottest private equity stories of 2036. (Semafor <https://www.semafor.com/article/03/23/2026/carlyles-admiral-james-stavridis-says-investors-are-already-mapping-out-post-war-iran?utm_medium=business&utm_campaign=First+Word&utm_source=newsletterlink>)
If you’re an “almost unemployable misfit”, but in a “creative or technical savant” way rather than an “obvious compliance risk” way, Hummingbird Ventures might want to talk to you. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/4d29c556-bbd9-490e-a3c8-90f5b894af9e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Alvarez & Marsal had the biggest partner promotion class ever last year and they haven’t stopped – they want to add “hundreds” of partners by hiring and promoting, to reach ambitious revenue targets. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/alvarez-marsal-to-add-hundreds-of-partners-in-push-for-8bn-revenue-6f9a2e67?mod=topic_news>)
One of the Big Four firms has apparently won the contract to audit Tether, although they’re currently not saying which one or when the audit is going to be carried out. Although Tether is one of the world’s most profitable companies per employee, it’s not necessarily one of the most transparent, so somewhere a compliance specialist is looking forward to a wild ride. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-24/tether-s-landmark-fundraise-on-hold-as-crypto-giant-seeks-audit>)
JP Morgan has hired quite a few Bank of America bankers recently – after Roy Wouters and Kevin Brunner, it’ s now recruited Brian Henderson to be head of North American business services investment banking. (Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/jpmorgan-recruits-bank-america-services-banker-latest-senior-hire-rival-2026-03-23/>)
The Chinese government’s morality drive has put a stop to the serving of expensive wine at official functions, leaving some producers with as much as $150m of unsellable stock. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/world/china/xi-jinpings-morality-crackdown-has-a-new-victim-the-global-wine-trade-f030b778?st=JmfBkt&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink>)

item.title: JPMorgan hired an AI scientist from Meta's Reality Labs after its VR head quietly left
item.description:
Meta </news/working-hours-banking-vs-tech>'s VR arm Reality Labs (formerly known as Oculus) has been having a rough time as of late. Its parent company has reportedly soured on Metaverse <https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-mark-zuckerberg-metaverse-80-billion-loss-ai-2026-3> and laid off 10% of Reality Labs staff <https://www.nytimes.com/2026/01/12/technology/meta-layoffs-reality-labs.html> to spend more money on AI. One of the top AI scientists within Reality Labs is moving on, joining a bank that curiously just lost the man leading its VR initiatives.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Lambert Mathias announced via LinkedIn that he's joining JPMorgan in Seattle as a managing director (MD). Rather than virtual reality, he says he's working on agentic enterprise AI </news/agentic-ai-generative-use-cases-finance>. He spent six years with Reality Labs "building multimodal, egocentric perception systems" using LLMs </news/how-barclays-is-addressing-it-s-biggest-problem-with-ll-ms>and AI agents.
Before Meta, Mathais spent eight years with Amazon as a machine learning scientist. He worked in the unit behind its Alexa Echo devices, with a focus on improving its conversational capabilities. This might come in handy; JPMorgan chief data officer Theresa Heitsenrether said last year that junior bankers will soon manage teams of 'digital colleagues,' </news/jpmorgan-bankers-ai#:~:text=Teresa%20Heitsenrether%2C%20JPMorgan%27s,managing%20%22digital%20colleagues.%22> which will be much less lonely if those colleagues can hold a conversation.
Mathias arrives at a particularly interesting time given his VR background; JPMorgan's global head of immersive technology and spatial computing research, Blair McIntyre, quietly left the bank this month according to his LinkedIn. He worked on augmented reality in addition to VR since 2022, </news/2022/07/jpmorgan-virtual-reality-jobs> and initially worked under the bank's quantum </news/quantum-computing-jobs-banking>guru Marco Pistoia, </news/it-s-not-just-jp-morgan-goldman-sachs-and-others-lost-quantum-talent-too>who left the bank last year. McIntyre continues to work as a professor at Northeastern University, which he joined before coming to JPMorgan.
JPMorgan was one of the banks most enamoured by VR. It was the first major bank to enter the Metaverse, where it opened a virtual lounge, and has previously used VR to train provide immersive training for juniors. McIntyre said the technology could be combined with AI and integrated into the workflow of JPMorgan staff to provide "serendipitous access to information." </news/2023/12/jpmorgan-virtual-reality>
It's not clear whether JPMorgan has a replacement lined up for McIntyre, nor whether Mathias will lend his VR-expertise to the division in addition to is agentic AI duties. JPMorgan did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Hedge fund Millennium hired an ex-JPMorgan MD whose team made a loss
item.description:
It's been a while since we've written about Gianfranco Canepa, the former head of high yield for EMEA at JPMorgan. In 2022, we noted that he'd left JPMorgan after his team made a €500m bet </news/2022/12/jpmorgan-high-yield-loss> on a French grocery store, and that the bet had lost €70m after Canepa's exit.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Post-JPMorgan, Canepa spent the past few years at hedge fund GoldenTree Asset Management, but now he's moving again. We understand that he is joining Millennium in London.
Millennium declined to comment. Canepa will be amidst some familiar faces there. In January, Millennium created a standalone credit business under Dan Friedman, a former Goldman Sachs partner whom it hired from Diameter's London business in 2023. Canepa also worked for Goldman between 2017 and 2020.
Millennium is thought to have another new London hire too. Michael Boam, the former head of leverage finance research at Sona Asset Management is also thought to be enroute.

item.title: Bill Ackman’s hedge fund employs 44 people. It seems nice there
item.description:
Bill Ackman’s pro tennis debut, which he lost in straight sets, was rather sternly criticised <https://edition.cnn.com/2025/07/11/sport/bill-ackman-criticized-professional-tennis-hall-of-fame-open-spt> by former pros such as former world No 1 Andy Roddick. Luckily, there is one field he is above most reproach: running his hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>, Pershing Square Capital Management.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Pershing Square wants to IPO soon. It’s not clear how soon, but soon enough. Two weeks ago, the SEC dropped its prospectus, as it does with all companies that intend to IPO imminently.
In that prospectus, Pershing Square noted that it had 44 employees at the end of 2025, and that it paid out a total of $20.2m in compensation in 2025. That comes to around $460k per person. The people who work for Ackman are well paid.
Of its 44 employees, it notes separately that just nine are “investment professionals”. That’s a very small team, but it makes sense for an investor whose public portfolio consists of just 13 huge, publicly listed firms. It also says that it has “a legal and compliance team, a technology team, a trading team, a finance team, a public relations team and an investor relations teams [sic].”
Who’s getting the big bucks, then? The executive team. CEO and founder Bill Ackman earned $143m for his work in 2025, and $47m for his work in 2024. Ryan Israel, his second-in-command and Chief Investment Officer, earned $44m and $28m in those same years. Ben Hakim, the firm’s president, earned $18m for his work in 2025.
Pershing Square’s 44 employees puts it firmly in the realm of the world’s little tiny hedge funds </news/small-hedge-funds> such as Chris Hohn’s TCI Fund Management, which has around $70bn in AUM and just 50ish employees. Unlike Pershing Square, TCI Fund Management is a long-only firm.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.
