item.title: Morning Coffee: 22-year-old JPMorgan bankers on $160k are wild for overwork. Goldman Sachs' newly perpetual job cuts
item.description:
If you work for JPMorgan, you're supposed to work no more than 80 hours a week. </news/jpmorgan-restrict-working-hours-banking>There are exceptions, like times of live deals, but 80 hours are the mandatory norm. And yet junior bankers seem to be abusing JPMorgan's trust.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The bank has mandated 80 hours, but JPMorgan's 22 year-old bankers earning circa $160k (as per the Prospect Rock Compensation Report <https://prospectrockpartners.com/prospect-rock-partners-2025-ib-compensation-report/>) cannot be trusted to abide by its edict. They want to work more. Therefore, the Financial Times <https://prospectrockpartners.com/prospect-rock-partners-2025-ib-compensation-report/>says JPMorgan has stepped up its scrutiny of what its junior bankers are doing and will no longer rely upon the old self-reported time sheets as a measure of hours worked, but examine what they're up to on their computers. "Digital footprints" don't lie; if a 22 year-old analyst spends 100 hours making "keyboard strokes," video calls and attending "scheduled meetings", JPMorgan will know. JPMorgan will command that less work be done.
Given that in many walks of life people attempt to evade work, the situation at JPMorgan might seem freaky. But junior bankers are well documented </news/2020/05/why-bankers-overwork>as having a tendency towards working so hard that their hair falls out. This can be self-imposed. "Their sense of self-esteem is constructed around being an elite individual," said Alexandra Michel, the ex-Goldman Sachs associate who's built a career studying the phenomenon. </news/2020/05/why-bankers-overwork>
Not all young bankers would agree that they're wild for overwork though. Nor would they agree that they're intentionally misleading employers by marking down the hours they work on self-reported time sheets. In many cases, senior bankers at all banks are said to encourage juniors to understate their hours so that they can keep on working. Bank of America, for example, denied </news/2020/05/why-bankers-overwork> that Leo Lukenas worked 120 hour weeks before the associate died in 2024, but the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/podcasts/the-journal/outcry-at-bank-of-america-over-dangerous-workloads/55c88dae-09ca-44c3-9e65-ed17451ebae5?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqf_8GvGA1V9FkJT5jsKaWJDAhip0rWq6U17cWont-YbDTBHUZsipO7ISQp2_i0%3D&gaa_ts=69bd1342&gaa_sig=jU56fcUudLCCvX8ZQKdm2wgqdeHYDnoxgFKIY_z6L6bDuMFDTy9h79-Rg55cXp7xmamM4pmxJ9UKYVF-XPRZ4A%3D%3D> subsequently discovered that managers had been asking juniors to under-record banking hours to avoid HR intervention and keep working.
There will no more of this at JPMorgan, and this be welcome. From now on, every keyboard stroke at the bank must matter.
Separately, as we reported yesterday </news/hsbc-ai-job-cuts>, Goldman Sachs is job cuts. Insiders at the bank tell us that lists have been drawn up over the past month. The bank had been hoping to avoid layoffs through natural post-bonus attrition but this hasn't come to pass.
Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-layoffs-cuts-underperformers-sra-spring-summer-2026-3> confirms that cuts are coming. They will start in April, but they will not stop then. Instead of a single round or rounds Goldman is reportedly planning for a series of "ongoing, smaller rolling cuts" that may never end.
The new approach is intended to "give divisional leaders more control over timing than waiting months for the next firmwide review," says BI. Goldman Sachs says there is nothing to see here. "Regular, consistent head count management is nothing out of the ordinary for a public company," a spokesman declares.
Meanwhile...
Point72 and Balyasny recently banned employees from trading on prediction markets in their personal accounts. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/point72-balyasny-ban-staff-bets-in-prediction-market-crackdown?taid=69bc56e4a072250001e9aa3f&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Being a trader is an exhausting business now, but AI is helping. “I was up for almost 48 hours straight, monitoring the interceptions in the United Arab Emirates while simultaneously running scenarios and preparing for the market open. That’s precisely the kind of moment where AI becomes indispensable." (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/traders-overwhelmed-by-iran-news-are-turning-to-ai-for-help>
Traders have been pouring over real time alt data </news/hedge-funds-and-market-makers-need-data-strategists-as-war-heats-up>. This week, the key data point was whether Ras Laffan had gone into “cold shutdown" (refrigeration is still ongoing), or “warm,” which would take much longer to recover from. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/commodities-futures/frantic-pace-of-mideast-energy-strikes-leaves-markets-trading-blind-e2b67532?mod=finance_lead_story&mod=djemHeardEUH>
Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan have assembled baskets of listed companies with exposure to private credit and are creating products for hedge funds to trade against. Goldman's index includes European financial institutions. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/jpmorgan-goldman-offer-hedge-funds-way-to-short-private-credit?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260319&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
Bank of America suggested that clients bet against companies exposed to European private credit, including Deutsche Bank, but now it says this was all a terrible mistake. “Those views do not reflect those of BofA Research or the broader organisation. We apologise to the companies that were inaccurately referenced.” (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/363a2bc3-566a-4cc2-b69c-28db1782816e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Morgan Stanley hired James Machin, a managing director at Bank of America in London for its healthcare team. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/morgan-stanley-hires-bank-of-americas-machin-to-bolster-london-healthcare-team-79becd70?mod=homepage>
Fabrizio Campelli who runs they investment bank at Deutsche Bank might replace Christian Sewing. But so might Stefan Hoops, who's just been promoted to the management board and who runs the asset management arm. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/bdf1851f-8c9a-4aaf-a49a-178b8a3c6808?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Michele Faissola says Deutsche Bank caused irreparable harm to his career and wants £500m in damages. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/ex-trader-suing-deutsche-bank-wants-500-million-in-paschi-case?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260319&utm_campaign=author_23851719>
Ex-Point 72 guy says you need to work weekends at the start of your career to compounds knowledge. "What I learned was if you work six hours a Sunday, 50 Sundays a year, right? That's 300 hours." (YouTube) <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=byEXabVAxus>
Starting next month, bankers may not be able to make long haul flights to clients due to a shortage of jet fuel. (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/uk/transport/article/long-haul-flights-easter-holiday-travel-g2rfsn623?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqdcqFxc5bGL46euQvzckQHFkLh6_AwsFkJQHwm3WUZK7xYzZmewI64DfPJePow%3D&gaa_ts=69bbbf75&gaa_sig=u_zWaEPiuL5fZ0jFlVuLQ9aNhNnGg_iBxnJijuw_wRi_3EkYFbh4tQVkjmpcBzEuyHxHmAc4QxSFvzC-neFXbA%3D%3D>
Jes Staley's wife wants a divorce. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/jes-staley-s-wife-files-for-divorce-after-epstein-fallout?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260319&utm_campaign=author_3487187>

item.title: UBS's "brief" tech outage may have lasted three hours and been caused by an oversight
item.description:
What happened at UBS on Tuesday? As we reported </news/ubs-technology-outage>on Wednesday, the bank was said to have experienced a "brief" technology outage that impacted "some" of its trading business. The incident was resolved within the day.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
All is now back to normal, but the outage came at a challenging time amidst the volatility imposed by war in the Middle East and there are suggestions that it was not all that brief. Multiple sources at the Swiss bank say it lasted for up to three hours and affected trading on different desks, including FX and futures.
As such, it's thought that the failure was caused by infrastructure rather than a specific trading application. "If it's transversal and cross-business, it's due to infrastructure," observed one insider.
It's thought that UBS employees were unable to login to internal messaging systems like Teams as a result of the outage. One insider suggested it was due to Citrix, which provides software that UBS uses to validate people logging into its systems. "We had no access," he says. "Even Neo [UBS's cross asset trading platform] was down." The problem may have been a security certificate that was noted updated or corrupted.
UBS's outage came as the bank rang ritual cowbells <https://www.ft.com/content/c7c9e78b-e007-4ec6-a4b4-9a559e931fe3?syn-25a6b1a6=1> to celebrate the integration of most of Credit Suisse's computer systems. In December it parted company with Mike Dargan </news/first-ubs-s-head-of-technology-left-now-tech-people-are-being-cut>, its former CTO. Thousands of technology jobs are expected to go at the bank this year now that work on the integration is done.
One senior insider said the outage had nothing to do with the integration or the coming job cuts. However, they also observed that UBS has not has these kind of non-brief outages much before. Hopefully it will not have them much again.

item.title: HSBC's 20,000 potential AI job cuts could be duplicated at Goldman Sachs & Citi
item.description:
HSBC is not unfamiliar with the concept of cutting tens of thousands of jobs. In 2015, then-CEO Stuart Gulliver promised <https://www.ft.com/content/2fddac06-0e4b-11e5-9a65-00144feabdc0?syn-25a6b1a6=1>to cut 50,000 of the bank's 266,000 workforce by closing some operations and pivoting to Asia. In early 2020, Noel Quinn, Gulliver's successor, produced another plan </news/2020/02/hsbc-results-2019-cost-cutting> to cut 35,000 jobs by simplifying the bank's management "matrix" and further pivoting to Asia. And then last January, Georges Elhedery </news/hsbc-s-self-proclaimed-ruthless-ceo-is-not-its-most-feared-person>, Quinn's bold successor, announced a plan to close the bank's equity capital markets (ECM) and M&A businesses in both Europe and the US, and to focus more intently on Asia, plus the Middle East.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Despite all this, HSBC's headcount at the end of last year stood at 210,000 people. Even without Elhedery's efforts, it should now be 181,000 or below.
AI may help the bank to make the final push.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-19/hsbc-mulls-deep-job-cuts-from-multiyear-ai-fueled-overhaul>reported today that under Elhedery, HSBC is hoping to cut 20,000 jobs in the next three to five years by automating jobs in the middle and back office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> using AI, and maybe also exiting some more businesses. It's not clear exactly which jobs would be automated, but the favourites are reportedly "non-client facing roles in global service centers," which usually means operations, </news/operations-jobs-banking> compliance <https://efinancialcareers.com/news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance#:~:text=If%20you%20want%20a%20job,the%20overall%20financial%20services%20industry.>, finance, and IT roles away from major financial hubs.
Speaking yesterday <https://seekingalpha.com/article/4883769-hsbc-holdings-plc-hsbc-presents-at-european-financials-conference-2026-transcript> before Bloomberg published its revelations at the Morgan Stanley Financial Conference, HSBC CFO Manveen Kaur, said HSBC has been using AI at its Hang Seng subsidiary for KYC onboarding, small ticket credit lending, transaction monitoring, and other "volume-driven activities" that require "very basic binary decision-making."
HSBC's endeavours with AI at Heng Seng are not a "severance story," said Kaur. Instead the bank will be "upskilling colleagues" and "doing more with the same number of people," she explained.
For the bank as a whole, Bloomberg suggests this may not be so. HSBC didn't respond to a request to comment.
While HSBC's binary decision makers brace for pain, they may not be alone. Off the record, insiders at Goldman Sachs and Citi suggest that similar intentions are in the works there.
At Goldman Sachs, a memo from CEO David Solomon in October last year described plans to introduce a new AI driven "operating system" for the bank titled "OneGS 3.0." <https://finance.yahoo.com/news/david-solomon-says-theres-key-215243387.html?guccounter=1&guce_referrer=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ29vZ2xlLmNvbS8&guce_referrer_sig=AQAAAHJsokWNWZ7XpKguB7yoY05U2ov6SDWL8ZLXG2GEPbfcJ1c8YO78JtorLirnrqg7tc6tEsR4E0DTsOCHrymLOCwuDqiSXCLia7gg0K4YxJkTNVCLC9Xc0QYn2u3gNWtiZoa_S1r06t4H9wp-GtTPgCQyg3K15hO4GS0kQwTnmDGp>In January, Solomon said he was "excited" <https://seekingalpha.com/article/4860209-the-goldman-sachs-group-inc-gs-q4-2025-earnings-call-transcript>about this effort and that Goldman had already begun looking at "six work streams," where it will "invest to reengineer these processes from the ground up." Solomon didn't say what these six work streams are, but he promised news "over the course of the next quarters."
Speaking off the record, one Goldman Sachs insider says this news could come as soon as April. The firm is allegdly planning to make another round of job cuts focused on underperformers and has tightened its performance criteria in light of AI. There are suggestions this will just the start. A spokesman for Goldman Sachs said: “As always, Goldman Sachs is focused on supporting our people through our talent management, development and engagement efforts. We do not comment on speculation or rumors.”
While there are rumblings at Goldman Sachs, there are rumblings at Citi too. The US bank announced a small number of job cuts in January </news/citi-job-cuts-2026-bonuses>, but there are suggestions that a considerably larger round may be coming. Roles across compliance, risk, controls, and data are likely to bear the brunt of future cuts. Citi added thousands of jobs in this areas as a result of its 2020 consent order. </news/citi-is-still-out-there-hiring-m-ds-to-work-on-its-consent-order>Citi CEO Jane Fraser said in January that resulting transformation work is nearly done. AI is now being used by the bank for "over 50 processes," added Fraser.
A spokesperson for Citi said: “As we said previously, we will continue to reduce our headcount in 2026. These changes reflect adjustments we’re making to ensure our staffing levels, locations and expertise align with current business needs; efficiencies we have gained through technology; and progress against our Transformation work, which is nearing Citi’s Target State. We are grateful for the contributions these colleagues have made to Citi.”
As AI bites, some roles are likely to be savaged more comprehensively than others. Relationship-focused front office jobs could be comparatively immune. In December, David Solomon at Goldman Sachs even suggested </news/goldman-sachs-ai-hiring> that the firm might reinvest the money it saves from process-roles in new bankers to talk to clients. Humans can but hope.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Coinbase fired an engineer after they refused to use AI coding tools
item.description:
AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job>coding tools are all the rage in fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech>. Last summer, Robinhood </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-robinhood-culture-fintech> CEO Vlad Tenev said his engineers were writing half their code with AI. </news/robinhood-ai-coding> Coinbase </news/coinbase-pay-hiring> CEO Brian Armstrong has just admitted the same, having gone to slightly more extreme lengths to tout that fact.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Speaking on a podcast by Norges Bank <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jmdf35zSdA8>, Armstrong said, "on our software engineering team, more than 50% of the code is being written by AI." Armstrong said that about a year ago, he pushed for Coinbase's engineers to start using tools like Cursor and Claude; he said people were "a little hesitant... now almost all the engineers are using it every day."
This didn't come about without some nudging. When around 70% of Coinbase engineers had experimented with AI, Armstrong said he got "impatient" and sent a note out to staff saying that, if they hadn't signed up to try an AI tool by the end of the week, they'd be put in a meeting on Saturday with Armstrong so they could tell him why they hadn't.
Armstrong said one employee got fired because of this; they'd refused to use AI tools and didn't show up to the meeting. He said that the engineer didn't have a "good reason" like vacation or parental leave, so they were told they were "not a good fit for the company," and let go. Armstrong said the ordeal created a "very clear tone from the top that we need to stay on top of these things to stay relevant as a company."
It's hard not to place some blame on the developer in that instance, having ghosted their CEO, but it's not the first time Coinbase has taken an unconventional approach to its engineering culture. At the start of last year, the firm initiated a 'new norm' </news/coinbase-s-300k-software-engineers-aren-t-happy-with-the-firm-s-new-norm> in which projects could have no more than one bug per developer working on it.
Armstrong also noted that, thanks to AI coding, Coinbase has "started to see some really impressive potential cost savings." Given that the firm made a $667m loss </news/coinbase-pay-hiring>last year, it will be keen to realize as many cost savings as it can in 2026.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Financial Services is using AI more than other industries but seeing a fraction of the benefits
item.description:
Like most industries, financial services is bullish on the potential of AI. </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> It's in a unique position, however, in that teams have adopted AI far more than the average but seen far fewer benefits. </news/ai-projects-failing>
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
In a new survey from product development firm Studio Graphene, 500 managers and executives in UK businesses were asked about the usage of AI within their teams. 78% of respondents said they used AI in some capacity but, for financial services professionals, the usage rate was 91%.
Despite this, fewer financial services professionals were able to clearly define what 'successful' AI implementation was. While 31% of all respondents using AI said they'd seen a return on investment from their AI usage, just 13% of managers in finance were able to justify the cost.
There are many reasons for this. For one, finance and banking in particular are highly regulated industries full of publicly traded companies. They need to be able to justify their actions to investors and some AI tools are either hard to explain or prone to hallucinations.
There's also the fact that the tools today simply aren't good enough for industries prone to perfectionism. Claude for Excel, for example has received various complaints from people in finance; the tried and tested method of having your analysts work 100-hour weeks yields more consistent results. Mo Achour, a Goldman Sachs executive director who left to build an AI startup, told us last week </news/ex-goldman-sachs-banker-that-worked-on-thousands-of-financial-models-wants-ai-to-build-it-for-you>that "there’s no such thing as good enough" in financial services. Goldman CIO Marco Argenti, another AI advocate, told Bloomberg in 2024 that even "0.1% inaccuracy is totally unacceptable." </news/the-other-problem-goldman-sachs-faces-with-ll-ms>
Despite all this, it's no surprise that financial services firms are trying to make AI work for them. A report from Anthropic </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking> earlier this month said that business and finance was one of (if not the) most exposed sector to AI in theory. The bank that is able to realize that potential while 87% see it as a money suck will be very happy indeed.

item.title: Citi's head of culture and behavioural insights has left for HSBC
item.description:
Citi wants its culture to foster champions </news/andy-sieg-women#:~:text=%E2%80%9CWhat%20champions%20do%2C%20when%20their%20backs%20are%20to%20the%20wall%2C%20they%20find%20a%20way%20to%20win.%20They%20know%20they%E2%80%99ve%20got%20to%20change%2C%20take%20risks%2C%20play%20outside%20the%20box.%22>. It'll have to do so without one of the senior women </news/citi-parted-company-with-the-nice-woman-leading-its-unpopular-internal-hit-squad>tasked with improving that culture.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Anna Koczwara, an MD at Citi and its head of cultural and behavioural insights, has left to join HSBC </news/hsbc-s-md-promotions-are-back-to-normal-but-never-simple> in London. She arrived this week as its global head of behavioural science and research. Now-closed job listings in Citi's behavioural insights team say the division is tasked with "improv[ing Citi's] working environment, engagement levels, satisfaction, and intent to stay."
Koczwara, a psychological safety advocate, </news/citi-s-latest-effort-to-rectify-its-culture-hiring-a-ph-d-psychologist> was only at Citi for a year and a half. Before that, she spent seven years at RBS in London, where she was head of strategic capability and assessment.
HSBC's behavioural science team was first led by David Grosse, who joined HSBC in 2017 and left in 2022. He led a team of four according to Reuters <https://www.thomsonreuters.com/en-us/posts/investigation-fraud-and-risk/behavioral-science-bank-risk/>, but the team appears to have shown little growth since he left; Koczwara's arrival may be an attempt to revitalize it. The bank was also seen hiring for a behavioural scientist to work with its employee listening and dialogue team last month.
Grosse was different to other behavioural science leaders in that he had a banking risk background. Koczwara has a more standard background with a PhD </news/do-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance>in psychology. For her thesis <https://research.gold.ac.uk/id/eprint/28520/1/PSY_thesis_KoczwaraA_2007.pdf>, she interviewed 40 managers in banking and finance to assess their bias in evaluating the potential of male and female talent. </news/gender-pay-gap-investment-banking-goldman-sachs-jpmorgan>
HSBC did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Hong Kong’s global talent push gains pace
item.description:
As global talent hubs grapple with economic uncertainty, Hong Kong stands out. This resilient international business centre has both regulatory clarity and long‑term stability.
One of the latest initiatives supporting this is the Innovating Hong Kong Global Online TalentX Carnival <https://recruiterhub.efinancialcareers.com/InnovatingHK_Global_Online_TalentX_Carnival_Partner.html>. The careers event will take place online on 26 and 27 March 2026. Ambitious finance, technology, and innovation professionals will have access to employers, career insights, and relocation guidance.
TalentX Carnival: More than a traditional job fair
Innovating Hong Kong TalentX Carnival goes beyond the format of a recruitment fair. It brings together talent, enterprise, policy, and technology within one integrated platform. This reflects Hong Kong’s ambition to position itself as an innovation hub and a high-value professional services centre.
The Carnival features cloud-based interview functionality, creating a hybrid recruitment experience that bridges online and offline engagement.
Event highlights
Over two days, candidates can connect with employers and get visa policy guidance, career support and practical relocation tools.
Insight-sharing sessions and seminars on career development, industry growth and Hong Kong’s evolving economic landscape. Direct access to 3,000+ roles across financial services, technology, and property. AI-powered matching: The platform features an enhanced AI suite, including mock interview practice and CV analysis, to provide feedback before speaking with employers.
The Carnival is the official designated recruitment event of the Global Talent Summit (GTS) <https://gts.hkengage.gov.hk/en/> and a strategic partner of Hong Kong Talent Engage <https://www.hkengage.gov.hk/en>, underscoring its central role in Hong Kong’s global talent strategy.
AsiaWorld-Expo in Hong Kong
Participants will be invited to engage further at the physical career fair on 28 and 29 March <https://www.innovatinghk.com/> 2026 at AsiaWorld-Expo in Hong Kong. The in-person fair will host more than 400 employers and 10,000 open roles, offering face-to-face interaction, in-depth interviews, and extra seminars. Featured employers will include FUTU, Hang Seng Bank, MOUTAI, UNITREE and Liepin, alongside established institutions and emerging companies.
Past offline events have attracted over 10,000 participants, with 30% coming from outside the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area. The average interview-to-offer conversion rate for companies reached 25%.
The online event serves as a warm-up, offering company previews, early job applications, and AI tool experiences. The offline event focuses on in-depth interviews, seminars, and face-to-face interactions.
Sector-wide opportunities
This year’s online Carnival features roles across financial services, technology, property and other growth sectors. Roles are available for graduates, early-career professionals and experienced specialists. Candidates can upload their CVs in advance, speak to recruiters pre-application, and engage in live chat conversations.
Why Hong Kong?
The recruitment market is pivoting from ‘backfilling’ to value creation; 34% of employers <https://hongkongbusiness.hk/hr-education/in-focus/whats-driving-hong-kongs-hiring-rebound> plan to increase headcount this year. Hong Kong offers proximity to fast-growing regional markets and excellent career progression.
Its location and role as a bridge between mainland China and the West continue to drive demand for experienced professionals.
The city is also home to five universities ranked among the world’s top 100, reinforcing its depth of academic and research capability. In the International Institute for Management Development World Talent Ranking 2025, Hong Kong rose to fourth globally. Its highest position to date and first in Asia.
Hong Kong’s reach extends beyond the city. For those looking beyond the city limits, the event highlights GBA talent mobility. This includes cross-border career consultation, GBA policy interpretation sessions, and access to joint recruitment channels for enterprises operating across Guangdong, Hong Kong, and Macao.
Working and living in Hong Kong
Understanding that a move to Hong Kong is about more than a job, the Carnival is a one-stop support system for relocation logistics and long-term career planning.
The government encourages talent from beyond the region. This includes programmes such as the Quality Migrant Admission Scheme. There is also an Admission Scheme for Mainland Talents and Professionals, as well as the General Employment Policy.
The Carnival provides authoritative guidance on Hong Kong’s key talent admission schemes. This includes the Top Talent Pass Scheme (TTPS) and the Immigration Arrangements for Non-local Graduates (IANG), including all recent updates.
Besides visa guidance, participants can access:
Career support through official job databases Advice on visa renewal pathways Education consulting and school information for families Broader relocation resources
Taking the next step
With competition for skilled professionals intensifying worldwide, Hong Kong is open, competitive, and globally connected. For international candidates, the online Carnival is the perfect warm-up to explore the market and secure early interviews.
If you're considering new horizons in Asia, the online Carnival offers a practical first step towards building a long-term career in Hong Kong.
Registration for the online event is now open. Sign up here: https://recruiterhub.efinancialcareers.com/InnovatingHK_Global_Online_TalentX_Carnival_Partner.html <https://recruiterhub.efinancialcareers.com/InnovatingHK_Global_Online_TalentX_Carnival_Partner.html>
item.author: editor@efinancialcareers.com (Innovating Hong Kong)

item.title: Morning Coffee: The new power move if you’re leaving Citi and want a better job. Last year’s superstar bankers are getting worried
item.description:
If you’re frustrated in your banking career – perhaps you’ve reached a point at which the only job you can be promoted into is occupied by someone two years older than you who seems to be doing well – then you might start looking for ways to get a promotion by moving to the competition. If there are no vacancies there, then you could consider a lateral move, but if there aren’t any empty seats at your level either, you have two choices.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
One is to stick around getting progressively more and more bitter, and the other is to resign, take a sabbatical and write a social media post titled something like “Stepping Out Of My Comfort Zone”. Or if you’re Mark Mason and up until recently the Chief Financial Officer of Citigroup, you don’t even need to fire up ChatGPT or break out the book of inspirational quotes, as the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/hes-leaving-citigroup-to-be-a-ceo-can-he-land-the-big-job-79148099?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqcF8CG7Rx-pk4aACWDhKGizq0SoO43V9tUznaW1MdcocDLMiQsuOU9trP1Us3A%3D&gaa_ts=69bbbd47&gaa_sig=iX8riNL4nyDABfaaxjwDRCmMXluQl0vZrsrG7lM30BFdxuBh4T9qezqAmoYYVJ9KO6WR-DMl12ysiqyiiwACgw%3D%3D>might do a profile which allows you to announce your availability for bigger and better roles.
Mason does not waste the opportunity, making it clear that he now wants his next job to be as a CEO. Some recruiters are quoted as saying that he will probably have to “go down the food chain in terms of the size of enterprise” to achieve this, but he was probably aware of that risk; there are, after all, fewer than half a dozen banks which aren’t smaller than Citi.
Mason has the right resume for quite a lot of possible roles; he came to prominence in a wealth management business unit, but he also managed a “bad bank” winding down problem assets in the aftermath of the last financial crisis, and has had a key role in trying to sort out the back-office problems which once caused Citi to accidentally pay $900m </news/2020/12/jpmorgan-forecast-2021> to the creditors of Revlon. If you’re interested in shrinking something, he’s closed down over 60 business lines and cut around 60,000 jobs.
So why’s he leaving? Well, he isn’t, not immediately; until the end of the year, he’s going to be an Executive Vice-Chair and a senior advisor to Jane Fraser. (Although, since Citi have presumably signed off on the interview, they probably wouldn’t make him work out the whole contract if something big came up). But it seems pretty clear, reading between the lines, that this was a matter of “up or out”. Mason was offered the top job at Carlyle Group in 2023 and turned it down, something which really only makes sense if he had ambitions to be CEO of Citi.
And he might have been, if things had turned out differently; while the big restructuring project was going on, he would have been the natural choice if the board had decided that things weren’t working out and Citi needed a course correction. But things didn’t turn out differently, the board have demonstrated confidence in Jane Fraser by making her Chair as well as CEO. In that sort of situation, it’s best for all concerned to make a fresh start.
Elsewhere, European private credit was one of the hottest labour markets of 2024 and 2025. But the latest news from that market segment feels rather like the scene in Jurassic Park <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LFLB68JalpI> where the characters are wondering “why is the water in that glass rippling?” It’s not just software company loans any more; even private credit funds based on consumer and SME <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/private-credits-investor-exodus-spreads-to-consumer-loans-de2507d7?st=eCQuPD&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink> loans are seeing heightened redemptions. And the problems might not be confined to North America.
At industry conferences this week, banks like Deutsche, SocGen and UBS <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-17/socgen-s-krupa-sees-private-credit-clean-up-as-investors-exit?embedded-checkout=true> have been emphasising their sensible scale, cautious underwriting and long experience in the market, to make the case that they don’t have a private credit problem. But Bank of America <https://www.ft.com/content/9719a659-083d-4605-9238-4292b851439a?syn-25a6b1a6=1> doesn’t believe it, and is pitching a “medium-size short” basket trade of European stocks with private credit exposure which they believe to have 30% downside or more, since they haven’t sold off as much as US peers.
It can’t help that some LBO debt deals <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-18/wall-street-buckles-up-for-white-knuckle-ride-on-lbo-debt-sales?srnd=homepage-uk> are in danger of getting “hung” <https://www.ft.com/content/ce9a67da-62df-484e-9f50-075d4be7817f?syn-25a6b1a6=1>, and IPOs postponed <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/companies-trim-delay-ipos-2026-volatility-tests-valuations-2026-02-20/>. This was meant to be a “top decile” year for investment banking revenue, and that may yet turn out to be the case. But it seems that, like a good dinosaur movie, it’s going to give us a few jump-scares on the way to the happy ending.
Meanwhile …
It might be a sign of the times that 51% of buy-side traders are complaining that their biggest source of stress, fatigue and burnout is the internal technology that they have to work with. That’s almost twice as stressful as compliance (27%), and much worse than annoying brokers and counterparties (17%) or even career uncertainty (25%). But an ice-cool 11% of traders claim that they don’t feel any stress at all. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-18/stock-traders-are-facing-burnout-from-tech-angst-study-shows>)
UBS has completed the transfer of Credit Suisse clients in Switzerland onto their own IT systems, so far without major issues. The integration team had apparently got into the habit of ringing cowbells to celebrate major goalposts. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/c7c9e78b-e007-4ec6-a4b4-9a559e931fe3?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
One of the main reasons to have co-heads is that if one goes, you still have the other. So it’s potentially quite interesting that both of the “co-managing directors of real assets” responsible for private markets at the Australian sovereign wealth fund are leaving simultaneously. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-18/private-markets-chiefs-quit-australia-s-sovereign-wealth-fund>)
Charlotte Nutting has joined Capula to be its new head of central execution trading, after six years at Millennium. Capula is apparently beefing up its execution team; it’s got a vacancy advertised for a trader in New York. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/capula-hires-senior-millennium-trader-nutting-b7e1c0e7?mod=topic_news>)
JPMorgan is setting up an “athletes’ council” with some of the biggest names from professional sport, to help it create products that take sports stars from college to retirement and beyond. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/jpmorgan-partners-athletes-brady-wade-wealth-management-2026-3>)
While some Asian firms are reinstating work-from-home arrangements in order to save money on rising energy prices, bankers in Bangladesh are even being asked to take the bus rather than drive private cars into work. (Daily Star <https://www.thedailystar.net/business/news/bangladesh-bank-asks-bankers-avoid-cars-use-public-transport-4126286>)
